# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define an inner product on a real vector space. List its axioms.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **inner product** on a real vector space $V$ is a real-valued function $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ that assigns to each pair of vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}$ a real number $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$, satisfying the following axioms for all $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \in V$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$:

1. **Symmetry:** $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle$
2. **Additivity:** $\langle \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \rangle = \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{w} \rangle + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \rangle$
3. **Homogeneity:** $\langle c\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = c\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$
4. **Positive Definiteness:** $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle \geq 0$, and $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle = 0 \iff \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{0}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality. When does equality hold?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Statement:** For any two vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}$ in an inner product space $V$,
$$\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$$

**Equality Condition:** Equality holds if and only if the two vectors are linearly dependent, i.e., either $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{0}$ or $\mathbf{v} = c\mathbf{u}$ for some scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$ (the vectors are parallel or anti-parallel).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Define an inner product space. Show that $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = u_1 v_1 + 2u_2 v_2 + 3u_3 v_3$ defines an inner product on $\mathbb{R}^3$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the vectors $\mathbf{u} = (1, 1, 0)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (2, -1, 1)$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ equipped with the above inner product, compute:
(i) $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$
(ii) $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert$ and $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$
(iii) the angle between $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$.
**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition:** A real vector space $V$ together with a function $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying the four axioms (symmetry, additivity, homogeneity, positive definiteness) is called an **inner product space**.

**Verification:** Let $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, u_3), \mathbf{v} = (v_1, v_2, v_3), \mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, w_3) \in \mathbb{R}^3$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$.

**1. Symmetry:**
$$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = u_1 v_1 + 2u_2 v_2 + 3u_3 v_3 = v_1 u_1 + 2v_2 u_2 + 3v_3 u_3 = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle \checkmark$$

**2. Additivity:**
$$\langle \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \rangle = (u_1 + v_1) w_1 + 2(u_2 + v_2) w_2 + 3(u_3 + v_3) w_3$$
$$= (u_1 w_1 + 2u_2 w_2 + 3u_3 w_3) + (v_1 w_1 + 2v_2 w_2 + 3v_3 w_3) = \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{w} \rangle + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \rangle \checkmark$$

**3. Homogeneity:**
$$\langle c\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = (cu_1)v_1 + 2(cu_2)v_2 + 3(cu_3)v_3 = c(u_1 v_1 + 2u_2 v_2 + 3u_3 v_3) = c \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \checkmark$$

**4. Positive Definiteness:**
$$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle = u_1^2 + 2u_2^2 + 3u_3^2 \geq 0$$
since it is a sum of three non-negative terms.
Also, $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle = 0 \iff u_1^2 = 0, u_2^2 = 0, u_3^2 = 0 \iff \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{0}$ $\checkmark$

Therefore, all four axioms are satisfied, and the function defines a valid inner product on $\mathbb{R}^3$.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

Given $\mathbf{u} = (1, 1, 0)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (2, -1, 1)$.

**(i) Inner product:**
$$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = (1)(2) + 2(1)(-1) + 3(0)(1) = 2 - 2 + 0 = 0$$

**(ii) Norms:**
$$\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle} = \sqrt{1^2 + 2(1)^2 + 3(0)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 2 + 0} = \sqrt{3}$$

$$\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle} = \sqrt{2^2 + 2(-1)^2 + 3(1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 2 + 3} = \sqrt{9} = 3$$

**(iii) Angle:** Since $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$, the vectors are **orthogonal**, so the angle between them is:
$$\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2} = 90^\circ$$

Alternatively, $\cos\theta = \dfrac{0}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 3} = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ$.

### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** State and prove the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for an inner product space. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, prove that for any two vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$:
$$\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i \right)^2 \leq \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2 \right) \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2 \right)$$
Hence find the maximum value of $f(x, y, z) = 3x + 4y + 5z$ subject to the constraint $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Statement:** For any vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}$ in an inner product space $V$,
$$\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$$

**Proof:** Consider the function $f(t) = \Vert \mathbf{u} - t\mathbf{v} \Vert^2$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

Expanding:
$$f(t) = \langle \mathbf{u} - t\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} - t\mathbf{v} \rangle = \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^2 - 2t \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle + t^2 \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2$$

By positive definiteness, $f(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$. If $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$, inequality is trivial. Otherwise $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 > 0$, and the discriminant of this quadratic in $t$ must be non-positive:

$$(-2\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle)^2 - 4 \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^2 \leq 0$$
$$4 \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle^2 \leq 4 \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^2 \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2$$
$$\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert \qquad \blacksquare$$

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**First part (the inequality):** Apply Cauchy–Schwarz with the standard inner product on $\mathbb{R}^n$:
$$\langle \mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i, \quad \Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2, \quad \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2$$

Squaring Cauchy–Schwarz:
$$\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i \right)^2 \leq \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2 \right) \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2 \right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Second part (maximization):** Let $\mathbf{a} = (x, y, z)$ and $\mathbf{b} = (3, 4, 5)$.

By Cauchy–Schwarz:
$$f(x, y, z) = 3x + 4y + 5z = \langle \mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b} \rangle \leq \Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert$$

Given $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$, we have $\Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert = 1$.

$$\Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 25} = \sqrt{50} = 5\sqrt{2}$$

Therefore, the maximum value is:
$$f_{\max} = 1 \cdot 5\sqrt{2} = 5\sqrt{2}$$

**Equality** holds when $\mathbf{a}$ is parallel to $\mathbf{b}$, i.e., $(x, y, z) = \dfrac{1}{5\sqrt{2}}(3, 4, 5) = \left( \dfrac{3}{5\sqrt{2}}, \dfrac{4}{5\sqrt{2}}, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \right)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often **omit the proof of symmetry** when verifying inner product axioms. The examiner strictly allocates **1 mark** for symmetry verification alone. Always check $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In Cauchy–Schwarz proofs, students **forget to mention the trivial case** when $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ or $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{0}$. This costs **1 mark**. Always state "if $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$, inequality holds trivially with equality; assume $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ for the main proof."
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In the discriminant step, students **forget to take the absolute value** on the left-hand side. Remember: $(\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle)^2$ is the same as $\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert^2$, so squaring is safe, but the final result must have $\vert \cdot \vert$ on the left.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In optimization problems, students **write the maximum without justifying the equality case** (which vector achieves it). Always specify the vector that achieves the bound.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Many students **do not explicitly use the angle formula** $\cos\theta = \frac{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert}$ — they jump directly to "they are perpendicular" without computing it. Always show the calculation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — INNER PRODUCT**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** A real-valued function $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying the four axioms (symmetry, additivity, homogeneity, positive definiteness).
>
> ✅ **Standard inner product** on $\mathbb{R}^n$: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = \sum u_i v_i$.
>
> ✅ **Induced norm:** $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle}$ — always non-negative.
>
> ✅ **Induced distance:** $d(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \Vert \mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v} \Vert$.
>
> ✅ **Angle formula:** $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}{\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert}$, valid only for non-zero vectors.
>
> ✅ **Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality:** $\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert$ — proven via discriminant method.
>
> ✅ **Equality in Cauchy–Schwarz** holds **iff** $\mathbf{v} = c\mathbf{u}$ (parallel vectors).
>
> ✅ **Orthogonality:** $\mathbf{u} \perp \mathbf{v} \iff \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$.
>
> ✅ **Pythagorean Theorem:** If $\mathbf{u} \perp \mathbf{v}$, then $\Vert \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 = \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^2 + \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2$.
>
> ✅ **Projection formula:** $\text{proj}_{\mathbf{u}} \mathbf{v} = \dfrac{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle}{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle} \mathbf{u}$ — always a scalar multiple of $\mathbf{u}$.
>
> ✅ **Polarization identity:** Inner product is recoverable from the norm: $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\left[\Vert \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 - \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^2 - \Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2\right]$.
>
> ✅ **Verification tip:** To verify an inner product, **check all 4 axioms**; never skip positive definiteness.
>
> ✅ **Angle interpretation:**
>   - $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle > 0 \Rightarrow$ acute angle ($< 90°$)
>   - $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0 \Rightarrow$ right angle ($= 90°$)
>   - $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle < 0 \Rightarrow$ obtuse angle ($> 90°$)
>
> ✅ **Engineering link:** Cosine similarity in ML, dot product lighting in computer graphics, correlation in signal processing, Fourier coefficients in $L^2$ spaces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples in Rn and M2x2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples in Rn and M2x2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — The Real Coordinate Space $\mathbb{R}^n$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Vector Space of n-tuples):**
> The set $\mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as the collection of all ordered $n$-tuples of real numbers:
> $$\mathbb{R}^n = \{(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \mid x_i \in \mathbb{R}, \; i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$
> Equipped with the standard operations of **component-wise addition** and **scalar multiplication**, $\mathbb{R}^n$ forms a **vector space over the field $\mathbb{R}$**. The **zero vector** is the $n$-tuple $\mathbf{0} = (0, 0, \ldots, 0)$, and the dimension of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is exactly **$n$**.

**Special cases frequently used in KTU problems:**
- $\mathbb{R}^1$ = the real number line
- $\mathbb{R}^2$ = the plane (every point is a 2-tuple)
- $\mathbb{R}^3$ = ordinary 3-dimensional physical space
- $\mathbb{R}^n$ = the general $n$-dimensional space used in data science and signal processing

### 1.2 Formal Definition — The Matrix Space $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Vector Space of 2×2 Real Matrices):**
> The set $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ is defined as the collection of all $2 \times 2$ matrices with real entries:
> $$M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}) = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \;\middle|\; a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{R} \right\}$$
> Under the standard operations of **matrix addition** and **scalar multiplication of matrices**, $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ forms a vector space over $\mathbb{R}$ of **dimension 4**, with the zero vector being the **zero matrix** $\mathbf{0}_{2 \times 2} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for students:** Think of $M_{2 \times 2}$ as $\mathbb{R}^4$ in disguise! Each $2 \times 2$ matrix is equivalent to a 4-tuple $(a, b, c, d)$ by reading entries row-wise. Hence every algebraic fact about $\mathbb{R}^4$ has a direct matrix analogue.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy 1 — $\mathbb{R}^n$ as a GPS Address System:**
Imagine a courier company tracking packages. Each package is described by **$n$ numerical attributes** — weight, volume, destination latitude, destination longitude, insurance value, etc. The state of *one* package is a single point in $\mathbb{R}^n$. Operations like:
- **Adding two packages' attributes** = combining their load profiles
- **Multiplying by a scalar** = scaling all attributes uniformly (e.g., a 2× bulk order)

…are exactly the operations of the vector space $\mathbb{R}^n$.

**Analogy 2 — $M_{2 \times 2}$ as a Transformation Recipe Book:**
A $2 \times 2$ matrix is a *recipe* that transforms a 2D input vector into a 2D output vector (think of a rotation, scaling, or shearing operation in computer graphics). The four entries $(a, b, c, d)$ are the "ingredients." Adding two such recipes = composing two transformations by summing their effects. Multiplying by a scalar = uniformly amplifying/weakening a transformation. This is precisely the vector space $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why these two examples matter most for KTU:**
> $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $M_{2 \times 2}$ are the **canonical concrete examples** asked in KTU exams for testing the abstract concept of a *vector space*. You will be asked to **verify the axioms** (closure, associativity, identity, inverses, distributivity) explicitly on these sets.

---

### 1.4 The Two Operations — Quick Reference

| Operation | In $\mathbb{R}^n$ | In $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Addition** | $(x_1, \ldots, x_n) + (y_1, \ldots, y_n) = (x_1+y_1, \ldots, x_n+y_n)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} e & f \\ g & h \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a+e & b+f \\ c+g & d+h \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **Scalar Multiplication** | $\alpha (x_1, \ldots, x_n) = (\alpha x_1, \ldots, \alpha x_n)$ | $\alpha \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha a & \alpha b \\ \alpha c & \alpha d \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **Zero Element** | $(0, 0, \ldots, 0)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **Additive Inverse** | $(-x_1, -x_2, \ldots, -x_n)$ | $\begin{bmatrix} -a & -b \\ -c & -d \end{bmatrix}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse matrix addition with matrix multiplication. **For the vector space structure**, we use *entry-wise* addition — NOT matrix multiplication. Matrix multiplication is a *separate* algebraic operation and is **not commutative**; vector space addition is always commutative.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Vector Space Axioms — Verified for $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $M_{2 \times 2}$

A set $V$ equipped with two operations $(+, \cdot)$ is a **real vector space** if and only if the following **eight axioms** hold for all $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} \in V$ and all scalars $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Eight Vector Space Axioms (KFU Exam Favorite):**
>
> | # | Axiom | Symbolic Form |
> |---|---|---|
> | A1 | Closure under addition | $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in V$ |
> | A2 | Commutativity of addition | $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{u}$ |
> | A3 | Associativity of addition | $(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{u} + (\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w})$ |
> | A4 | Existence of zero vector | $\exists \, \mathbf{0} \in V: \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{v}$ |
> | A5 | Existence of additive inverse | $\exists \, (-\mathbf{v}) \in V: \mathbf{v} + (-\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}$ |
> | S1 | Closure under scalar multiplication | $\alpha \mathbf{v} \in V$ |
> | S2 | Distributivity (scalar over vector addition) | $\alpha(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) = \alpha \mathbf{u} + \alpha \mathbf{v}$ |
> | S3 | Distributivity (vector over scalar addition) | $(\alpha + \beta)\mathbf{v} = \alpha \mathbf{v} + \beta \mathbf{v}$ |
> | S4 | Associativity of scalar multiplication | $\alpha(\beta \mathbf{v}) = (\alpha \beta)\mathbf{v}$ |
> | S5 | Identity scalar | $1 \cdot \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}$ |

*(Note: A1–A5 govern the additive group structure; S1–S5 govern compatibility with scalar multiplication.)*

### 2.2 Why $\mathbb{R}^n$ Satisfies All Eight Axioms

**The "Why" (Intuition):** The operations in $\mathbb{R}^n$ are inherited *component-wise* from the familiar real number line $\mathbb{R}$. Since $\mathbb{R}$ itself is a field (and hence satisfies all field axioms), transferring these properties coordinate-by-coordinate guarantees that $\mathbb{R}^n$ inherits them collectively.

**The "How" (Logic Steps):**
- **Closure (A1, S1):** Sum/product of two real numbers is real ⇒ sum/scaled-tuple of $\mathbb{R}^n$ tuples is still in $\mathbb{R}^n$. ✓
- **Commutativity (A2):** $x_i + y_i = y_i + x_i$ for each coordinate $i$ (real number addition is commutative). ✓
- **Associativity (A3):** $(x_i + y_i) + z_i = x_i + (y_i + z_i)$ (real number addition is associative). ✓
- **Zero (A4):** $\mathbf{0} = (0, 0, \ldots, 0)$ acts as the additive identity. ✓
- **Inverse (A5):** $-(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = (-x_1, \ldots, -x_n)$ exists in $\mathbb{R}^n$. ✓
- **Distributivity (S2, S3):** Follows from distributivity in $\mathbb{R}$ applied coordinate-wise. ✓
- **Associativity of scalars (S4):** $\alpha(\beta x_i) = (\alpha\beta) x_i$ for each $i$. ✓
- **Identity scalar (S5):** $1 \cdot x_i = x_i$ for each $i$. ✓

### 2.3 Why $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ Satisfies All Eight Axioms

**The "Why":** Every $2 \times 2$ matrix is essentially a "package" of four real numbers arranged in a box. The vector space axioms reduce to the axioms of $\mathbb{R}$ applied to *each* of the four entries separately.

**The "How":** For $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} e & f \\ g & h \end{bmatrix}$:
- $A + B = \begin{bmatrix} a+e & b+f \\ c+g & d+h \end{bmatrix}$ — entries are real (closure). ✓
- Addition of $2 \times 2$ matrices is commutative because real-number addition is commutative. ✓
- All other axioms follow by the **same coordinate-wise argument** as $\mathbb{R}^n$, since $M_{2 \times 2} \cong \mathbb{R}^4$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Properties of Linear Combinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Combination Definition (KTU Module 3 High-Yield):**
> A vector $\mathbf{v} \in V$ is a **linear combination** of vectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k$ if there exist scalars $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k \in \mathbb{R}$ such that:
> $$\mathbf{v} = c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k \mathbf{v}_k$$
> The set of *all* such linear combinations is called the **span**, denoted $\text{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$.

| Property | In $\mathbb{R}^n$ | In $M_{2 \times 2}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Canonical basis** | $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1,0,\ldots,0), \ldots, \mathbf{e}_n = (0,\ldots,0,1)$ | $E_{11}, E_{12}, E_{21}, E_{22}$ (single-entry matrices) |
| **Standard basis cardinality** | $n$ | $4$ |
| **Dimension of space** | $n$ | $4$ |
| **Number of scalar unknowns in linear combo** | depends on $k$ | depends on $k$ |
| **Resulting homogeneous system** | $n$ equations, $k$ unknowns | $4$ equations, $k$ unknowns |

### 2.5 The Standard Basis of $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The four "elementary" $2 \times 2$ matrices form the standard basis:**
> $$E_{11} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_{12} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_{21} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_{22} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Any matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ can be written uniquely as $A = aE_{11} + bE_{12} + cE_{21} + dE_{22}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **$\mathbb{R}^n$ in Information Science:** Represents feature vectors in machine learning (e.g., a 784-dimensional vector for a $28 \times 28$ MNIST image pixel), signal samples, and search-engine document embeddings.
- **$M_{2 \times 2}$ in Computer Graphics:** Represents 2D affine transformations (rotations, scalings, shears) used to manipulate sprites, game objects, and UI elements in real-time graphics pipelines.
- **Linear combinations in DSP:** Every discrete-time signal in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a linear combination of impulse basis vectors — the foundational fact behind the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Verifying $\mathbb{R}^2$ as a Vector Space (Closure + Identity + Inverse)

**Problem:** Let $\mathbf{u} = (3, -1)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (4, 5)$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Verify the closure, identity, and inverse axioms for these two vectors.

**Step 1 — Axiom A1 (Closure under addition):**
We must show $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^2$.

$$\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = (3, -1) + (4, 5) = (3 + 4, \; -1 + 5) = (7, 4)$$

Since $7 \in \mathbb{R}$ and $4 \in \mathbb{R}$, the result $(7, 4)$ is a valid 2-tuple of real numbers. Hence $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^2$. ✓

**Step 2 — Axiom A4 (Zero vector):**
We need $\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{v}$, where $\mathbf{0} = (0, 0)$.

$$\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{0} = (4, 5) + (0, 0) = (4 + 0, \; 5 + 0) = (4, 5) = \mathbf{v} \; \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Axiom A5 (Additive inverse):**
We need $(-\mathbf{v})$ such that $\mathbf{v} + (-\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}$.

$$-\mathbf{v} = -(4, 5) = (-4, -5)$$

Verification:

$$\mathbf{v} + (-\mathbf{v}) = (4, 5) + (-4, -5) = (4 + (-4), \; 5 + (-5)) = (0, 0) = \mathbf{0} \; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The three selected axioms hold for the given vectors. By the *coordinate-wise inheritance principle*, the same holds for any vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$, confirming that $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a vector space.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Verifying Axioms in $M_{2 \times 2}$ for Two Specific Matrices

**Problem:** Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} -5 & 6 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. Verify the axioms of closure, scalar multiplication distributivity, and additive inverse.

**Step 1 — Axiom A1 (Closure under addition):**

$$A + B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -5 & 6 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 + (-5) & -3 + 6 \\ 1 + 0 & 4 + 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

Since the result is a $2 \times 2$ matrix with real entries, it belongs to $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$. ✓

**Step 2 — Axiom S2 (Distributivity: $\alpha(A + B) = \alpha A + \alpha B$):**
Let $\alpha = 3$.

Left-hand side:

$$\alpha(A + B) = 3 \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -9 & 9 \\ 3 & 18 \end{bmatrix}$$

Right-hand side:

$$\alpha A + \alpha B = 3 \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} + 3 \begin{bmatrix} -5 & 6 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -9 \\ 3 & 12 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -15 & 18 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 15 & -9 + 18 \\ 3 + 0 & 12 + 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -9 & 9 \\ 3 & 18 \end{bmatrix}$$

Both sides match. ✓

**Step 3 — Axiom A5 (Additive inverse):**

$$-A = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 3 \\ -1 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verification:

$$A + (-A) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 3 \\ -1 & -4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0}_{2 \times 2} \; \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Expressing an $\mathbb{R}^3$ Vector as a Linear Combination

**Problem:** Express $\mathbf{w} = (7, 1, -5)$ as a linear combination of $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1, 0, -1)$, $\mathbf{v}_2 = (2, 1, 0)$, and $\mathbf{v}_3 = (0, 1, 1)$.

**Step 1 — Set up the equation:**
We seek scalars $c_1, c_2, c_3 \in \mathbb{R}$ such that:

$$c_1 (1, 0, -1) + c_2 (2, 1, 0) + c_3 (0, 1, 1) = (7, 1, -5)$$

**Step 2 — Expand component-wise:**

$$(c_1 + 2c_2 + 0c_3, \; 0c_1 + c_2 + c_3, \; -c_1 + 0c_2 + c_3) = (7, 1, -5)$$

This produces the **linear system of 3 equations in 3 unknowns**:

$$\begin{aligned} c_1 + 2c_2 \quad\quad\quad &= 7 \\ c_2 + c_3 &= 1 \\ -c_1 \quad\quad + c_3 &= -5 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system using row reduction:**

From Equation (1): $c_1 = 7 - 2c_2$.

Substituting into Equation (3):

$$-(7 - 2c_2) + c_3 = -5$$

$$-7 + 2c_2 + c_3 = -5$$

$$2c_2 + c_3 = 2$$

From Equation (2): $c_3 = 1 - c_2$.

Substitute:

$$2c_2 + (1 - c_2) = 2 \implies c_2 + 1 = 2 \implies c_2 = 1$$

Back-substitute to find $c_3$ and $c_1$:

$$c_3 = 1 - 1 = 0, \quad c_1 = 7 - 2(1) = 5$$

**Step 4 — Verify the solution:**

$$5(1, 0, -1) + 1(2, 1, 0) + 0(0, 1, 1) = (5, 0, -5) + (2, 1, 0) + (0, 0, 0) = (7, 1, -5) \; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** $\mathbf{w} = 5\mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2 + 0 \cdot \mathbf{v}_3$ is the unique linear combination.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Linear Combination in $M_{2 \times 2}$

**Problem:** Express $W = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ as a linear combination of:

$$E_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Set up the equation:**
We seek $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4 \in \mathbb{R}$ such that:

$$c_1 E_1 + c_2 E_2 + c_3 E_3 + c_4 E_4 = W$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} c_1 & c_2 \\ c_3 & c_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Read off entry-wise:**

$$c_1 = 5, \quad c_2 = 7, \quad c_3 = -3, \quad c_4 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

$$W = 5E_1 + 7E_2 - 3E_3 + 2E_4$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** When the spanning set is the *standard basis* of $M_{2 \times 2}$, the coefficients are **literally the entries** of the target matrix — no system to solve! This is why the standard basis is the most "natural" choice.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Showing a Custom Subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a Subspace

**Problem:** Determine if $S = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : 2x - 3y = 0\}$ is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 1 — Zero vector test:**
Plug $\mathbf{0} = (0, 0)$: $2(0) - 3(0) = 0$. ✓ (Zero is in $S$.)

**Step 2 — Closure under addition:**
Let $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2)$ and $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, v_2)$ be in $S$. Then $2u_1 - 3u_2 = 0$ and $2v_1 - 3v_2 = 0$.

Compute $2(u_1 + v_1) - 3(u_2 + v_2)$:

$$= 2u_1 + 2v_1 - 3u_2 - 3v_2 = (2u_1 - 3u_2) + (2v_1 - 3v_2) = 0 + 0 = 0$$

So $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in S$. ✓

**Step 3 — Closure under scalar multiplication:**
Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$. Then:

$$2(\alpha u_1) - 3(\alpha u_2) = \alpha(2u_1 - 3u_2) = \alpha \cdot 0 = 0$$

So $\alpha \mathbf{u} \in S$. ✓

**Conclusion:** $S$ is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^2$. In fact, $S = \text{span}\{(3, 2)\}$, a 1-dimensional line through the origin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-step subspace test (must memorize for KTU):**
> 1. Verify $\mathbf{0} \in S$ (or equivalently, $S$ is non-empty).
> 2. Closure under addition: $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in S \implies \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in S$.
> 3. Closure under scalar multiplication: $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}, \mathbf{u} \in S \implies \alpha\mathbf{u} \in S$.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 6 — A Subset of $M_{2 \times 2}$ That Is **Not** a Subspace

**Problem:** Let $T = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \in M_{2 \times 2} : ad - bc = 1 \right\}$. Is $T$ a subspace?

**Step 1 — Zero test:**
The zero matrix has $ad - bc = 0 \cdot 0 - 0 \cdot 0 = 0 \neq 1$. So $\mathbf{0} \notin T$. ✗

**Conclusion:** $T$ is **not** a subspace. (In fact, $T$ is the set of $2 \times 2$ matrices with determinant 1 — the special linear group $SL_2(\mathbb{R})$ — which is a *group* but not closed under addition or scalar multiplication.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** Sets defined by nonlinear conditions (like determinant = 1) almost always fail to be subspaces. The defining condition should be a *homogeneous linear equation in the entries*.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Vector Space Verification Pipeline

The diagram below depicts the standard KTU-engineered **axiom verification workflow** that should be applied to *any* candidate vector space, with explicit checkpoints for both $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $M_{2 \times 2}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Candidate Set V with operations + and scalar mult]) --> B{Zero vector in V?}
    B -- No --> Z([REJECT: Not a vector space])
    B -- Yes --> C{Closure under addition?}
    C -- No --> Z
    C -- Yes --> D{Additive inverses exist?}
    D -- No --> Z
    D -- Yes --> E{Closure under scalar mult?}
    E -- No --> Z
    E -- Yes --> F{Distributivity holds?}
    F -- No --> Z
    F -- Yes --> G{Associativity + identity scalar?}
    G -- No --> Z
    G -- Yes --> H([ACCEPT: V is a real vector space])
    H --> I[Determine dimension of V]
    I --> J[Construct standard basis]
    J --> K[Identify canonical examples in V]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Map: Isomorphism Between $M_{2 \times 2}$ and $\mathbb{R}^4$

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Matrix A in M2x2 with entries a,b,c,d"] --> B((Isomorphism phi))
    B --> C["Tuple a,b,c,d in R4"]
    C --> D((Inverse phi inverse))
    D --> E["Reconstructed matrix"]
    F["Vector addition in M2x2"] --> G((Commutes with))
    G --> H["Component-wise addition in R4"]
    I["Scalar multiplication in M2x2"] --> J((Commutes with))
    J --> K["Component-wise scalar mult in R4"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The map $\phi: M_{2 \times 2} \to \mathbb{R}^4$ defined by $\phi\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}\right) = (a, b, c, d)$ is a **bijection** that preserves both vector space operations. Such a structure-preserving map is called a **vector space isomorphism**. In KTU problems, this allows you to "translate" any $\mathbb{R}^4$ problem into an $M_{2 \times 2}$ problem and vice versa.

### 4.3 Subspace Test Decision Flow for $M_{2 \times 2}$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P([Subset W of M2x2 given by matrix condition]) --> Q{Condition homogeneous<br/>and linear in entries?}
    Q -- No --> R([Likely NOT a subspace])
    Q -- Yes --> S{Zero matrix satisfies<br/>the condition?}
    S -- No --> R
    S -- Yes --> T{Sum of two arbitrary<br/>members stays in W?}
    T -- No --> R
    T -- Yes --> U{Scalar multiple of<br/>arbitrary member stays in W?}
    U -- No --> R
    U -- Yes --> V([W is a subspace of M2x2])
    V --> W[Find a basis for W]
    W --> X[Compute dim W via basis size]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Tree: Hierarchy of $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $M_{2 \times 2}$ Examples

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT([Real Vector Spaces V over R]) --> R1[R1 - real line]
    ROOT --> R2[R2 - the plane]
    ROOT --> R3[R3 - physical space]
    ROOT --> RN[Rn - n-dimensional]
    ROOT --> M22[M2x2 matrices]
    ROOT --> MMM[Mmxn general matrices]
    R2 --> S1["S1 = span of single nonzero vector<br/>(a line through origin)"]
    R2 --> S2["S2 = span of two independent vectors<br/>(the whole plane)"]
    R2 --> S3["S3 = trivial subspace {0}"]
    M22 --> T1["T1 = upper triangular 2x2 matrices<br/>(dim 3)"]
    M22 --> T2["T2 = symmetric 2x2 matrices<br/>(dim 3)"]
    M22 --> T3["T3 = scalar matrices aI<br/>(dim 1)"]
    M22 --> T4["T4 = trace-zero 2x2 matrices<br/>(dim 3)"]
```
