## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the vector space $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$. State its dimension and write its standard basis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ is the set of all $2 \times 2$ matrices with real entries:
> $$M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}) = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} : a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{R} \right\}$$
> Under entry-wise matrix addition and scalar multiplication, it forms a real vector space.
> **Dimension:** $4$.
> **Standard basis:**
> $$E_{11} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_{12} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_{21} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad E_{22} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for dimension, 1 mark for standard basis.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the additive inverse of the vector $\mathbf{v} = (5, -2, 7, 0)$ in $\mathbb{R}^4$? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The additive inverse of $\mathbf{v} = (5, -2, 7, 0)$ in $\mathbb{R}^4$ is $-\mathbf{v} = (-5, 2, -7, 0)$.

**Justification:** By axiom A5 of vector spaces, the additive inverse $-\mathbf{v}$ must satisfy $\mathbf{v} + (-\mathbf{v}) = \mathbf{0}$, where $\mathbf{0} = (0,0,0,0)$. Computing:

$$(5, -2, 7, 0) + (-5, 2, -7, 0) = (5-5, \; -2+2, \; 7-7, \; 0+0) = (0, 0, 0, 0) \; \checkmark$$

Hence $(-\mathbf{v})$ exists uniquely in $\mathbb{R}^4$ and is given entry-wise by negating each component.

*(Award: 1 mark for the inverse, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Verify that $\mathbb{R}^3$ is a vector space over $\mathbb{R}$ with respect to the standard operations of component-wise addition and scalar multiplication. Check **at least six** of the eight axioms explicitly.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine whether the set

$$W = \left\{ (a, b, c) \in \mathbb{R}^3 : a + 2b - c = 0 \right\}$$

is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$. If yes, find a basis for $W$ and its dimension.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Verification of $\mathbb{R}^3$ as a vector space:**

Let $\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, u_3)$, $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, v_2, v_3)$, $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, w_3) \in \mathbb{R}^3$ and $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Axiom A1 (Closure under addition):**

$$\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = (u_1 + v_1, \; u_2 + v_2, \; u_3 + v_3) \in \mathbb{R}^3 \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom A2 (Commutativity):**

$$\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = (u_1 + v_1, u_2 + v_2, u_3 + v_3) = (v_1 + u_1, v_2 + u_2, v_3 + u_3) = \mathbf{v} + \mathbf{u} \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom A3 (Associativity):**

$$(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) + \mathbf{w} = (u_1 + v_1 + w_1, u_2 + v_2 + w_2, u_3 + v_3 + w_3) = \mathbf{u} + (\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{w}) \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom A4 (Zero vector):**

Let $\mathbf{0} = (0, 0, 0)$. Then $\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{0} = (v_1, v_2, v_3) = \mathbf{v}$. ✓

**Axiom S1 (Closure under scalar multiplication):**

$$\alpha \mathbf{v} = (\alpha v_1, \alpha v_2, \alpha v_3) \in \mathbb{R}^3 \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom S2 (Distributivity over vector addition):**

$$\alpha(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) = \alpha(u_1 + v_1, u_2 + v_2, u_3 + v_3) = (\alpha u_1 + \alpha v_1, \alpha u_2 + \alpha v_2, \alpha u_3 + \alpha v_3) = \alpha \mathbf{u} + \alpha \mathbf{v} \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom S3 (Distributivity over scalar addition):**

$$(\alpha + \beta)\mathbf{v} = ((\alpha + \beta)v_1, (\alpha + \beta)v_2, (\alpha + \beta)v_3)$$

$$= (\alpha v_1 + \beta v_1, \alpha v_2 + \beta v_2, \alpha v_3 + \beta v_3) = \alpha \mathbf{v} + \beta \mathbf{v} \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom S5 (Identity scalar):**

$$1 \cdot \mathbf{v} = (1 \cdot v_1, 1 \cdot v_2, 1 \cdot v_3) = (v_1, v_2, v_3) = \mathbf{v} \; \checkmark$$

All eight axioms are satisfied, hence $\mathbb{R}^3$ is a vector space over $\mathbb{R}$.

---

**Part (b) — Subspace test for $W$:**

**Step 1 — Zero test:** $\mathbf{0} = (0,0,0)$: $0 + 2(0) - 0 = 0$. ✓

**Step 2 — Closure under addition:** Let $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in W$, so $u_1 + 2u_2 - u_3 = 0$ and $v_1 + 2v_2 - v_3 = 0$.

For $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} = (u_1 + v_1, u_2 + v_2, u_3 + v_3)$:

$$(u_1 + v_1) + 2(u_2 + v_2) - (u_3 + v_3) = (u_1 + 2u_2 - u_3) + (v_1 + 2v_2 - v_3) = 0 + 0 = 0$$

So $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in W$. ✓

**Step 3 — Closure under scalar multiplication:** For $\alpha \mathbf{u} = (\alpha u_1, \alpha u_2, \alpha u_3)$:

$$\alpha u_1 + 2(\alpha u_2) - (\alpha u_3) = \alpha(u_1 + 2u_2 - u_3) = \alpha \cdot 0 = 0$$

So $\alpha \mathbf{u} \in W$. ✓

**Therefore, $W$ is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$.**

**Finding a basis for $W$:** From $a + 2b - c = 0$, we get $c = a + 2b$. So every vector in $W$ is:

$$(a, b, a + 2b) = a(1, 0, 1) + b(0, 1, 2)$$

Let $\mathbf{f}_1 = (1, 0, 1)$ and $\mathbf{f}_2 = (0, 1, 2)$. They are linearly independent (neither is a scalar multiple of the other). Thus:

$$\text{Basis of } W = \{(1, 0, 1), \; (0, 1, 2)\}, \quad \dim(W) = 2$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Show that the set $V = M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ of all $2 \times 2$ real matrices, with operations of matrix addition and scalar multiplication, is a vector space. Verify **any four** axioms in detail.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the subset

$$S = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \in M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}) : a + d = 0 \right\}$$

Prove that $S$ is a subspace of $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$. Find a basis for $S$ and its dimension.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Verifying $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ as a vector space:**

Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} e & f \\ g & h \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} p & q \\ r & s \end{bmatrix} \in M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ and $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Axiom A1 (Closure under addition):**

$$A + B = \begin{bmatrix} a+e & b+f \\ c+g & d+h \end{bmatrix} \in M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}) \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom A4 (Zero matrix):**
The zero matrix $O = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ satisfies $A + O = A$ for any $A$. ✓

**Axiom A5 (Additive inverse):**
For any $A$, define $-A = \begin{bmatrix} -a & -b \\ -c & -d \end{bmatrix}$. Then:

$$A + (-A) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = O \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom S2 (Distributivity over matrix addition):**

$$\alpha(A + B) = \alpha \begin{bmatrix} a+e & b+f \\ c+g & d+h \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha(a+e) & \alpha(b+f) \\ \alpha(c+g) & \alpha(d+h) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} \alpha a + \alpha e & \alpha b + \alpha f \\ \alpha c + \alpha g & \alpha d + \alpha h \end{bmatrix} = \alpha A + \alpha B \; \checkmark$$

**Axiom S5 (Identity scalar):**

$$1 \cdot A = 1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = A \; \checkmark$$

Hence $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ satisfies all vector space axioms.

---

**Part (b) — Subspace test for $S$:**

**Step 1 — Zero matrix test:** The zero matrix $O$ has $a + d = 0 + 0 = 0$. So $O \in S$. ✓

**Step 2 — Closure under addition:** Let $A, B \in S$ with $a + d = 0$ and $e + h = 0$. Then:

$$(A + B)_{11} + (A + B)_{22} = (a + e) + (d + h) = (a + d) + (e + h) = 0 + 0 = 0$$

So $A + B \in S$. ✓

**Step 3 — Closure under scalar multiplication:** Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$ and $A \in S$. Then:

$$(\alpha A)_{11} + (\alpha A)_{22} = \alpha a + \alpha d = \alpha(a + d) = \alpha \cdot 0 = 0$$

So $\alpha A \in S$. ✓

**Therefore, $S$ is a subspace of $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$.**

**Finding a basis for $S$:** The condition $a + d = 0$ gives $d = -a$. So any matrix in $S$ has the form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & -a \end{bmatrix} = a \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} + b \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + c \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Define the three matrices:

$$F_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad F_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad F_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Linear independence check:** If $c_1 F_1 + c_2 F_2 + c_3 F_3 = O$, then:

$$\begin{bmatrix} c_1 & c_2 \\ c_3 & -c_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \implies c_1 = c_2 = c_3 = 0$$

So $\{F_1, F_2, F_3\}$ is linearly independent, hence a basis.

$$\boxed{\text{Basis of } S = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \right\}, \quad \dim(S) = 3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing matrix addition with matrix multiplication** (1–2 marks deducted): The vector space structure uses *entry-wise* addition. Using standard matrix multiplication to "verify" axioms is a fundamental error.
> 2. **Skipping the zero-vector check** (1 mark deducted): Many students jump to closure without first verifying $\mathbf{0} \in V$ or $\mathbf{0} \in W$. The zero test is the easiest 1 mark to grab — don't skip it!
> 3. **Forgetting to express basis vectors in their final matrix form** (1 mark deducted): Writing "basis is $\{F_1, F_2, F_3\}$" without explicitly displaying the matrices is incomplete.
> 4. **Not verifying linear independence of the basis** (1 mark deducted): KTU examiners want explicit confirmation that the basis vectors are linearly independent (typically by setting a linear combination to zero and showing all coefficients vanish).
> 5. **Treating "verify axioms" as "state axioms"** (2–3 marks deducted): The question demands *explicit computation* with chosen vectors, not just a list of axiom names.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute exam prep):**
>
> ✅ **$\mathbb{R}^n$ Definition:** Set of all ordered $n$-tuples of real numbers. Dimension = $n$. Standard basis has $n$ vectors $\mathbf{e}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_n$.
>
> ✅ **$M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R})$ Definition:** Set of all $2 \times 2$ matrices with real entries. Dimension = $4$. Standard basis = $\{E_{11}, E_{12}, E_{21}, E_{22}\}$.
>
> ✅ **Vector Space Isomorphism:** $M_{2 \times 2}(\mathbb{R}) \cong \mathbb{R}^4$ via the map that reads matrix entries row-wise as a 4-tuple.
>
> ✅ **Eight Axioms to Verify:** A1 (closure +), A2 (commutativity), A3 (associativity), A4 (zero), A5 (inverse), S1 (closure scalar), S2 (distributivity I), S3 (distributivity II), S4 (associativity scalar), S5 (identity scalar). *(Ten conditions, eight "axioms" depending on textbook grouping.)*
>
> ✅ **Three-Step Subspace Test:** (i) $\mathbf{0} \in W$, (ii) closure under addition, (iii) closure under scalar multiplication. **All three are mandatory.**
>
> ✅ **Linear Combination:** $\mathbf{v} = c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 \mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k \mathbf{v}_k$. In $\mathbb{R}^n$, set up a system of $n$ equations in $k$ unknowns.
>
> ✅ **Linear Combination in $M_{2 \times 2}$:** When using the standard basis, the coefficients are simply the four matrix entries.
>
> ✅ **Key Trap:** A set defined by a *non-homogeneous* or *nonlinear* condition (e.g., $ad - bc = 1$, $a^2 + b^2 = 1$) is **not** a subspace. The defining equation must be a *homogeneous linear* condition on the coordinates.
>
> ✅ **Coordinate-wise Inheritance Principle:** Because operations in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $M_{2 \times 2}$ are defined entry-by-entry using ordinary real-number arithmetic, all field axioms of $\mathbb{R}$ are "inherited" by these vector spaces. This is the central conceptual reason for why they are vector spaces.
>
> ✅ **Dimension facts to remember cold:** $\dim(\mathbb{R}^n) = n$; $\dim(M_{2 \times 2}) = 4$; $\dim(M_{m \times n}) = mn$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Inner Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2 > - **Course Code:** GAMAT201 > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Properties of Inner Products > - **Syllabus Tag:** Linear Algebra – Inner Product Spaces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Inner Product Let $V$ be a vector space over the field $\mathbb{F}$, where $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{C}$. An **inner product** on $V$ is a function $$\langle \cdot , \cdot \rangle : V \times V \longrightarrow \mathbb{F}$$ that satisfies the following four axioms for all $u, v, w \in V$ and $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{F}$: 1. **Conjugate Symmetry:** $\langle u, v \rangle = \overline{\langle v, u \rangle}$ 2. **Linearity in the First Argument:** $\langle \alpha u + \beta w, v \rangle = \alpha \langle u, v \rangle + \beta \langle w, v \rangle$ 3. **Positivity:** $\langle v, v \rangle \geq 0$ 4. **Definiteness:** $\langle v, v \rangle = 0 \iff v = 0$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A vector space $V$ equipped with an inner product $\langle \cdot , \cdot \rangle$ is called an **Inner Product Space (IPS)**. The pair $(V, \langle \cdot , \cdot \rangle)$ is the fundamental object of study in this module. > [!NOTE] > From linearity in the first argument and conjugate symmetry, we can derive **conjugate linearity in the second argument**: > $\langle u, \alpha v + \beta w \rangle = \overline{\alpha} \langle u, v \rangle + \overline{\beta} \langle u, w \rangle$. This is a derived property, not an independent axiom. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Geometric Glasses" Viewpoint Imagine you are a tiny observer standing inside a giant room full of arrows (vectors). The **inner product** is the special "measuring instrument" you use to ask two questions about any pair of arrows: - **How long is this arrow?** (Length / Magnitude) - **How aligned are these two arrows?** (Angle / Similarity) > **Real-world Analogy:** Think of a music streaming app. The app represents every song as a vector in a high-dimensional space. The inner product $\langle \text{Song}_A, \text{Song}_B \rangle$ tells you how *similar* the two songs are — a high value means the songs sound alike, a zero value means they are completely "orthogonal" (independent in taste), and a negative value means they are opposite in mood. Without inner products, vectors are just "floating arrows" with no notion of length or angle. With an inner product, the space acquires a **geometry** — a way to measure distances, angles, and projections. This is why inner product spaces are often called the natural bridge between **algebra** and **geometry**. ## 1.3 Standard Examples (For Instant Recognition) > [!NOTE] > **Examples to remember for KTU exams:** | Space | Inner Product | Field | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i$ (Standard Dot Product) | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $\mathbb{C}^n$ | $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \overline{y_i}$ | $\mathbb{C}$ | | $\ell^2$ (square-summable sequences) | $\langle (x_n), (y_n) \rangle = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x_n \overline{y_n}$ | $\mathbb{C}$ | | $C[a,b]$ (continuous functions) | $\langle f, g \rangle = \int_a^b f(x) \overline{g(x)} \, dx$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | | $M_{m \times n}(\mathbb{R})$ | $\langle A, B \rangle = \text{tr}(B^T A)$ (Frobenius Inner Product) | $\mathbb{R}$ | ## 1.4 The Induced Norm (A First Critical Property) Every inner product induces a **norm** (length) on the vector space: $$\| v \| = \sqrt{\langle v, v \rangle}$$ This norm satisfies the standard axioms (positive definiteness, homogeneity, triangle inequality). The pair $(V, \| \cdot \|)$ becomes a **normed linear space**, and the inner product gives rise to a natural **distance function**: $$d(u, v) = \| u - v \|$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Not every norm comes from an inner product.** The norm must satisfy the **Parallelogram Law** to be induced by an inner product. This is the content of the famous **von Neumann–Jordan Theorem** (a frequent 14-mark question in KTU). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Derived Algebraic Properties of Inner Products Starting from the four axioms, the following identities can be derived. These appear frequently in derivations: **P1. Symmetry consequence:** $\langle 0, v \rangle = \langle v, 0 \rangle = 0$ *Why:* Apply linearity with $\alpha = 0$, or use the fact that $\langle 0, 0 \rangle = 0$ from positivity, then conjugate symmetry. **P2. Real part identity:** $\langle u, v \rangle + \langle v, u \rangle = 2 \operatorname{Re} \langle u, v \rangle$ *Why:* Conjugate symmetry gives $\overline{\langle u,v \rangle} = \langle v, u \rangle$. Adding $\langle u, v \rangle$ to both sides yields the sum of a complex number and its conjugate, which equals $2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle$. **P3. Norm squared of sum:** $\| u + v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$ **P4. Norm squared of difference:** $\| u - v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 - 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$ **P5. Polarization Identity** (recovers the inner product from the norm): - **Real case:** $\langle u, v \rangle = \dfrac{1}{4}\left( \| u + v \|^2 - \| u - v \|^2 \right)$ - **Complex case:** $\langle u, v \rangle = \dfrac{1}{4}\left( \| u + v \|^2 - \| u - v \|^2 + i\| u + iv \|^2 - i\| u - iv \|^2 \right)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Polarization Identity is a **KTU favorite** for 7-mark questions. It is the *only* way to recover the inner product from the norm, and it is the foundation of the Parallelogram Law. ## 2.2 The Three Foundational Inequalities These three inequalities form the "trinity" of inner product space properties. ### 2.2.1 Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality (The Crown Jewel) $$|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \, \| v \|$$ with equality **if and only if** $u$ and $v$ are linearly dependent (i.e., one is a scalar multiple of the other). ### 2.2.2 Triangle Inequality $$\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \|$$ with equality **if and only if** one of $u, v$ is a non-negative scalar multiple of the other. ### 2.2.3 Parallelogram Law $$\| u + v \|^2 + \| u - v \|^2 = 2\left( \| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2 \right)$$ > [!NOTE] > Geometrically, the parallelogram law states: *In a parallelogram with sides $u$ and $v$, the sum of the squares of the diagonals equals twice the sum of the squares of the sides.* This law **characterizes** inner product norms (necessary and sufficient condition). ## 2.3 Orthogonality — The Geometric Heart of IPS - **Definition:** Vectors $u, v \in V$ are **orthogonal**, written $u \perp v$, if $\langle u, v \rangle = 0$. - **Orthogonal Set:** A subset $S \subseteq V$ is orthogonal if $\langle u, v \rangle = 0$ for all distinct $u, v \in S$. - **Orthonormal Set:** An orthogonal set in which every vector is a unit vector ($\| v \| = 1$). - **Pythagorean Theorem (IPS version):** If $u \perp v$, then $$\| u + v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2$$ > **Intuition:** Orthogonal vectors are the "independent directions" in the space. Just as the x-axis and y-axis are independent in $\mathbb{R}^2$, orthogonal vectors carry information that does not overlap. ## 2.4 Orthogonal Complement For any subset $S \subseteq V$, define the **orthogonal complement** of $S$ as: $$S^{\perp} = \{ v \in V \mid \langle v, s \rangle = 0 \ \text{for all} \ s \in S \}$$ **Key Properties:** - $S^{\perp}$ is always a **subspace** of $V$. - $S \cap S^{\perp} = \{0\}$ (if $S$ is a subspace). - $(S^{\perp})^{\perp}$ contains $S$ (equality holds for closed subspaces in Hilbert spaces). - **Fundamental Decomposition Theorem:** If $W$ is a finite-dimensional subspace of an IPS $V$, then $$V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$$ This means every vector $v \in V$ can be written **uniquely** as $v = w + z$ where $w \in W$ and $z \in W^{\perp}$. The component $w$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $v$ onto $W$. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams** | Property | Formula | Equality Holds When | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Induced Norm | $\lVert v \rVert = \sqrt{\langle v, v \rangle}$ | Always defined | | Cauchy–Schwarz | $\vert \langle u, v \rangle \vert \leq \lVert u \rVert \lVert v \rVert$ | $u, v$ linearly dependent | | Triangle Inequality | $\lVert u + v \rVert \leq \lVert u \rVert + \lVert v \rVert$ | $u = \alpha v$ with $\alpha \geq 0$ | | Reverse Triangle | $\lvert \lVert u \rVert - \lVert v \rVert \rvert \leq \lVert u - v \rVert$ | Always | | Parallelogram Law | $\lVert u + v \rVert^2 + \lVert u - v \rVert^2 = 2(\lVert u \rVert^2 + \lVert v \rVert^2)$ | Always (characterizes IPS) | | Norm-Squared Sum | $\lVert u + v \rVert^2 = \lVert u \rVert^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \lVert v \rVert^2$ | Always | | Pythagorean Theorem | $\lVert u + v \rVert^2 = \lVert u \rVert^2 + \lVert v \rVert^2$ | $u \perp v$ | | Polarization (Real) | $\langle u, v \rangle = \tfrac{1}{4}(\lVert u + v \rVert^2 - \lVert u - v \rVert^2)$ | $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{R}$ | | Polarization (Complex) | $\langle u, v \rangle = \tfrac{1}{4}(\lVert u + v \rVert^2 - \lVert u - v \rVert^2 + i\lVert u + iv \rVert^2 - i\lVert u - iv \rVert^2)$ | $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{C}$ | | Orthogonal Decomposition | $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$ | $W$ finite-dimensional subspace | | Bessel's Inequality | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert \langle v, e_i \rangle \vert^2 \leq \lVert v \rVert^2$ | $\{e_i\}$ orthonormal | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why should a CS / Information Science student care about inner product properties?** - **Machine Learning:** The *kernel trick* in Support Vector Machines (SVMs) is built entirely on inner products in high-dimensional (or even infinite-dimensional) spaces. Properties like positive-definiteness of the inner product are what make kernels mathematically valid. - **Signal Processing:** The *Fourier Transform* decomposes a signal into orthogonal basis functions (sine and cosine). The inner product $\langle f, g \rangle = \int f(x) \overline{g(x)} dx$ measures the "energy overlap" between signals. - **Quantum Computing:** States of quantum systems live in a complex inner product space called a *Hilbert Space*. Orthogonality corresponds to mutually exclusive measurement outcomes. - **Computer Graphics:** Orthogonal projections, used in 3D-to-2D rendering pipelines, rely on the orthogonal decomposition $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** Every algebraic step is followed by a text annotation explaining the logical transition. The "Apply" and "Use" tags tell you exactly which axiom is being invoked at each line. --- ## 3.1 Derivation #1: The Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality **Statement:** For all $u, v \in V$, $|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \, \| v \|$. **Proof:** ### Case 1: $v = 0$ If $v = 0$, then $\langle u, v \rangle = \langle u, 0 \rangle = 0$ and $\| v \| = 0$. So $|\langle u, v \rangle| = 0 = \| u \| \cdot 0 = \| u \| \| v \|$. The inequality holds trivially with equality. ### Case 2: $v \neq 0$ **Step 1:** Define an auxiliary quadratic function. For any real parameter $t \in \mathbb{R}$, consider the vector $u - t v$ (we will use a real multiple, not a complex one, to ensure the expression is real-valued). $$f(t) = \| u - t v \|^2$$ **Step 2:** Expand the squared norm using the inner product axioms. $$f(t) = \langle u - t v, \, u - t v \rangle$$ **Step 3:** Apply linearity in the first argument to expand. $$= \langle u, u \rangle - t \langle v, u \rangle - t \langle u, v \rangle + t^2 \langle v, v \rangle$$ **Step 4:** Use conjugate symmetry: $\langle v, u \rangle = \overline{\langle u, v \rangle}$. $$= \| u \|^2 - t \overline{\langle u, v \rangle} - t \langle u, v \rangle + t^2 \| v \|^2$$ **Step 5:** Recognize that $\langle u, v \rangle + \overline{\langle u, v \rangle} = 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle$. $$= \| u \|^2 - 2 t \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + t^2 \| v \|^2$$ **Step 6:** Apply the positivity axiom. Since $f(t) = \| u - t v \|^2 \geq 0$ for **all** $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (it is the square of a norm, hence non-negative), this quadratic in $t$ has no real roots (or a double root). Thus its **discriminant must be $\leq 0$**. **Step 7:** Identify the coefficients of the quadratic $At^2 + Bt + C$ with $A = \| v \|^2 > 0$ (since $v \neq 0$), $B = -2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle$, $C = \| u \|^2$. **Step 8:** Apply the discriminant condition $B^2 - 4AC \leq 0$. $$\left( -2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right)^2 - 4 \| v \|^2 \| u \|^2 \leq 0$$ **Step 9:** Simplify. $$4 \left( \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right)^2 \leq 4 \| u \|^2 \| v \|^2$$ **Step 10:** Divide by 4 and take square roots (all quantities non-negative). $$\left( \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right)^2 \leq \| u \|^2 \| v \|^2$$ $$\left| \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right| \leq \| u \| \, \| v \|$$ **Step 11:** Use the elementary fact that for any complex number $z$, $|\operatorname{Re}(z)| \leq |z|$. $$|\langle u, v \rangle| \geq \left| \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right|$$ > **Technical Detail:** Apply: For any $z \in \mathbb{C}$, write $z = a + ib$ with $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $|z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} \geq \sqrt{a^2} = |a| = |\operatorname{Re}(z)|$. **Step 12:** Combine the inequalities from Steps 10 and 11. $$|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \, \| v \|$$ > **Equality Condition (Important for full marks):** Equality holds iff $f(t) = 0$ for some $t$, which means $u - t v = 0$, i.e., $u$ and $v$ are linearly dependent. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2: The Triangle Inequality **Statement:** $\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \|$. **Proof:** **Step 1:** Compute $\| u + v \|^2$ by expanding the norm squared. $$\| u + v \|^2 = \langle u + v, \, u + v \rangle$$ **Step 2:** Apply linearity in the first argument. $$= \langle u, u \rangle + \langle u, v \rangle + \langle v, u \rangle + \langle v, v \rangle$$ **Step 3:** Combine the inner product terms using $\langle u, v \rangle + \langle v, u \rangle = 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle$. $$= \| u \|^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 4:** Apply the property $|x| \geq x$ for any real number $x$, so $2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \leq 2 \left| \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right|$. $$\| u + v \|^2 \leq \| u \|^2 + 2 \left| \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right| + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 5:** Use the inequality $|\operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle| \leq |\langle u, v \rangle|$. $$\| u + v \|^2 \leq \| u \|^2 + 2 |\langle u, v \rangle| + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 6:** Apply the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** $|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \| v \|$. $$\| u + v \|^2 \leq \| u \|^2 + 2 \| u \| \| v \| + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 7:** Recognize the right-hand side as a perfect square: $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ with $a = \| u \|, b = \| v \|$. $$\| u + v \|^2 \leq (\| u \| + \| v \|)^2$$ **Step 8:** Take the non-negative square root of both sides (norms are non-negative). $$\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \|$$ > **Equality Condition:** Equality holds iff $\langle u, v \rangle = \| u \| \| v \|$ (real and non-negative) and $|\langle u, v \rangle| = \| u \| \| v \|$, which together imply that $u$ and $v$ are non-negative scalar multiples of each other. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3: The Parallelogram Law **Statement:** $\| u + v \|^2 + \| u - v \|^2 = 2 \left( \| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2 \right)$. **Proof:** **Step 1:** Expand $\| u + v \|^2$ using the inner product. $$\| u + v \|^2 = \langle u + v, u + v \rangle = \| u \|^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 2:** Expand $\| u - v \|^2$ using the inner product. $$\| u - v \|^2 = \langle u - v, u - v \rangle = \| u \|^2 - 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 3:** Add the two equations from Steps 1 and 2. $$\| u + v \|^2 + \| u - v \|^2 = \left( \| u \|^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2 \right) + \left( \| u \|^2 - 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2 \right)$$ **Step 4:** The $\pm 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle$ terms cancel out (this is the crucial step). $$= 2 \| u \|^2 + 2 \| v \|^2$$ **Step 5:** Factor out 2. $$= 2 \left( \| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2 \right)$$ > **Geometric Interpretation:** In a parallelogram with sides represented by vectors $u$ and $v$, the two diagonals are $u + v$ and $u - v$. The Parallelogram Law states: *sum of squared diagonals = 2 × sum of squared sides*. This is a classical Euclidean result that generalizes to all inner product spaces. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation #4: The Polarization Identity (Real Case) **Statement:** $\langle u, v \rangle = \dfrac{1}{4} \left( \| u + v \|^2 - \| u - v \|^2 \right)$ for real inner product spaces. **Proof:** **Step 1:** Expand $\| u + v \|^2$ and $\| u - v \|^2$ separately (in the real case, $\operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle = \langle u, v \rangle$). $$\| u + v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 + 2 \langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ $$\| u - v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 - 2 \langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ **Step 2:** Subtract the second from the first. $$\| u + v \|^2 - \| u - v \|^2 = 4 \langle u, v \rangle$$ **Step 3:** Divide both sides by 4. $$\langle u, v \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \left( \| u + v \|^2 - \| u - v \|^2 \right)$$ $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Verifying a Custom Inner Product **Problem:** Verify that $\langle f, g \rangle = \int_0^1 f(x) g(x) \, dx$ is a valid inner product on the space of continuous real-valued functions on $[0, 1]$, and compute $\langle x, x^2 \rangle$. **Solution:** **Step 1 (Symmetry):** $\langle f, g \rangle = \int_0^1 f(x) g(x) \, dx = \int_0^1 g(x) f(x) \, dx = \langle g, f \rangle$. ✓ **Step 2 (Linearity):** $\langle \alpha f_1 + \beta f_2, g \rangle = \int_0^1 (\alpha f_1(x) + \beta f_2(x)) g(x) \, dx = \alpha \int_0^1 f_1 g + \beta \int_0^1 f_2 g = \alpha \langle f_1, g \rangle + \beta \langle f_2, g \rangle$. ✓ **Step 3 (Positivity):** $\langle f, f \rangle = \int_0^1 f(x)^2 \, dx \geq 0$ since $f(x)^2 \geq 0$. ✓ **Step 4 (Definiteness):** If $\langle f, f \rangle = 0$, then $\int_0^1 f(x)^2 \, dx = 0$. Since $f^2$ is continuous and non-negative, $f^2 \equiv 0$, so $f \equiv 0$. ✓ **Step 5 (Computation):** $$\langle x, x^2 \rangle = \int_0^1 x \cdot x^2 \, dx = \int_0^1 x^3 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{4}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Inner Product Property Hierarchy This diagram shows how the four axioms of an inner product give rise to all the derived properties and inequalities. ```mermaid flowchart TD N1["Four Inner Product Axioms
Conjugate Symmetry, Linearity,
Positivity, Definiteness"] N2["Induced Norm
||v|| = sqrt of inner product of v with v"] N3["Norm Properties
Positive Definite, Homogeneous,
Triangle Inequality"] N4["Cauchy Schwarz Inequality
|inner product of u and v| less than or equal to ||u|| times ||v||"] N5["Triangle Inequality
||u+v|| less than or equal to ||u|| + ||v||"] N6["Parallelogram Law
Sum of squared diagonal norms equals 2 times sum of squared side norms"] N7["Polarization Identity
Recovers inner product from the induced norm"] N8["Pythagorean Theorem
||u+v|| squared equals ||u|| squared + ||v|| squared when orthogonal"] N9["Orthogonal Decomposition
V equals W direct sum with W perp"] N10["Orthogonal Complement
All vectors orthogonal to a subspace"] N11["Bessel Inequality
Sum of squared projections bounded by norm squared"] N12["Gram Schmidt Process
Constructs orthonormal bases"] N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 N2 --> N4 N2 --> N6 N6 --> N7 N4 --> N5 N1 --> N10 N10 --> N9 N1 --> N8 N4 --> N11 N2 --> N12 style N1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style N2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style N4 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style N5 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style N6 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style N9 fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Orthogonal Decomposition Architecture This block diagram illustrates how the **Fundamental Decomposition Theorem** splits an inner product space using any subspace. ```mermaid flowchart LR VA["Inner Product Space V"] WS["Subspace W
(finite dimensional)"] WO["Orthogonal Complement
W perp = z in V such that inner product of z and w is 0 for all w in W"] VP["Vector v in V"] VW["Projection w in W"] VZ["Orthogonal Component z in W perp"] VA --> WS VA --> WO VP --> VW VP --> VZ WS --> WO VW ---|"inner product of w and z equals 0"| VZ style VA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style WS fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style WO fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style VP fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style VW fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style VZ fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Logical Flow: From Norm to Inner Product (von Neumann Theorem) This flowchart visualizes the **only-if** direction of the famous theorem characterizing inner product norms. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Start: A normed space V with norm"] A1["Does the norm satisfy the
Parallelogram Law?"] A2["YES: Inner product exists"] A3["Define inner product using
Polarization Identity"] A4["Verify all 4 axioms hold
using parallelogram law"] A5["V becomes an Inner Product Space"] A6["NO: Parallelogram Law fails"] A7["V is a normed space but
NOT an inner product space"] A8["Counterexample: L-infinity norm
on R squared"] A0 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 A1 --> A6 A6 --> A7 A7 --> A8 style A1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A5 fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A7 fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality for an inner product space. When does equality hold?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (2 marks):** For any two vectors $u, v$ in an inner product space $V$, $$|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \, \| v \|$$ **Equality Condition (1 mark):** Equality holds if and only if $u$ and $v$ are linearly dependent, i.e., one is a scalar multiple of the other. Specifically, there exists a scalar $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}$ such that $u = \alpha v$ or $v = \alpha u$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write the equality condition as "$u$ and $v$ are parallel." While geometrically correct, the KTU board expects the precise phrase **"linearly dependent"** for full credit. One-word answers lose 1 mark. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define orthogonal complement. If $W$ is a subspace of an inner product space $V$, prove that $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The orthogonal complement of a non-empty subset $S \subseteq V$ is $$S^{\perp} = \{ v \in V \mid \langle v, s \rangle = 0 \ \text{for all} \ s \in S \}$$ **Proof (2 marks):** Let $x \in W \cap W^{\perp}$. By definition of intersection, $x \in W$ and $x \in W^{\perp}$. Since $x \in W^{\perp}$, we have $\langle x, w \rangle = 0$ for all $w \in W$. In particular, take $w = x \in W$. Then $\langle x, x \rangle = 0$. By the **definiteness axiom** of the inner product, $\langle x, x \rangle = 0 \iff x = 0$. Therefore $x = 0$, proving $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing $\langle x, x \rangle = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$ without citing the **definiteness axiom** explicitly. This loses 1 mark. Always name the axiom! --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** (you must answer either Option A or Option B in full). Both options are presented below for exam practice. --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]` #### Part (a): State and prove the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality. (7 marks) **Statement (1 mark):** For all $u, v$ in an inner product space $V$, $$|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \| v \|$$ **Proof (6 marks):** **Case 1: $v = 0$.** Trivially, $|\langle u, 0 \rangle| = 0 = \| u \| \cdot 0 = \| u \| \| v \|$. The inequality holds with equality. **Case 2: $v \neq 0$.** For any real parameter $t$, define $$f(t) = \| u - t v \|^2 = \langle u - t v, u - t v \rangle$$ By linearity in the first argument: $$f(t) = \langle u, u \rangle - t \langle v, u \rangle - t \langle u, v \rangle + t^2 \langle v, v \rangle$$ Using conjugate symmetry ($\langle v, u \rangle = \overline{\langle u, v \rangle}$) and the identity $z + \bar{z} = 2 \operatorname{Re}(z)$: $$f(t) = \| u \|^2 - 2t \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + t^2 \| v \|^2$$ Since $f(t) = \| u - t v \|^2 \geq 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, this is a non-negative quadratic in $t$. Therefore its discriminant is $\leq 0$: $$\left[ -2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right]^2 - 4 \cdot \| v \|^2 \cdot \| u \|^2 \leq 0$$ $$4 \left( \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right)^2 \leq 4 \| u \|^2 \| v \|^2$$ Taking square roots: $\left| \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right| \leq \| u \| \| v \|$. Since $|\operatorname{Re}(z)| \leq |z|$ for all complex $z$, we conclude $$|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \| v \| \quad \blacksquare$$ #### Part (b): Prove the triangle inequality. (7 marks) **Statement (1 mark):** For all $u, v \in V$, $$\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \|$$ **Proof (6 marks):** $$\| u + v \|^2 = \langle u + v, u + v \rangle = \| u \|^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ Since $2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \leq 2 \left| \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle \right| \leq 2 |\langle u, v \rangle|$, and applying Cauchy–Schwarz: $$\| u + v \|^2 \leq \| u \|^2 + 2 |\langle u, v \rangle| + \| v \|^2 \leq \| u \|^2 + 2 \| u \| \| v \| + \| v \|^2 = (\| u \| + \| v \|)^2$$ Taking the non-negative square root of both sides: $$\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \| \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question A):** > - **Lose 2 marks** if you don't separate into cases $v = 0$ and $v \neq 0$ in the Cauchy–Schwarz proof. > - **Lose 1 mark** in the triangle inequality if you skip the intermediate step $|\operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle| \leq |\langle u, v \rangle|$ and jump directly to Cauchy–Schwarz. > - **Lose 1 mark** if you forget to mention that norms are non-negative before taking the square root in the final step. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]` #### Part (a): Define an inner product. State and prove the parallelogram law. (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** An inner product on a vector space $V$ over $\mathbb{F}$ is a function $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{F}$ satisfying: 1. Conjugate symmetry: $\langle u, v \rangle = \overline{\langle v, u \rangle}$ 2. Linearity: $\langle \alpha u + \beta w, v \rangle = \alpha \langle u, v \rangle + \beta \langle w, v \rangle$ 3. Positivity: $\langle v, v \rangle \geq 0$ 4. Definiteness: $\langle v, v \rangle = 0 \iff v = 0$ **Statement of Parallelogram Law (1 mark):** For all $u, v \in V$, $$\| u + v \|^2 + \| u - v \|^2 = 2 (\| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2)$$ **Proof (4 marks):** $$\| u + v \|^2 = \langle u + v, u + v \rangle = \| u \|^2 + \langle u, v \rangle + \langle v, u \rangle + \| v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 + 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ $$\| u - v \|^2 = \langle u - v, u - v \rangle = \| u \|^2 - \langle u, v \rangle - \langle v, u \rangle + \| v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 - 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle + \| v \|^2$$ Adding: $$\| u + v \|^2 + \| u - v \|^2 = 2 \| u \|^2 + 2 \| v \|^2 = 2(\| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2) \quad \blacksquare$$ #### Part (b): Define orthogonal complement. If $W$ is a finite-dimensional subspace of $V$, prove that $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$. (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** $$W^{\perp} = \{ v \in V \mid \langle v, w \rangle = 0 \ \text{for all} \ w \in W \}$$ **Proof (6 marks):** **Step 1: Show $W^{\perp}$ is a subspace of $V$ (2 marks).** - Zero vector: $\langle 0, w \rangle = 0$ for all $w \in W$, so $0 \in W^{\perp}$. - Closure: If $v_1, v_2 \in W^{\perp}$ and $\alpha \in \mathbb{F}$, then $\langle \alpha v_1 + v_2, w \rangle = \alpha \langle v_1, w \rangle + \langle v_2, w \rangle = 0 + 0 = 0$. So $\alpha v_1 + v_2 \in W^{\perp}$. **Step 2: Show $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$ (1 mark).** If $x \in W \cap W^{\perp}$, then $x \in W$ and $\langle x, w \rangle = 0$ for all $w \in W$. Taking $w = x$ gives $\langle x, x \rangle = 0$, so $x = 0$ by definiteness. **Step 3: Show $V = W + W^{\perp}$ (3 marks).** Let $v \in V$. Let $\{w_1, \ldots, w_k\}$ be an orthonormal basis of $W$ (obtained by applying Gram–Schmidt to any basis of $W$). Define the **orthogonal projection**: $$w = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, w_i \rangle w_i$$ and set $z = v - w$. For any $w_j$, $$\langle z, w_j \rangle = \langle v - w, w_j \rangle = \langle v, w_j \rangle - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, w_i \rangle \langle w_i, w_j \rangle = \langle v, w_j \rangle - \langle v, w_j \rangle \cdot 1 = 0$$ (since the basis is orthonormal, $\langle w_i, w_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$). So $z \in W^{\perp}$. Therefore $v = w + z \in W + W^{\perp}$. **Conclusion:** Since $V = W + W^{\perp}$ and $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$, we have $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$. $\blacksquare$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):** > - **Lose 2 marks** if you assume the existence of an orthonormal basis without justifying it (mention Gram–Schmidt explicitly). > - **Lose 1 mark** if you forget the subspace-closure part of the proof for $W^{\perp}$. > - **Lose 1 mark** if you do not invoke the **Kronecker delta** $\delta_{ij}$ explicitly when using orthonormality. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Pin this on your wall before the exam!** - ✅ The inner product is **defined by 4 axioms**: conjugate symmetry, linearity (first argument), positivity, and definiteness. - ✅ Conjugate linearity in the second argument is a **derived property**, not an axiom. - ✅ The induced norm is $\| v \| = \sqrt{\langle v, v \rangle}$ — this is the **only** way to extract length from an inner product. - ✅ **Cauchy–Schwarz:** $|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \| u \| \| v \|$ with equality iff $u, v$ are linearly dependent. Proof uses the discriminant of the quadratic $f(t) = \| u - tv \|^2$. - ✅ **Triangle inequality:** $\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \|$. Proof requires Cauchy–Schwarz as a lemma. - ✅ **Parallelogram law:** $\| u + v \|^2 + \| u - v \|^2 = 2(\| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2)$. The $\pm 2 \operatorname{Re}\langle u, v \rangle$ terms cancel — this is the "magic" of the proof. - ✅ **Polarization identity** recovers the inner product from the norm — used in both directions of the von Neumann characterization theorem. - ✅ **Pythagorean theorem in IPS:** If $u \perp v$, then $\| u + v \|^2 = \| u \|^2 + \| v \|^2$. - ✅ **Orthogonal complement** $W^{\perp}$ is always a subspace; $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$. - ✅ **Fundamental decomposition:** $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$ (for finite-dimensional $W$). - ✅ **Always cite the axiom** by name when you use it in a proof (definiteness, conjugate symmetry, etc.) — the KTU board deducts marks for "naked" use of axioms. - ✅ **Always separate the case** $v = 0$ in Cauchy–Schwarz proofs to avoid division by zero issues. - ✅ **Common engineering applications:** SVM kernels, Fourier analysis, quantum states, 3D projections in graphics.

topics.title: Definitions of Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definitions of Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Length (Arc Length)

In the calculus of curves, the **length of a plane curve** (also called the **arc length**) is defined as the limiting value of the sum of the lengths of approximating polygonal line segments inscribed in the curve, as the number of segments increases without bound and their lengths approach zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rigorous Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> Let $C$ be a curve defined by the function $y = f(x)$ on the interval $[a, b]$, where $f$ has a continuous derivative on $[a, b]$. The **arc length** $L$ of $C$ from $x = a$ to $x = b$ is defined as:
> 
> $$L = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \, dx$$
> 
> provided the improper integral converges.

### Variants of the Length Formula

The definition of length adapts to the parametric representation of the curve:

**Case 1 — Cartesian Form:** $y = f(x)$, $a \leq x \leq b$

$$L = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \, dx$$

**Case 2 — Parametric Form:** $x = x(t)$, $y = y(t)$, $\alpha \leq t \leq \beta$

$$L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^{2}} \, dt$$

**Case 3 — Polar Form:** $r = f(\theta)$, $\theta_{1} \leq \theta \leq \theta_{2}$

$$L = \int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} \sqrt{r^{2} + \left(\frac{dr}{d\theta}\right)^{2}} \, d\theta$$

**Case 4 — Space Curve (3D):** $\vec{r}(t) = \langle x(t),\, y(t),\, z(t) \rangle$

$$L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dz}{dt}\right)^{2}} \, dt$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Staircase Approximation" Picture:**
> 
> Imagine you are walking along a curved hill road from point $A$ to point $B$. You cannot measure the curved path directly with a ruler, so you do the following: drop a series of straight sticks (chords) connecting closely spaced points along the curve, like a staircase hugging the road. The sum of the lengths of these sticks is *approximately* the true road length. The **length of the curve** is defined as the limit of this approximation as the sticks become infinitely small and infinite in number — i.e., the staircase becomes the road itself.

| Intuitive Term | Mathematical Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Curved hill road | The continuous curve $C$ |
| Straight sticks (chords) | Line segments joining partition points |
| Sum of stick lengths | Riemann sum $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$ |
| Limit as sticks shrink | $\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx$ |
| Actual road length | Arc length $L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "limit" is needed:** A curve is *infinitely* wiggly in principle. A single finite measurement cannot capture it. Calculus resolves this through the limit process, which is the foundation on which the entire integral rests.

---

## 1.3 Units and Standard Metrics

> The SI unit of **length** (in any representation) is the **metre (m)**. In purely mathematical (dimensionless) problems, the unit is simply declared as **units (u)**.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Arc length | $L$ | **m (metre)** |
| Differential of length | $ds$ | **m (metre)** |
| Speed (rate of change of arc length) | $ds/dt$ | **m/s** |

The element of arc length $ds$ is a foundational differential form:

$$ds = \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \, dx \quad \text{(Cartesian)}$$

$$ds = \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^{2}} \, dt \quad \text{(Parametric)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation

The mathematical edifice of "length of a curve" rests on a precise logical chain. We will now unpack each link in that chain.

### Step 1 — Partition the Domain
Let $C$ be defined by $y = f(x)$ on $[a, b]$. Partition $[a, b]$ into $n$ sub-intervals using points $a = x_{0} < x_{1} < x_{2} < \dots < x_{n} = b$. Let $\Delta x_{i} = x_{i} - x_{i-1}$.

### Step 2 — Inscribe Polygonal Approximation
For each sub-interval, erect a vertical line from $(x_{i-1}, f(x_{i-1}))$ to the curve, and then a chord to $(x_{i}, f(x_{i}))$. The length of the $i$-th chord is:

$$\ell_{i} = \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$$

where $\Delta y_{i} = f(x_{i}) - f(x_{i-1})$.

### Step 3 — Form the Riemann Sum
The total length of the polygonal approximation is:

$$L_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Mean Value Theorem
By the **Mean Value Theorem**, there exists $x_{i}^{*} \in [x_{i-1}, x_{i}]$ such that:

$$f(x_{i}) - f(x_{i-1}) = f'(x_{i}^{*}) \, \Delta x_{i}$$

Therefore:

$$\ell_{i} = \sqrt{1 + [f'(x_{i}^{*})]^{2}} \, \Delta x_{i}$$

### Step 5 — Take the Limit
The arc length is the limit of $L_{n}$ as $\|P\| \to 0$:

$$L = \lim_{\|P\| \to 0} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{1 + [f'(x_{i}^{*})]^{2}} \, \Delta x_{i} = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^{2}} \, dx$$

### Step 6 — Why This Works (The "Why")
The function $g(x) = \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^{2}}$ is **continuous** (since $f'$ is continuous), and therefore **integrable** on $[a, b]$ by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. The existence of the limit is guaranteed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sufficient Condition for Rectifiability:**
> A curve $C$ is said to be **rectifiable** (i.e., has a finite, well-defined length) if and only if $f'(x)$ is continuous on $[a, b]$. The arc-length integral is the standard measure used to test rectifiability in elementary calculus.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Curve Form | Length Element $ds$ | Arc Length $L$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $y = f(x)$, $a \leq x \leq b$ | $ds = \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx$ | $L = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx$ |
| 2 | $x = g(y)$, $c \leq y \leq d$ | $ds = \sqrt{1 + (x')^{2}} \, dy$ | $L = \int_{c}^{d} \sqrt{1 + (x')^{2}} \, dy$ |
| 3 | Parametric: $x = x(t), y = y(t)$ | $ds = \sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2}} \, dt$ | $L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2}} \, dt$ |
| 4 | Polar: $r = r(\theta)$ | $ds = \sqrt{r^{2} + (r')^{2}} \, d\theta$ | $L = \int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} \sqrt{r^{2} + (r')^{2}} \, d\theta$ |
| 5 | Space curve: $\vec{r}(t) = \langle x, y, z \rangle$ | $ds = \sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2} + (z')^{2}} \, dt$ | $L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \|\vec{r}\,'(t)\| \, dt$ |
| 6 | Speed along curve | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | $v = \sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2} + (z')^{2}}$ |
| 7 | Vector magnitude | $\vert \vec{v} \vert = \sqrt{v_{x}^{2} + v_{y}^{2} + v_{z}^{2}}$ | Euclidean norm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE for Tables:** In all KTU board exam answers, when you write absolute value or vector magnitude **inside** a sentence or table, write it as $\mid x \mid$ or $\lvert x \rvert$, never as the raw `|` character. Some markers use the pipe only for matrix determinants; for absolute value, $\mid \; \mid$ is the correct form.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Arc Length |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Civil Engineering** | Computing the length of road bends, railway tracks, arch bridges, cable-stayed structures. |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Designing cam profiles, gear teeth curves, and the path of robotic arms. |
| **Computer Graphics & CAD** | Rendering the length of Bezier curves and splines for vector graphics. |
| **GPS & Navigation** | Distance along a great-circle path on Earth (geodesic approximation). |
| **Optics & Physics** | Path length of light rays through varying media (Fermat's principle). |
| **Robotics** | Trajectory length of an end-effector — used to minimize time/energy. |

> [!NOTE]
> The arc-length integral is also the **starting point for line integrals** of scalar and vector fields, which KTU covers in the higher modules. The quantity $ds$ is the "weight" you assign to each infinitesimal piece of a curve when integrating a function *along* the curve rather than along the $x$-axis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Arc Length Formula (Cartesian Form)

We will now re-derive the arc-length formula from scratch, exposing every algebraic step and the geometric reasoning behind it.

**Setup.** Let $f$ be differentiable with continuous derivative on $[a, b]$. We want to find $L$, the length of the graph of $f$ from $(a, f(a))$ to $(b, f(b))$.

**Step 1 — Partition the interval.** Choose a partition $P$ of $[a, b]$:

$$a = x_{0} < x_{1} < x_{2} < \dots < x_{n} = b$$

Set $\Delta x_{i} = x_{i} - x_{i-1}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$. The norm of the partition is $\|P\| = \max_{i} \Delta x_{i}$.

**Step 2 — Identify the chord endpoints.** The points on the curve corresponding to the partition are:

$$P_{i} = (x_{i},\, f(x_{i})), \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step 3 — Compute chord length using the distance formula.** The Euclidean distance between $P_{i-1}$ and $P_{i}$ is:

$$\ell_{i} = \sqrt{(x_{i} - x_{i-1})^{2} + [f(x_{i}) - f(x_{i-1})]^{2}} = \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Form the polygonal approximation sum.** The total length of the inscribed polygonal path is:

$$L(P) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ell_{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Mean Value Theorem for Derivatives.** Since $f$ is differentiable on $[x_{i-1}, x_{i}]$ and continuous on $[x_{i-1}, x_{i}]$, by **MVT**, $\exists \, c_{i} \in (x_{i-1}, x_{i})$ such that:

$$f(x_{i}) - f(x_{i-1}) = f'(c_{i}) \, (x_{i} - x_{i-1}) = f'(c_{i}) \, \Delta x_{i}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the MVT result into the chord length expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_{i} &= \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + [f'(c_{i})]^{2} \, (\Delta x_{i})^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{[1 + (f'(c_{i}))^{2}] \, (\Delta x_{i})^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{1 + [f'(c_{i})]^{2}} \cdot \vert \Delta x_{i} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta x_{i} > 0$, $\vert \Delta x_{i} \vert = \Delta x_{i}$.

**Step 7 — Rewrite the polygonal sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(P) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{1 + [f'(c_{i})]^{2}} \, \Delta x_{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Recognize the Riemann sum.** This is a Riemann sum for the function $g(x) = \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^{2}}$ over $[a, b]$, with sample points $c_{i}$.

**Step 9 — Take the limit as the partition is refined.** If $f'$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, then $g$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, and therefore integrable. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \lim_{\|P\| \to 0} L(P) = \lim_{\|P\| \to 0} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{1 + [f'(c_{i})]^{2}} \, \Delta x_{i} \\
&= \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^{2}} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Q.E.D. — The arc length of $y = f(x)$ from $a$ to $b$ is the integral of $\sqrt{1 + (f'(x))^{2}}$ over $[a, b]$.**

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Model Example (Full Board-Exam Style)

**Problem.** Find the length of the arc of the curve $y = \frac{2}{3} x^{3/2}$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 3$.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the derivative.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{2}{3} x^{3/2} \\
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \, x^{1/2} = x^{1/2} = \sqrt{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Substitute into the arc-length formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{3} \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \, dx = \int_{0}^{3} \sqrt{1 + (\sqrt{x})^{2}} \, dx \\
&= \int_{0}^{3} \sqrt{1 + x} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Evaluate the integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{3} (1 + x)^{1/2} \, dx \\
&= \left[ \frac{(1 + x)^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{3} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \left[ (1 + x)^{3/2} \right]_{0}^{3} \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \left[ (1 + 3)^{3/2} - (1 + 0)^{3/2} \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \left[ 4^{3/2} - 1^{3/2} \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \left[ (\sqrt{4})^{3} - 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \left[ 2^{3} - 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{3} \left[ 8 - 1 \right] = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 7 = \frac{14}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $L = \dfrac{14}{3}$ units.

---

## 3.3 Second Worked Example — Parametric Curve

**Problem.** Find the length of one arch of the cycloid $x = a(t - \sin t)$, $y = a(1 - \cos t)$, $0 \leq t \leq 2\pi$.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute the derivatives with respect to $t$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx}{dt} &= a(1 - \cos t) \\
\frac{dy}{dt} &= a \sin t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Form the integrand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^{2} &= a^{2}(1 - \cos t)^{2} + a^{2} \sin^{2} t \\
&= a^{2} \left[ (1 - \cos t)^{2} + \sin^{2} t \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Simplify using the trigonometric identity $(1 - \cos t)^{2} + \sin^{2} t = 2(1 - \cos t)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - \cos t)^{2} + \sin^{2} t &= 1 - 2\cos t + \cos^{2} t + \sin^{2} t \\
&= 1 - 2\cos t + 1 \\
&= 2 - 2\cos t \\
&= 2(1 - \cos t) \\
&= 2 \cdot 2 \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \\
&= 4 \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Take the square root and integrate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2}} &= a \cdot 2 \left| \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \right| = 2a \left| \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \right|
\end{aligned}
$$

For $0 \leq t \leq 2\pi$, $\sin(t/2) \geq 0$, so the absolute value can be dropped:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} 2a \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \, dt = 2a \left[ -2 \cos\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right) \right]_{0}^{2\pi} \\
&= -4a \left[ \cos(\pi) - \cos(0) \right] = -4a \left[ -1 - 1 \right] = 8a
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $L = 8a$ units.

---

## 3.4 Third Worked Example — Polar Curve

**Problem.** Find the perimeter of the cardioid $r = a(1 + \cos \theta)$.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute $dr/d\theta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dr}{d\theta} &= -a \sin \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Form the integrand $r^{2} + (dr/d\theta)^{2}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^{2} + \left(\frac{dr}{d\theta}\right)^{2} &= a^{2}(1 + \cos \theta)^{2} + a^{2} \sin^{2} \theta \\
&= a^{2} \left[ (1 + \cos \theta)^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 + \cos \theta)^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta &= 1 + 2\cos \theta + \cos^{2} \theta + \sin^{2} \theta \\
&= 2 + 2\cos \theta \\
&= 2(1 + \cos \theta) \\
&= 4 \cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Integrate over the full $0$ to $2\pi$ range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} 2a \left| \cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \right| \, d\theta = 2a \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left| \cos\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \right| \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry of $\cos$ over a full period, this integral equals $2a \cdot 4 = 8a$.

> **Final Answer:** $L = 8a$ units.

---

## 3.5 The Arc Length Function and Reparametrization

A more advanced concept tied to the definition of length is the **arc length function**:

$$s(x) = \int_{a}^{x} \sqrt{1 + [f'(t)]^{2}} \, dt$$

This gives the *distance along the curve* from a fixed point $(a, f(a))$ to a variable point $(x, f(x))$. By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$\frac{ds}{dx} = \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Differential Form of Arc Length:** Solving for $ds$ gives the celebrated differential identity:
> 
> $$(ds)^{2} = (dx)^{2} + (dy)^{2}$$
> 
> This is the **Pythagorean theorem in differential form** — the infinitesimal version of the chord-length formula. It is the cornerstone of line integrals, surface integrals, and general relativity.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparative Mind Map of the Four Length Formulae

This mind map visually links all four standard representations of arc length to their underlying geometry.

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Arc Length L))
    CartesianForm
      Formula: integral of sqrt of 1 plus y prime squared dx
      UsedWhen: y is given explicitly as function of x
      KeyDifferential: ds squared equals dx squared plus dy squared
    ParametricForm
      Formula: integral of sqrt of x prime squared plus y prime squared dt
      UsedWhen: x and y are both functions of parameter t
      KeyDifferential: ds squared equals dx squared plus dy squared
    PolarForm
      Formula: integral of sqrt of r squared plus r prime squared d theta
      UsedWhen: r is given as function of theta
      KeyDifferential: ds squared equals dr squared plus r d theta squared
    SpaceCurveForm
      Formula: integral of sqrt of x prime squared plus y prime squared plus z prime squared dt
      UsedWhen: Curve moves through 3D space
      KeyDifferential: ds equals magnitude of r prime of t dt
```
