# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the arc length of a curve $y = f(x)$ from $x = a$ to $x = b$. State the necessary and sufficient condition for the curve to be rectifiable.**

**Model Answer:**

> The arc length $L$ of a plane curve defined by $y = f(x)$ on $[a, b]$ is defined as:
> 
> $$L = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \, dx$$
> 
> provided the integral converges. **Necessary and sufficient condition for rectifiability:** $f'(x)$ must be continuous on $[a, b]$, i.e., the curve must be **smooth** (differentiable with a continuous derivative) throughout the interval.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Write the formula for the length of a curve given in parametric form $x = x(t), y = y(t)$ for $\alpha \leq t \leq \beta$.**

**Model Answer:**

> The arc length of a parametric curve is:
> 
> $$L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sqrt{\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^{2}} \, dt$$
> 
> In vector notation, if $\vec{r}(t) = \langle x(t), y(t) \rangle$, then $L = \displaystyle\int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \lVert \vec{r}\,'(t) \rVert \, dt$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for the arc length of a curve $y = f(x)$ from $x = a$ to $x = b$ using the method of polygonal approximation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Hence, find the length of the curve $y^{2} = 4x^{3}$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 1$.

**Model Solution to (a):**

*Step 1 — Partition the interval $[a, b]$ into $n$ sub-intervals by points $a = x_{0} < x_{1} < \dots < x_{n} = b$, with $\Delta x_{i} = x_{i} - x_{i-1}$.*

*Step 2 — On each sub-interval, approximate the curve by the chord joining $(x_{i-1}, f(x_{i-1}))$ and $(x_{i}, f(x_{i}))$. The length of the $i$-th chord is:*

$$\ell_{i} = \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$$

*Step 3 — The polygonal approximation to the total length is:*

$$L_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ell_{i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + (\Delta y_{i})^{2}}$$

*Step 4 — By the Mean Value Theorem, there exists $c_{i} \in [x_{i-1}, x_{i}]$ such that $f(x_{i}) - f(x_{i-1}) = f'(c_{i}) \, \Delta x_{i}$. Therefore:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell_{i} &= \sqrt{(\Delta x_{i})^{2} + [f'(c_{i})]^{2} \, (\Delta x_{i})^{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{1 + [f'(c_{i})]^{2}} \, \Delta x_{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5 — Substituting back:*

$$L_{n} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{1 + [f'(c_{i})]^{2}} \, \Delta x_{i}$$

*Step 6 — This is a Riemann sum for $g(x) = \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^{2}}$. If $f'$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, then $g$ is integrable. Taking the limit as $\|P\| \to 0$:*

$$
\boxed{L = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}} \, dx}
$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

*Step 1 — From $y^{2} = 4x^{3}$, we get $y = 2x^{3/2}$ (taking the positive branch).*

*Step 2 — Compute the derivative:*

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = 2 \cdot \frac{3}{2} x^{1/2} = 3 \sqrt{x}
$$

*Step 3 — Substitute into the arc length formula:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{1 + (3\sqrt{x})^{2}} \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{1 + 9x} \, dx \\
&= \int_{0}^{1} (1 + 9x)^{1/2} \, dx \\
&= \left[ \frac{(1 + 9x)^{3/2}}{9 \cdot (3/2)} \right]_{0}^{1} \\
&= \frac{2}{27} \left[ (1 + 9)^{3/2} - (1 + 0)^{3/2} \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{27} \left[ 10^{3/2} - 1 \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{27} \left[ 10\sqrt{10} - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** Derived formula $L = \displaystyle\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx$.
> **Final Answer (b):** $L = \dfrac{2}{27} (10\sqrt{10} - 1)$ units.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the length of the curve $y = \ln(\sec x)$ from $x = 0$ to $x = \pi/3$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the length of the curve $x = a(\cos t + t \sin t)$, $y = a(\sin t - t \cos t)$ from $t = 0$ to $t = \pi/2$.

**Model Solution to (a):**

*Step 1 — Compute the derivative.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \ln(\sec x) \\
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{1}{\sec x} \cdot \sec x \tan x = \tan x
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Substitute into the arc length formula.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sqrt{1 + \tan^{2} x} \, dx = \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sqrt{\sec^{2} x} \, dx \\
&= \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sec x \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Evaluate using the standard antiderivative $\int \sec x \, dx = \ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert + C$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \Big[ \ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert \Big]_{0}^{\pi/3} \\
&= \ln \left( \sec(\pi/3) + \tan(\pi/3) \right) - \ln \left( \sec(0) + \tan(0) \right) \\
&= \ln(2 + \sqrt{3}) - \ln(1 + 0) \\
&= \ln(2 + \sqrt{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $L = \ln(2 + \sqrt{3})$ units.

**Model Solution to (b):**

*Step 1 — Compute derivatives of $x$ and $y$ with respect to $t$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx}{dt} &= a(-\sin t + \sin t + t \cos t) = a t \cos t \\
\frac{dy}{dt} &= a(\cos t - \cos t + t \sin t) = a t \sin t
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Form the integrand.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right)^{2} &= a^{2} t^{2} \cos^{2} t + a^{2} t^{2} \sin^{2} t \\
&= a^{2} t^{2} (\cos^{2} t + \sin^{2} t) = a^{2} t^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Take the square root and integrate.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \sqrt{a^{2} t^{2}} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} a t \, dt \\
&= a \left[ \frac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\pi/2} = a \cdot \frac{(\pi/2)^{2}}{2} = \frac{a \pi^{2}}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer (b):** $L = \dfrac{a \pi^{2}}{8}$ units.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $1+$ inside the square root.** Students frequently write $\int \sqrt{(y')^{2}} \, dx = \int y' \, dx$, which gives the *net vertical change*, not the arc length. Always include the $1+$.
> 
> 2. **Dropping the absolute value in polar/parametric forms.** When $r$ or $f'(t)$ can be negative, $\sqrt{r^{2}}$ must be written as $\lvert r \rvert$, not $r$. The arc length is *always* non-negative.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up the variable of integration.** If the curve is $y = f(x)$, the variable of integration is $x$ and the integrand is in terms of $x$. If the curve is parametric in $t$, the variable of integration is $t$.
> 
> 4. **Wrong simplification of $1 + \tan^{2} x$ or $1 + \cot^{2} \theta$.** Remember $1 + \tan^{2} x = \sec^{2} x$ and $1 + \cot^{2} \theta = \csc^{2} \theta$. These trigonometric Pythagorean identities are used *very* often in arc length problems.
> 
> 5. **Not showing the MVT step in derivations.** For a 7-mark derivation, you must explicitly show $\Delta y_{i} = f'(c_{i}) \Delta x_{i}$ via the Mean Value Theorem, then the algebraic simplification leading to the Riemann sum, and finally the limit. Skipping any one of these steps costs 2–3 marks.
> 
> 6. **Unit omission.** Always state the unit (or "units") in the final answer. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 mark for a missing unit in the final boxed answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - **Core formula (Cartesian):** $L = \int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx$
> - **Core formula (Parametric):** $L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2}} \, dt$
> - **Core formula (Polar):** $L = \int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} \sqrt{r^{2} + (r')^{2}} \, d\theta$
> - **Core formula (Space Curve):** $L = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sqrt{(x')^{2} + (y')^{2} + (z')^{2}} \, dt$
> - **Differential form:** $ds = \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx$, or equivalently $ds^{2} = dx^{2} + dy^{2}$
> - **Vector form:** $L = \int \lVert \vec{r}\,'(t) \rVert \, dt$
> - **Rectifiability requires $f'$ to be continuous** on the closed interval.
> - **Always add $1+$ inside the radical** — never forget it.
> - **Standard trigonometric identities used:** $1 + \tan^{2} \theta = \sec^{2} \theta$, $1 + \cot^{2} \theta = \csc^{2} \theta$, $1 - \cos \theta = 2\sin^{2}(\theta/2)$, $1 + \cos \theta = 2\cos^{2}(\theta/2)$.
> - **Symmetry shortcut:** If the curve is symmetric about an axis, compute the length over a single half and double it.
> - **Speed relation:** $v = ds/dt$ — speed is the rate of change of arc length.
> - **Arc length function:** $s(x) = \int_{a}^{x} \sqrt{1 + [f'(t)]^{2}} \, dt$ — used in reparametrization by arc length.
> - **Unit:** The final answer must always carry a unit (**units** for abstract problems, **m** for physical problems).
> - **Pitfall to avoid:** $\int_{a}^{b} \sqrt{1 + (y')^{2}} \, dx \neq \int_{a}^{b} \lvert y' \rvert \, dx$. The former is the *arc length*, the latter is the *total vertical variation* — they are equal only when the function is monotonic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance and Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distance and Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the context of three-dimensional analytical geometry, **Distance and Angle** constitute the two fundamental metrics used to characterise the relative spatial orientation of geometric entities — points, lines, and planes — in $\mathbb{R}^3$. For vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$, the **distance** measures the shortest length separating two geometric objects, while the **angle** quantifies their mutual orientation (i.e., how much one is rotated away from the other).

More formally:
- The **angle** $\theta$ between two non-zero vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is defined by the dot-product relation:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\vert \vec{a} \vert \, \vert \vec{b} \vert}, \quad 0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$$
- The **distance** between two points $P(x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $Q(x_2, y_2, z_2)$ is given by:
$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of GAMAT201 mandates mastery of (i) distance between two points, (ii) distance from a point to a line, (iii) distance between two lines (including skew lines), (iv) angle between two lines, (v) angle between a line and a plane, and (vi) angle between two planes. These are tested in the form of 7-mark and 14-mark analytical problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing in a large empty room:
- **Distance** is like the *length of a string* stretched tightly between two objects in the room — it tells you "how far apart" they are, taking the shortest possible path.
- **Angle** is like the *opening of a door* — it tells you "how tilted" one object is relative to another. An angle of $0$ means they are parallel (door fully closed and aligned with the wall); an angle of $\pi/2$ means they are perpendicular (door fully open at right angles to the wall); an angle of $\pi$ means they point in exactly opposite directions (door folded back onto itself).

In 3D space, however, things become more interesting: two lines can be **skew** (not parallel, yet never meeting) — think of two wires in a tangle of headphone cables. The *shortest distance* between such skew lines is not along either line but along a unique line perpendicular to both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The dot product gives you **angle information** (orientation), while the cross product gives you **distance information** (perpendicularity). Together, they are the two pillars of 3D analytical geometry.

### 1.3 Fundamental Constants and Metrics

The two structural constants of this topic are:
- The **dot product operator** $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + a_3 b_3$ — used to extract cosine-based angular measure.
- The **cross product operator** $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}$ — whose magnitude equals $\vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert \sin\theta$ and whose direction is perpendicular to both vectors — used to extract perpendicular distance.

The standard **angular range** is $\mathbf{0 \leq \theta \leq \pi}$, and the standard **distance range** is $\mathbf{d \geq 0}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: Direction Cosines and Direction Ratios

Any line in 3D space can be characterised by a unit direction vector $(\ell, m, n)$, where $\ell, m, n$ are the **direction cosines (DCs)** — the cosines of the angles the line makes with the positive $x$-, $y$-, and $z$-axes respectively.

#### The "Why" Behind the Concept
- **Why direction cosines?** Because any vector in $\mathbb{R}^3$ can be decomposed along three orthogonal axes; the cosines give the normalised "weights" along each axis.
- **Why do they satisfy a unit constraint?** Because a unit vector has magnitude **1**, so by Pythagoras: $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$.

#### Direction Ratios (DRs)
Any set of three numbers $(a, b, c)$ proportional to the direction cosines is called the **direction ratios** of the line:
$$\ell = \frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}}, \quad m = \frac{b}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}}, \quad n = \frac{c}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}}$$

### 2.2 The Logic of Distances

- **Point to Point:** Direct application of the 3D Euclidean norm.
- **Point to Line:** Drop a perpendicular from the point to the line; the foot of the perpendicular is found by minimising the distance (or by vector projection).
- **Parallel Lines:** The perpendicular distance between them is constant everywhere.
- **Skew Lines:** Use the **scalar triple product** of the line of shortest distance and the direction vectors.

### 2.3 The Logic of Angles

- **Between Two Lines:** $\cos\theta = \vert \vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2} \vert / (\vert \vec{d_1} \vert \vert \vec{d_2} \vert)$ (modulus gives acute angle).
- **Line and Plane:** A line is perpendicular to the plane's normal. So the line makes angle $\theta$ with the plane iff the line makes angle $(\pi/2 - \theta)$ with the normal. Therefore $\sin\theta = \vert \vec{d} \cdot \vec{n} \vert / (\vert \vec{d} \vert \vert \vec{n} \vert)$.
- **Between Two Planes:** The angle between the planes equals the angle between their normals (or its supplement).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Direction cosines identity | $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ | Always true for unit vector |
| 2 | Angle between two lines (vectors $\vec{a}, \vec{b}$) | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\vert \vec{a} \vert \vert \vec{b} \vert}$ | $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$ |
| 3 | Angle between two lines (DRs $a_1, b_1, c_1$ and $a_2, b_2, c_2$) | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{a_1 a_2 + b_1 b_2 + c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2 + c_1^2}\sqrt{a_2^2 + b_2^2 + c_2^2}}$ | Acute angle if modulus taken |
| 4 | Angle between line (DRs $a, b, c$) and plane (normal $A, B, C$) | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{\vert aA + bB + cC \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}}$ | $\theta$ is angle with plane |
| 5 | Angle between two planes (normals $\vec{n_1}, \vec{n_2}$) | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{n_1} \cdot \vec{n_2}}{\vert \vec{n_1} \vert \vert \vec{n_2} \vert}$ | Acute angle between planes |
| 6 | Distance between two points $P_1, P_2$ | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$ | Direct 3D distance |
| 7 | Distance from point $P$ to line $\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda \vec{d}$ | $d = \dfrac{\vert \overrightarrow{AP} \times \vec{d} \vert}{\vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | $A$ is a point on the line |
| 8 | Distance between two parallel lines | $d = \dfrac{\vert \overrightarrow{P_1 P_2} \times \vec{d} \vert}{\vert \vec{d} \vert}$ | Same direction $\vec{d}$ |
| 9 | Shortest distance between two skew lines | $d = \dfrac{\vert (\vec{b_2} - \vec{b_1}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}) \vert}{\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert}$ | Skew lines only |
| 10 | Distance from point to plane $Ax + By + Cz + D = 0$ | $d = \dfrac{\vert Ax_0 + By_0 + Cz_0 + D \vert}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}}$ | Direct formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the computational backbone of **computer graphics** (ray-triangle intersection, camera projections), **robotics** (manipulator kinematics, collision avoidance), **CAD/CAM** (tool path optimisation), and **machine learning** (vector embeddings, cosine similarity in NLP). Mastery of "Distance and Angle" is therefore not merely mathematical — it is foundational to all spatial computing disciplines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Angle Between Two Lines (Direction Ratio Form)

**Statement:** If two lines have direction ratios $(a_1, b_1, c_1)$ and $(a_2, b_2, c_2)$, derive the formula for the acute angle $\theta$ between them.

**Step 1 — Express direction vectors:** Let the lines be along vectors $\vec{d_1} = a_1 \hat{i} + b_1 \hat{j} + c_1 \hat{k}$ and $\vec{d_2} = a_2 \hat{i} + b_2 \hat{j} + c_2 \hat{k}$.

**Step 2 — Apply dot product definition:** By the geometric definition of dot product:
$$\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2} = \vert \vec{d_1} \vert \vert \vec{d_2} \vert \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Compute left side (component form):**
$$\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2} = a_1 a_2 + b_1 b_2 + c_1 c_2$$

**Step 4 — Compute magnitudes (Pythagoras in 3D):**
$$\vert \vec{d_1} \vert = \sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2 + c_1^2}, \quad \vert \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{a_2^2 + b_2^2 + c_2^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\cos\theta$:** Substituting back:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{a_1 a_2 + b_1 b_2 + c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2 + b_1^2 + c_1^2} \, \sqrt{a_2^2 + b_2^2 + c_2^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To always obtain the **acute** angle (KTU convention), take the **modulus** of the numerator.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Angle Between a Line and a Plane

**Statement:** If a line has direction ratios $(a, b, c)$ and a plane has normal with DRs $(A, B, C)$, derive the formula for the angle $\theta$ between the line and the plane.

**Step 1 — Key geometric fact:** The angle $\phi$ between the line and the **normal** of the plane is complementary to the angle $\theta$ between the line and the **plane**:
$$\phi + \theta = \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta$$

**Step 2 — Apply angle-between-two-lines formula** to the line's direction vector $\vec{d} = (a, b, c)$ and the plane's normal $\vec{n} = (A, B, C)$:
$$\cos\phi = \frac{aA + bB + cC}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert from $\phi$ to $\theta$:** Using $\cos\phi = \cos(\pi/2 - \theta) = \sin\theta$:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{\vert aA + bB + cC \vert}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **why the formula uses $\sin$** for line–plane angle and $\cos$ for line–line angle — they are essentially the same formula applied to different reference vectors.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Shortest Distance Between Two Skew Lines

**Statement:** Derive the formula for the shortest distance between two skew lines $\vec{r_1} = \vec{a_1} + \lambda \vec{d_1}$ and $\vec{r_2} = \vec{a_2} + \mu \vec{d_2}$.

**Step 1 — Geometric setup:** The shortest distance line is **perpendicular to both** $\vec{d_1}$ and $\vec{d_2}$. Therefore, this common perpendicular lies along the direction $\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}$.

**Step 2 — Projection of vector between points onto common perpendicular:** The shortest distance equals the **absolute scalar triple product** of the connecting vector $(\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1})$ with the two direction vectors, divided by the magnitude of the cross product:
$$d = \frac{\vert (\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}) \vert}{\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Why this works (intuitive justification):**
- $\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}$ is perpendicular to both lines, so its unit vector $\hat{n} = \dfrac{\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}}{\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert}$ points along the common perpendicular.
- The projection of any vector along this common perpendicular is the dot product with $\hat{n}$:
$$d = \vert (\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot \hat{n} \vert$$
- Substituting $\hat{n}$:
$$d = \left\vert (\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot \frac{\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}}{\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert} \right\vert = \frac{\vert (\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}) \vert}{\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert}$$

**Step 4 — Scalar triple product form:** The numerator $(\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2})$ is recognised as the **scalar triple product** $[\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}, \vec{d_1}, \vec{d_2}]$, which equals the volume of the parallelepiped formed by these three vectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The shortest distance between skew lines equals the **volume of the parallelepiped** formed by $(\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1})$, $\vec{d_1}$, $\vec{d_2}$, divided by the **area of the base** $\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert$. This is the 3D analogue of "height = volume ÷ base area".

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style, 14-Mark)

**Problem:** Find the shortest distance between the lines
$$L_1: \frac{x-1}{2} = \frac{y-2}{3} = \frac{z-3}{4} \quad \text{and} \quad L_2: \frac{x+2}{1} = \frac{y-1}{-2} = \frac{z}{-1}$$
Also find the equation of the line of shortest distance.

**Step 1 — Extract data from symmetric form:**
- $L_1$ passes through $A_1(1, 2, 3)$ with direction $\vec{d_1} = (2, 3, 4)$.
- $L_2$ passes through $A_2(-2, 1, 0)$ with direction $\vec{d_2} = (1, -2, -1)$.

**Step 2 — Connecting vector:**
$$\vec{A_1 A_2} = (-2 - 1, \, 1 - 2, \, 0 - 3) = (-3, -1, -3)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}$:**
$$\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & -2 & -1 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:
$$= \hat{i}[(3)(-1) - (4)(-2)] - \hat{j}[(2)(-1) - (4)(1)] + \hat{k}[(2)(-2) - (3)(1)]$$

$$= \hat{i}[-3 + 8] - \hat{j}[-2 - 4] + \hat{k}[-4 - 3]$$

$$= 5\hat{i} + 6\hat{j} - 7\hat{k}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of $\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}$:**
$$\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{5^2 + 6^2 + (-7)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 36 + 49} = \sqrt{110}$$

**Step 5 — Scalar triple product:**
$$(\vec{A_1 A_2}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}) = (-3)(5) + (-1)(6) + (-3)(-7) = -15 - 6 + 21 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Since the scalar triple product is **0**, the three vectors are **coplanar** — the lines are not skew; they **intersect** (or are parallel). The shortest distance is:
$$d = \frac{\vert 0 \vert}{\sqrt{110}} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Verification by direct substitution:** From $L_1$: $x = 1 + 2\lambda$, $y = 2 + 3\lambda$, $z = 3 + 4\lambda$.
From $L_2$: $x = -2 + \mu$, $y = 1 - 2\mu$, $z = -\mu$.

Setting equal: $1 + 2\lambda = -2 + \mu \Rightarrow \mu = 3 + 2\lambda$ ... (i)
$2 + 3\lambda = 1 - 2\mu = 1 - 2(3 + 2\lambda) = -5 - 4\lambda \Rightarrow 7\lambda = -7 \Rightarrow \lambda = -1$ ... (ii)
Check: $\mu = 3 + 2(-1) = 1$. From $z$: $3 + 4(-1) = -1$ and $-1 = -1$ ✓.

**Intersection point:** $(1 + 2(-1), 2 + 3(-1), 3 + 4(-1)) = (-1, -1, -1)$.

**Conclusion:** The lines **intersect** at the point $(-1, -1, -1)$, and the shortest distance is **0**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map of Distance and Angle Computations

The following block-level functional architecture summarises the entire Module 3 decision tree for computing distances and angles in 3D space.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GIVEN Two Geometric Objects] --> B{Type of Object Pair}
    B -->|Two Points| C1[Point-to-Point Distance]
    B -->|Point and Line| C2[Point-to-Line Distance]
    B -->|Point and Plane| C3[Point-to-Plane Distance]
    B -->|Two Lines| D{Twist / Orientation}
    D -->|Parallel| E1[Parallel Line Distance]
    D -->|Intersecting| E2[Intersecting: distance = 0]
    D -->|Skew| E3[Skew Line Shortest Distance]
    B -->|Two Planes| F1[Plane-to-Plane Angle / Distance]
    B -->|Line and Plane| F2[Line-to-Plane Angle]

    C1 --> G1[Use 3D Euclidean norm]
    C2 --> G2[Use cross product formula]
    C3 --> G3[Use plane equation substitution]
    E1 --> H1[Use cross product on connecting vector]
    E3 --> H2[Use scalar triple product]
    E2 --> H3[Trivial: zero distance]
    F1 --> H4[Angle between normals; distance parallel to normals]
    F2 --> H5[sin theta via dot product with normal]
    F2 --> F2A[cos theta via dot product of directions]

    G1 --> RESULT[FINAL DISTANCE OR ANGLE]
    G2 --> RESULT
    G3 --> RESULT
    H1 --> RESULT
    H2 --> RESULT
    H3 --> RESULT
    H4 --> RESULT
    H5 --> RESULT
    F2A --> RESULT
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: The Role of Dot and Cross Products

This diagram clarifies the structural distinction between the two fundamental vector operators used throughout Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Two Vectors vecA and vecB] --> B{Which quantity do we need?}

    B -->|Angle Theta between them| C1[Use DOT PRODUCT]
    C1 --> C1A[cos theta = vecA dot vecB divided by magnitudes]
    C1A --> C1B[Output: orientation info]

    B -->|Perpendicular component| D1[Use CROSS PRODUCT]
    D1 --> D1A[magnitude equals vecA times vecB times sin theta]
    D1 --> D1B[direction perpendicular to plane of vecA vecB]
    D1 --> D1C[Output: distance / area / normal vector]

    C1B --> E[Combined yields full 3D spatial analysis]
    D1C --> E
```

### 4.3 Geometric Schematic: Skew Lines and the Common Perpendicular

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[Skew Line 1]
        A1[Point A1] --- L1[Direction vector d1]
    end
    subgraph P2[Skew Line 2]
        A2[Point A2] --- L2[Direction vector d2]
    end
    P1 -.->|Connecting vector A1A2| P2
    P1 -.->|d1 cross d2 = common perpendicular direction| P2
    P2 -->|Projection yields shortest distance| OUT[SHORTEST DISTANCE d]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The connecting vector $\overrightarrow{A_1 A_2}$ is decomposed into a component along the common perpendicular and a component parallel to the planes of the two lines. The length of the perpendicular component is the shortest distance. The scalar triple product captures exactly this projection.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define direction cosines of a line. Show that the sum of squares of the direction cosines of any line is unity.

**Model Answer:**
Direction cosines of a line are the cosines of the angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ that the line makes with the positive $x$-, $y$-, and $z$-axes, respectively. They are denoted by $\ell = \cos\alpha$, $m = \cos\beta$, $n = \cos\gamma$.

**Proof:** Let $P(x, y, z)$ be any point on the line and $OP$ be the line. If $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$, then the projections of $OP$ on the axes are:
$$x = r\cos\alpha = r\ell, \quad y = r\cos\beta = rm, \quad z = r\cos\gamma = rn$$

Since $(x/r)^2 + (y/r)^2 + (z/r)^2 = \cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma$, and $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = r^2$, we get $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$. ∎

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** State the formula for the angle between two planes. If two planes are parallel, what is the angle between them?

**Model Answer:**
The angle $\theta$ between two planes with normals $\vec{n_1} = (A_1, B_1, C_1)$ and $\vec{n_2} = (A_2, B_2, C_2)$ is given by:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{A_1 A_2 + B_1 B_2 + C_1 C_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 + B_1^2 + C_1^2}\sqrt{A_2^2 + B_2^2 + C_2^2}}$$

If the planes are **parallel**, their normals are parallel, so $\vec{n_1} \times \vec{n_2} = \vec{0}$ and $\cos\theta = \pm 1$, giving $\theta = 0$.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Analytical Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the shortest distance between the lines
$$L_1: \vec{r} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \lambda(2\hat{i} - \hat{j} + \hat{k})$$
$$L_2: \vec{r} = 2\hat{i} + \hat{j} - \hat{k} + \mu(3\hat{i} - 5\hat{j} + 2\hat{k})$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A line makes angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ with the coordinate axes such that $\sin^2\alpha + \sin^2\beta + \sin^2\gamma = 2$. Prove that the line lies in a plane, and find the equation of the plane.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $L_1$: $\vec{a_1} = (1, 1, 0)$, $\vec{d_1} = (2, -1, 1)$
- $L_2$: $\vec{a_2} = (2, 1, -1)$, $\vec{d_2} = (3, -5, 2)$

**Step 2 — Connecting vector:**
$$\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1} = (1, 0, -1)$$

**Step 3 — Cross product $\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}$:**
$$\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \\ 3 & -5 & 2 \end{vmatrix}$$
$$= \hat{i}[(-1)(2) - (1)(-5)] - \hat{j}[(2)(2) - (1)(3)] + \hat{k}[(2)(-5) - (-1)(3)]$$
$$= \hat{i}[-2 + 5] - \hat{j}[4 - 3] + \hat{k}[-10 + 3] = 3\hat{i} - \hat{j} - 7\hat{k}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude:**
$$\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{9 + 1 + 49} = \sqrt{59}$$

**Step 5 — Scalar triple product:**
$$(\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}) = (1)(3) + (0)(-1) + (-1)(-7) = 3 + 0 + 7 = 10$$

**Step 6 — Shortest distance:**
$$d = \frac{\vert 10 \vert}{\sqrt{59}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{59}} = \frac{10\sqrt{59}}{59} \text{ units}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Use the identity** $\sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta$:
$$\sin^2\alpha + \sin^2\beta + \sin^2\gamma = 3 - (\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma) = 2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the direction cosine identity** $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ where $\ell = \cos\alpha$, etc.:
$$\cos^2\alpha + \cos^2\beta + \cos^2\gamma = 1 \Rightarrow 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ (consistent)}$$

**Step 3 — Geometric insight:** Since $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ is identically true for any line, the given condition is automatically satisfied. **Wait** — let us re-examine: the problem states the line *makes angles* with axes; we need to show the line is *perpendicular to a fixed direction*, i.e., lies in a plane perpendicular to that direction.

**Step 4 — Reformulate:** If $\sin^2\alpha + \sin^2\beta + \sin^2\gamma = 2$, and we define $A = \sin\alpha, B = \sin\beta, C = \sin\gamma$ as direction ratios of a vector perpendicular to the line, then $A^2 + B^2 + C^2 = 2$, so this vector has constant direction.

**Step 5 — Equation of the plane:** The plane perpendicular to vector $(A, B, C) = (\sin\alpha, \sin\beta, \sin\gamma)$ passing through the origin is:
$$x\sin\alpha + y\sin\beta + z\sin\gamma = 0$$

But the actual problem intends: the line's direction $(\cos\alpha, \cos\beta, \cos\gamma)$ is perpendicular to $(\sin\alpha, \sin\beta, \sin\gamma)$ since $\cos\alpha \sin\alpha + \cos\beta \sin\beta + \cos\gamma \sin\gamma = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\beta + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\gamma$, which under the constraint equals **0** (provable by symmetric identities). Therefore the line lies in the plane:
$$x\cos\alpha + y\cos\beta + z\cos\gamma = 0 \text{ (passing through origin)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the angle between the line $\vec{r} = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + \hat{k}) + \lambda(\hat{i} - \hat{j} + 2\hat{k})$ and the plane $3x - 4y + 5z = 7$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Find the distance of the point $(2, 3, 4)$ from the plane $3x + 4y - 12z + 13 = 0$ measured parallel to the line $\dfrac{x+1}{2} = \dfrac{y-2}{1} = \dfrac{z}{-1}$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Direction of line:** $\vec{d} = (1, -1, 2)$. **Normal to plane:** $\vec{n} = (3, -4, 5)$.

**Step 2 — Angle between line and normal:**
$$\cos\phi = \frac{\vec{d} \cdot \vec{n}}{\vert \vec{d} \vert \vert \vec{n} \vert} = \frac{(1)(3) + (-1)(-4) + (2)(5)}{\sqrt{1+1+4}\sqrt{9+16+25}}$$
$$= \frac{3 + 4 + 10}{\sqrt{6}\sqrt{50}} = \frac{17}{\sqrt{300}} = \frac{17}{10\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Angle with plane:** $\theta = \pi/2 - \phi$, so $\sin\theta = \cos\phi$:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{17}{10\sqrt{3}} = \frac{17\sqrt{3}}{30}$$

Therefore:
$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{17\sqrt{3}}{30}\right) \approx 79.1°$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Equation of line through $P(2, 3, 4)$ parallel to $\dfrac{x+1}{2} = \dfrac{y-2}{1} = \dfrac{z}{-1}$:**
Direction: $\vec{d} = (2, 1, -1)$.
Parametric form: $x = 2 + 2t$, $y = 3 + t$, $z = 4 - t$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into plane equation** $3x + 4y - 12z + 13 = 0$:
$$3(2 + 2t) + 4(3 + t) - 12(4 - t) + 13 = 0$$
$$6 + 6t + 12 + 4t - 48 + 12t + 13 = 0$$
$$(22t) + (-17) = 0 \Rightarrow t = \frac{17}{22}$$

**Step 3 — Find foot of perpendicular $Q$ from $P$ to plane (along line direction):**
$$Q = \left(2 + 2 \cdot \frac{17}{22}, \, 3 + \frac{17}{22}, \, 4 - \frac{17}{22}\right) = \left(\frac{78}{22}, \frac{83}{22}, \frac{71}{22}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute distance $PQ$:**
$$PQ = \sqrt{(2t)^2 + (t)^2 + (-t)^2} = \sqrt{4t^2 + t^2 + t^2} = \sqrt{6t^2} = \vert t \vert \sqrt{6}$$
$$= \frac{17}{22}\sqrt{6} = \frac{17\sqrt{6}}{22} \text{ units}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Dot Product:** Students frequently drop the modulus in angle formulas and report obtuse angles instead of acute ones. KTU convention: **always report the acute angle**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Verify Skewness:** Before applying the skew-line distance formula, you **must** confirm the lines are not parallel ($\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \neq \vec{0}$) and not intersecting (scalar triple product $\neq 0$). Always check both.
> 3. **Confusing Line-Plane Angle with Line-Normal Angle:** The line-plane angle uses $\sin\theta$, NOT $\cos\theta$. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 4. **Direction Cosines vs. Direction Ratios:** Direction cosines must satisfy $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$. Direction ratios have no such constraint. Many students treat them as interchangeable.
> 5. **Scalar Triple Product Sign:** Forgetting the absolute value yields negative distances. Always enclose the scalar triple product in $\vert \cdot \vert$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Direction Cosines:** $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ — three numbers, one constraint.
- **Direction Ratios:** Proportional to DCs — three numbers, zero constraints.
- **Line-Line Angle:** $\cos\theta = \dfrac{a_1 a_2 + b_1 b_2 + c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{\sum a_i^2}\sqrt{\sum b_i^2}}$ — take modulus for acute.
- **Line-Plane Angle:** $\sin\theta = \dfrac{\vert aA + bB + cC \vert}{\sqrt{\sum a^2}\sqrt{\sum A^2}}$ — note the $\sin$ (not $\cos$).
- **Plane-Plane Angle:** Angle between their normal vectors.
- **Point-Line Distance:** $\dfrac{\vert \overrightarrow{AP} \times \vec{d} \vert}{\vert \vec{d} \vert}$ — cross product captures perpendicularity.
- **Point-Plane Distance:** $\dfrac{\vert Ax_0 + By_0 + Cz_0 + D \vert}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2}}$ — direct formula.
- **Parallel Lines Distance:** $\dfrac{\vert \overrightarrow{P_1 P_2} \times \vec{d} \vert}{\vert \vec{d} \vert}$ — same as point-line.
- **Skew Lines Shortest Distance:** $\dfrac{\vert (\vec{a_2} - \vec{a_1}) \cdot (\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}) \vert}{\vert \vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2} \vert}$ — scalar triple product over magnitude.
- **Always verify** whether lines are **intersecting, parallel, or skew** before selecting a formula.
- **Common Perpendicular Direction:** $\vec{d_1} \times \vec{d_2}$ is perpendicular to *both* lines simultaneously.
- **Volume Interpretation:** Skew distance = (Volume of parallelepiped) ÷ (Area of base parallelogram).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orthogonal Projections in Inner Product Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Orthogonal Projections in Inner Product Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inner Product Space — Quick Recap

An **inner product space** is a real or complex vector space $V$ equipped with a function $\langle \cdot , \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{F}$ (where $\mathbb{F} = \mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$) satisfying, for all $u, v, w \in V$ and $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{F}$:

1. **Linearity in the first argument:** $\langle \alpha u + \beta v, w \rangle = \alpha \langle u, w \rangle + \beta \langle v, w \rangle$
2. **Conjugate Symmetry:** $\langle u, v \rangle = \overline{\langle v, u \rangle}$
3. **Positive Definiteness:** $\langle v, v \rangle \geq 0$, and $\langle v, v \rangle = 0 \iff v = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **induced norm** is $\|v\| = \sqrt{\langle v, v \rangle}$, and the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** holds: $|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \|u\| \cdot \|v\|$.

## 1.2 Orthogonal Complement

For any subset $S \subseteq V$, the **orthogonal complement** of $S$ is the set

$$
S^{\perp} = \{ v \in V : \langle v, s \rangle = 0 \text{ for every } s \in S \}
$$

In particular, for a subspace $W \subseteq V$, the pair $(W, W^{\perp})$ satisfies $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$ and $W \oplus W^{\perp} = V$ (when $V$ is finite-dimensional).

> [!NOTE]
> $W^{\perp}$ is always a **subspace** of $V$, even when $W$ is just a subset.

## 1.3 Orthogonal Projection — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Orthogonal Projection).** Let $W$ be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space $V$. The **orthogonal projection** of $V$ onto $W$ is the unique linear map $P_W : V \to W$ such that for every $v \in V$,
> $$v = P_W(v) + (v - P_W(v)), \quad \text{where } P_W(v) \in W \text{ and } v - P_W(v) \in W^{\perp}.$$
> Equivalently, $P_W$ is the unique **idempotent self-adjoint** linear operator with range $W$, i.e., $P_W^{2} = P_W$ and $P_W^{*} = P_W$.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Shadow on a Wall

Imagine you hold a flashlight and aim it **straight down (perpendicular)** at a flat floor. Hold any object (your hand, a book, a pen) above the floor. The **shadow** that appears on the floor is the *orthogonal projection* of the object onto the plane of the floor.

- The **floor** is the subspace $W$.
- The **object** is the vector $v$.
- The **shadow** is the projected vector $P_W(v)$.
- The **height** (perpendicular distance from object to floor) is the component $v - P_W(v)$ lying in $W^{\perp}$.
- The light travels *orthogonally* to the floor — hence "orthogonal projection."

> [!NOTE]
> The shadow is the **closest point on the floor to the object**, because the connecting line (the light ray) is perpendicular (shortest path) to the floor. This geometric "best approximation" property is the heart of the Projection Theorem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Projection Theorem (Fundamental Theorem of Inner Product Spaces)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Projection Theorem).** Let $W$ be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space $V$. Then **every** $v \in V$ admits a **unique** decomposition
> $$v = u + z, \quad u \in W, \quad z \in W^{\perp}.$$
> The vector $u$ is called the **orthogonal projection** of $v$ onto $W$, denoted $u = P_W(v)$.

**Why is this powerful?**
- It guarantees that $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$ (orthogonal direct sum).
- It guarantees a **unique best approximation** in $W$: $\|v - u\| \leq \|v - w\|$ for all $w \in W$.

## 2.2 Projection Formula — Onto a Line (1-D Subspace)

Let $u \in V$, $u \neq 0$. The orthogonal projection of $v$ onto $\text{span}\{u\}$ is:

$$
P_{\text{span}\{u\}}(v) \;=\; \frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} \, u
$$

The **scalar** $\dfrac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle}$ is the projection coefficient; the **vector** is the parallel component of $v$ along $u$.

## 2.3 Projection Formula — Onto a Subspace with Orthonormal Basis

If $\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_k\}$ is an **orthonormal basis** of $W$, then for every $v \in V$:

$$
P_W(v) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, e_i \rangle \, e_i
$$

## 2.4 Projection Formula — Onto a Subspace with Arbitrary Basis

If $\{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_k\}$ is a basis of $W$ (not necessarily orthonormal), then $P_W(v) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} c_j u_j$ where the coefficients $c_j$ are obtained by solving the linear system

$$
G \, \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{b}, \quad G_{ij} = \langle u_i, u_j \rangle, \quad b_i = \langle v, u_i \rangle
$$

The matrix $G$ is the **Gram matrix**, which is symmetric positive definite.

## 2.5 Gram–Schmidt Orthogonalization Process

Given a linearly independent set $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ in $V$, we construct an **orthogonal** set $\{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n\}$ via:

$$
u_1 = v_1, \qquad u_k = v_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} \, u_i, \quad k = 2, 3, \ldots, n
$$

Normalizing yields an **orthonormal** set $e_k = \dfrac{u_k}{\|u_k\|}$.

## 2.6 Properties of the Orthogonal Projection Operator $P_W$

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Idempotent** | $P_W^{2} = P_W$ |
| 2 | **Self-adjoint** | $P_W^{*} = P_W$ |
| 3 | **Range** | $\text{Range}(P_W) = W$ |
| 4 | **Kernel** | $\ker(P_W) = W^{\perp}$ |
| 5 | **Norm contraction** | $\vert P_W(v) \vert \leq \vert v \vert$ |
| 6 | **Orthogonality of error** | $\langle P_W(v), \, v - P_W(v) \rangle = 0$ |
| 7 | **Variational characterization** | $\vert v - P_W(v) \vert = \min_{w \in W} \vert v - w \vert$ |
| 8 | **Complement relation** | $P_W + P_{W^{\perp}} = I$ (identity) |
| 9 | **Orthogonality of distinct projections** | $P_W P_{W^{\perp}} = P_{W^{\perp}} P_W = 0$ |
| 10 | **Eigenvalues** | Eigenvalues are $1$ (with multiplicity $\dim W$) and $0$ (with multiplicity $\dim W^{\perp}$) |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement |
|---------|---------------------|
| Projection onto line $\text{span}\{u\}$ | $\displaystyle P(v) = \frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} u$ |
| Projection onto subspace (orthonormal basis) | $\displaystyle P_W(v) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, e_i \rangle e_i$ |
| Decomposition | $v = P_W(v) + (v - P_W(v))$ |
| Component in $W^{\perp}$ | $P_{W^{\perp}}(v) = v - P_W(v)$ |
| Gram matrix | $G_{ij} = \langle u_i, u_j \rangle$ |
| Gram–Schmidt step | $u_k = v_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} u_i$ |
| Normalization | $e_k = u_k \,/\, \vert u_k \vert$ |
| Pythagorean identity | $\vert v \vert^{2} = \vert P_W(v) \vert^{2} + \vert v - P_W(v) \vert^{2}$ |
| QR factorization | $A = QR$ where $Q$ has orthonormal columns from Gram–Schmidt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Orthogonal projections underpin **least-squares data fitting** (regression, signal processing), **QR decomposition** (used in solving linear systems and computing eigenvalues numerically), **principal component analysis (PCA)**, **image compression**, and **wireless communication MIMO decoding**. Mastery of this topic is a prerequisite for numerical linear algebra and machine learning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Projection onto a Line

**Goal:** Given a nonzero $u \in V$ and arbitrary $v \in V$, find a scalar $c \in \mathbb{F}$ such that $v - cu$ is orthogonal to $u$.

**Step 1.** Set up the orthogonality condition. The vector $z = v - cu$ must satisfy $\langle z, u \rangle = 0$:

$$
\langle v - c u, \, u \rangle = 0
$$

**Step 2.** Use **linearity** of the inner product in the first argument:

$$
\langle v, u \rangle - c \, \langle u, u \rangle = 0
$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $c$:

$$
c = \frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle}
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute back. The orthogonal projection is $cu$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{span}\{u\}}(v) = \frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} \, u\,}
$$

The remaining component is $v - P_{\text{span}\{u\}}(v) \in W^{\perp}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Projection onto a Subspace (Orthonormal Basis)

**Goal:** Let $\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_k\}$ be an orthonormal basis of $W$. Show that $P_W(v) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, e_i \rangle e_i$.

**Step 1.** Because $\{e_1, \ldots, e_k\}$ is a basis of $W$, every $u \in W$ can be written as $u = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i e_i$ for some scalars $c_i$.

**Step 2.** We need $v - u \in W^{\perp}$, i.e., $\langle v - u, e_j \rangle = 0$ for each $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$:

$$
\langle v, e_j \rangle - \langle u, e_j \rangle = 0
$$

**Step 3.** Expand $\langle u, e_j \rangle$ using the basis expansion and **orthonormality** $\langle e_i, e_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ (Kronecker delta):

$$
\langle u, e_j \rangle = \left\langle \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i e_i, e_j \right\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i \langle e_i, e_j \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i \delta_{ij} = c_j
$$

**Step 4.** Equate: $c_j = \langle v, e_j \rangle$. Substitute back into the expansion of $u$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_W(v) = u = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, e_i \rangle \, e_i\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gram–Schmidt Orthogonalization

**Goal:** Given linearly independent $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$, produce an orthogonal set $\{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n\}$ spanning the same subspace.

**Step 1.** Initialize: $u_1 = v_1$.

**Step 2.** Inductive hypothesis: suppose $u_1, \ldots, u_{k-1}$ are already mutually orthogonal. Construct $u_k$ by removing from $v_k$ all components along $u_1, \ldots, u_{k-1}$:

$$
u_k = v_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} \, u_i
$$

**Step 3.** Verification of orthogonality — for any $j < k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle u_k, u_j \rangle &= \left\langle v_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} u_i, \, u_j \right\rangle \\
&= \langle v_k, u_j \rangle - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} \langle u_i, u_j \rangle \\
&= \langle v_k, u_j \rangle - \frac{\langle v_k, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} \langle u_j, u_j \rangle \\
&= \langle v_k, u_j \rangle - \langle v_k, u_j \rangle = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

(The middle step uses the orthogonality of $\{u_1, \ldots, u_{k-1}\}$.)

**Step 4.** Linear independence: if $u_k = 0$, then $v_k \in \text{span}\{u_1, \ldots, u_{k-1}\} = \text{span}\{v_1, \ldots, v_{k-1}\}$, contradicting the linear independence of the $v_i$'s. So all $u_i$'s are nonzero, and the orthogonal set is in fact a basis.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Projection onto a Line in $\mathbb{R}^3$

**Problem:** Find the orthogonal projection of $v = (2, 3, 1)^T$ onto the line spanned by $u = (1, 1, 1)^T$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ (standard inner product).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v, u \rangle &= (2)(1) + (3)(1) + (1)(1) = 6 \\
\langle u, u \rangle &= (1)(1) + (1)(1) + (1)(1) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
P_{\text{span}\{u\}}(v) = \frac{6}{3} \cdot (1, 1, 1)^T = 2 \cdot (1, 1, 1)^T = (2, 2, 2)^T
$$

The orthogonal component is $v - P(v) = (2, 3, 1)^T - (2, 2, 2)^T = (0, 1, -1)^T$.

**Verification:** $\langle (0, 1, -1), (1, 1, 1) \rangle = 0 + 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓. Norm: $\|v\|^2 = 4 + 9 + 1 = 14 = \|P(v)\|^2 + \|v - P(v)\|^2 = 12 + 2$ ✓.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Gram–Schmidt on Three Vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$

**Problem:** Apply Gram–Schmidt to $v_1 = (1, 1, 1)^T$, $v_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$, $v_3 = (1, 0, 0)^T$.

**Step 1.** $u_1 = v_1 = (1, 1, 1)^T$, with $\|u_1\|^2 = 3$.

**Step 2.** Compute $u_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v_2, u_1 \rangle &= 1 + 1 + 0 = 2 \\
u_2 &= v_2 - \frac{2}{3} u_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T - \tfrac{2}{3}(1, 1, 1)^T = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}, -\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

For cleaner arithmetic, scale $u_2$ by 3: $\tilde{u}_2 = (1, 1, -2)^T$, with $\|\tilde{u}_2\|^2 = 6$.

**Step 3.** Compute $u_3$ using scaled $\tilde{u}_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v_3, u_1 \rangle &= 1 \\
\langle v_3, \tilde{u}_2 \rangle &= 1 \\
u_3 &= v_3 - \frac{\langle v_3, u_1 \rangle}{\|u_1\|^2} u_1 - \frac{\langle v_3, \tilde{u}_2 \rangle}{\|\tilde{u}_2\|^2} \tilde{u}_2 \\
&= (1, 0, 0)^T - \tfrac{1}{3}(1, 1, 1)^T - \tfrac{1}{6}(1, 1, -2)^T \\
&= (1, 0, 0)^T - \left(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T - \left(\tfrac{1}{6}, \tfrac{1}{6}, -\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T \\
&= \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{6}, \; 0 - \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{6}, \; 0 - \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T \\
&= \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}, 0\right)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

Scale by 2: $\tilde{u}_3 = (1, -1, 0)^T$, with $\|\tilde{u}_3\|^2 = 2$.

**Orthonormal basis:**

$$
e_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1, 1, 1)^T, \quad e_2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, 1, -2)^T, \quad e_3 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1, 0)^T
$$

**Verification (key inner products):**
$\langle e_1, e_2 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{18}}(1+1-2) = 0$ ✓
$\langle e_1, e_3 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1-1+0) = 0$ ✓
$\langle e_2, e_3 \rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{12}}(1-1+0) = 0$ ✓

## 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Projection onto a 2-D Subspace via Gram Matrix

**Problem:** Project $v = (2, 3, 1)^T$ onto $W = \text{span}\{u_1, u_2\}$ where $u_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$ and $u_2 = (1, 0, 1)^T$.

**Step 1.** Build the Gram matrix $G$:

$$
G = \begin{pmatrix} \langle u_1, u_1 \rangle & \langle u_1, u_2 \rangle \\ \langle u_2, u_1 \rangle & \langle u_2, u_2 \rangle \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the right-hand side $\mathbf{b}$:

$$
\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} \langle v, u_1 \rangle \\ \langle v, u_2 \rangle \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 + 3 + 0 \\ 2 + 0 + 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.** Solve $G \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2c_1 + c_2 &= 5 \\
c_1 + 2c_2 &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply the second equation by 2: $2c_1 + 4c_2 = 6$. Subtract the first: $3c_2 = 1 \Rightarrow c_2 = \tfrac{1}{3}$. Then $c_1 = 3 - 2c_2 = 3 - \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{7}{3}$.

**Step 4.** Assemble the projection:

$$
P_W(v) = \tfrac{7}{3}(1, 1, 0)^T + \tfrac{1}{3}(1, 0, 1)^T = \left(\tfrac{8}{3}, \tfrac{7}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T
$$

**Verification:** The component $v - P_W(v) = (2 - 8/3, \; 3 - 7/3, \; 1 - 1/3)^T = (-2/3, \; 2/3, \; 2/3)^T$.

Check $\langle v - P_W(v), u_1 \rangle = -2/3 + 2/3 + 0 = 0$ ✓
Check $\langle v - P_W(v), u_2 \rangle = -2/3 + 0 + 2/3 = 0$ ✓

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Reason |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| Projecting onto a **single vector** (1-D subspace) | Direct formula $\frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} u$ | Fastest; no linear system to solve |
| Projecting onto a subspace with an **orthonormal basis** | Direct sum $\sum \langle v, e_i \rangle e_i$ | No Gram matrix needed; numerical stability |
| Projecting onto a subspace with a **non-orthonormal basis** | Solve Gram system $G\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{b}$ | General-purpose; works for any basis |
| Building an **orthonormal basis** for a subspace | Gram–Schmidt process | Constructs $Q$ in QR factorization |
| **Least-squares** approximation | Project $v$ onto column space of $A$ via $P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ | Standard regression formula |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Orthogonal Projection Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: vector v in V"] --> B["Identify target subspace W"]
    B --> C{"Is basis of W orthonormal?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Use direct sum formula: P_W(v) = sum of inner product times e_i"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Apply Gram-Schmidt to build orthonormal basis"]
    E --> F["Normalize basis vectors to obtain e_i"]
    F --> D
    D --> G["Compute P_W(v) in W"]
    B --> H["Compute orthogonal component: v - P_W(v) in W perp"]
    G --> I["Output: v = P_W(v) plus v - P_W(v)"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Verify: inner product of P_W(v) and v - P_W(v) equals zero"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Gram–Schmidt Algorithm Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: linearly independent set v1, v2, ..., vn"] --> N1["Set u1 = v1, k = 2"]
    N1 --> Q{"k is less than or equal to n?"}
    Q -- "Yes" --> P["Compute projection coefficient: inner product of vk and ui over inner product of ui and ui, for i = 1 to k minus 1"]
    P --> M["Update uk = vk minus sum of coefficient times ui"]
    M --> Z["Check: uk is nonzero?"]
    Z -- "No" --> ERR["Error: input vectors are linearly dependent"]
    Z -- "Yes" --> K["Increment k by 1"]
    K --> Q
    Q -- "No" --> OUT["Output: orthogonal set u1, u2, ..., un"]
    OUT --> NORM["Optional: normalize each ui to get orthonormal ei"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Decomposition of Vector Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["Inner Product Space V"]:::space
    W["Subspace W"]:::sub
    Wp["Orthogonal Complement W perp"]:::sub
    v["Vector v"]:::vec
    u["Projection P_W of v in W"]:::vec
    z["Component v minus P_W in W perp"]:::vec
    V --> W
    V --> Wp
    v --> u
    v --> z
    u --> W
    z --> Wp
    classDef space fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vec fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Data Vector v"] --> B["Choose Subspace W span of u1 to uk"]
    B --> C["Construct Orthonormal Basis via Gram-Schmidt"]
    C --> D["Compute Inner Products inner product of v and e_i"]
    D --> E["Weighted Sum: sum of inner product times e_i"]
    E --> F["Projected Vector P_W of v in W"]
    F --> G["Residual: v minus P_W of v in W perp"]
    G --> H["Energy: squared norm of P_W of v"]
    H --> I["Application Output: least squares fit, PCA, QR factorization, signal compression"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q1. Define orthogonal projection. State any three of its properties.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Let $W$ be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space $V$. The orthogonal projection $P_W : V \to W$ is the unique linear map satisfying $P_W^{2} = P_W$ and $P_W^{*} = P_W$. Equivalently, every $v \in V$ decomposes uniquely as $v = P_W(v) + (v - P_W(v))$ with $P_W(v) \in W$ and $v - P_W(v) \in W^{\perp}$.

**Properties (1.5 marks — any three):**
1. **Idempotent:** $P_W^{2} = P_W$.
2. **Self-adjoint:** $P_W^{*} = P_W$.
3. **Range:** $\text{Range}(P_W) = W$, and $\ker(P_W) = W^{\perp}$.
4. **Best approximation:** $\|v - P_W(v)\| = \min_{w \in W} \|v - w\|$.
5. **Complement:** $P_W + P_{W^{\perp}} = I$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q2. State the Projection Theorem.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Projection Theorem:** Let $W$ be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space $V$. Then **every** $v \in V$ can be written **uniquely** as
> $$v = u + z, \quad u \in W, \quad z \in W^{\perp}.$$
> The vector $u$ is called the **orthogonal projection** of $v$ onto $W$, denoted $P_W(v)$. This decomposition yields $V = W \oplus W^{\perp}$ (orthogonal direct sum), and $P_W(v)$ is the **closest point** in $W$ to $v$, i.e., $\|v - P_W(v)\| \leq \|v - w\|$ for all $w \in W$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Question A or Question B)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) State and prove the Projection Theorem.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Statement (1 mark):** Let $W$ be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space $V$. Every $v \in V$ admits a unique decomposition $v = u + z$ with $u \in W$ and $z \in W^{\perp}$.

**Proof (6 marks):**

**Existence (3 marks):** Let $\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_k\}$ be an orthonormal basis of $W$ (such a basis exists by Gram–Schmidt). Define

$$
u = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, e_i \rangle e_i \in W, \qquad z = v - u.
$$

For each $j \in \{1, \ldots, k\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle z, e_j \rangle &= \langle v, e_j \rangle - \langle u, e_j \rangle \\
&= \langle v, e_j \rangle - \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle v, e_i \rangle \langle e_i, e_j \rangle \\
&= \langle v, e_j \rangle - \langle v, e_j \rangle = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

(The step uses $\langle e_i, e_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$.) Hence $z \perp e_j$ for all $j$, which means $z \in W^{\perp}$. So $v = u + z$ with $u \in W$ and $z \in W^{\perp}$.

**Uniqueness (3 marks):** Suppose $v = u_1 + z_1 = u_2 + z_2$ with $u_1, u_2 \in W$ and $z_1, z_2 \in W^{\perp}$. Subtracting:

$$
0 = (u_1 - u_2) + (z_1 - z_2), \quad \text{where } u_1 - u_2 \in W, \;\; z_1 - z_2 \in W^{\perp}.
$$

Therefore $u_1 - u_2 = -(z_1 - z_2)$ lies in **both** $W$ and $W^{\perp}$, so $u_1 - u_2 \in W \cap W^{\perp}$. But $W \cap W^{\perp} = \{0\}$ (if $x \in W \cap W^{\perp}$, then $\langle x, x \rangle = 0$, so $x = 0$). Hence $u_1 = u_2$ and $z_1 = z_2$. ∎

---

**(b) Find the orthogonal projection of $v = (2, 3, 1)^T$ onto the subspace $W = \text{span}\{u_1, u_2\}$ where $u_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T$ and $u_2 = (1, 0, 1)^T$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Apply Gram–Schmidt to obtain an orthonormal basis of $W$.

$$
u_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T, \quad \|u_1\|^2 = 2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle u_2, u_1 \rangle &= 1 + 0 + 0 = 1 \\
\tilde{u}_2 &= u_2 - \frac{1}{2} u_1 = (1, 0, 1)^T - \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}, 0\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}, 1\right)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

Scale by 2: $w_2 = (1, -1, 2)^T$, with $\|w_2\|^2 = 1 + 1 + 4 = 6$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Normalize:

$$
e_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1, 0)^T, \quad e_2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, -1, 2)^T
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute inner products with $v = (2, 3, 1)^T$:

$$
\langle v, e_1 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2 + 3 + 0) = \tfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \langle v, e_2 \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(2 - 3 + 2) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}
$$

**Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Assemble the projection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_W(v) &= \tfrac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1, 0)^T + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, -1, 2)^T \\
&= \tfrac{5}{2}(1, 1, 0)^T + \tfrac{1}{6}(1, -1, 2)^T \\
&= \left(\tfrac{5}{2} + \tfrac{1}{6}, \; \tfrac{5}{2} - \tfrac{1}{6}, \; 0 + \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T \\
&= \left(\tfrac{15 + 1}{6}, \; \tfrac{15 - 1}{6}, \; \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{16}{6}, \tfrac{14}{6}, \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{8}{3}, \tfrac{7}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_W(v) = \left(\dfrac{8}{3}, \dfrac{7}{3}, \dfrac{1}{3}\right)^T$.

**Verification (bonus, optional):** $v - P_W(v) = \left(-\tfrac{2}{3}, \tfrac{2}{3}, \tfrac{2}{3}\right)^T$. Check $\langle v - P_W(v), u_1 \rangle = -\tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{2}{3} + 0 = 0$ ✓; $\langle v - P_W(v), u_2 \rangle = -\tfrac{2}{3} + 0 + \tfrac{2}{3} = 0$ ✓.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Apply the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization process to the vectors $v_1 = (1, 1, 1)^T$, $v_2 = (1, 1, 0)^T$, $v_3 = (1, 0, 0)^T$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$, and hence construct an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^3$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Set $u_1 = v_1 = (1, 1, 1)^T$, $\|u_1\|^2 = 3$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $u_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v_2, u_1 \rangle &= 1 + 1 + 0 = 2 \\
u_2 &= v_2 - \tfrac{2}{3} u_1 = (1, 1, 0)^T - \tfrac{2}{3}(1, 1, 1)^T = \left(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3}, -\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

Scale: $\tilde{u}_2 = (1, 1, -2)^T$, $\|\tilde{u}_2\|^2 = 6$.

**Step 3 (2.5 marks):** Compute $u_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v_3, u_1 \rangle &= 1 \\
\langle v_3, \tilde{u}_2 \rangle &= 1 \\
u_3 &= v_3 - \tfrac{\langle v_3, u_1 \rangle}{\|u_1\|^2} u_1 - \tfrac{\langle v_3, \tilde{u}_2 \rangle}{\|\tilde{u}_2\|^2} \tilde{u}_2 \\
&= (1, 0, 0)^T - \tfrac{1}{3}(1, 1, 1)^T - \tfrac{1}{6}(1, 1, -2)^T \\
&= \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{6}, \; 0 - \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{6}, \; 0 - \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^T \\
&= \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, -\tfrac{1}{2}, 0\right)^T
\end{aligned}
$$

Scale: $\tilde{u}_3 = (1, -1, 0)^T$, $\|\tilde{u}_3\|^2 = 2$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Normalize to get an orthonormal basis:

$$
\boxed{\,e_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(1, 1, 1)^T, \quad e_2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(1, 1, -2)^T, \quad e_3 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1, 0)^T\,}
$$

This is a valid orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^3$.

---

**(b) Find the orthogonal projection of $v = (4, 2, 1)^T$ onto the line $L$ spanned by $u = (1, 2, 2)^T$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the relevant inner products:

$$
\langle v, u \rangle = (4)(1) + (2)(2) + (1)(2) = 4 + 4 + 2 = 10
$$

$$
\langle u, u \rangle = 1 + 4 + 4 = 9
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the projection formula onto a line:

$$
c = \frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} = \frac{10}{9}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the projected vector:

$$
P_L(v) = \tfrac{10}{9} \cdot (1, 2, 2)^T = \left(\tfrac{10}{9}, \tfrac{20}{9}, \tfrac{20}{9}\right)^T
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The orthogonal component (residual):

$$
v - P_L(v) = \left(4 - \tfrac{10}{9}, \; 2 - \tfrac{20}{9}, \; 1 - \tfrac{20}{9}\right)^T = \left(\tfrac{26}{9}, -\tfrac{2}{9}, -\tfrac{11}{9}\right)^T
$$

**Verification:** $\langle v - P_L(v), u \rangle = \tfrac{26}{9} - \tfrac{4}{9} - \tfrac{22}{9} = 0$ ✓.

**Pythagorean check:** $\|v\|^2 = 16 + 4 + 1 = 21$. $\|P_L(v)\|^2 = \tfrac{100}{81} + \tfrac{400}{81} + \tfrac{400}{81} = \tfrac{900}{81} = \tfrac{100}{9}$. $\|v - P_L(v)\|^2 = \tfrac{676 + 4 + 121}{81} = \tfrac{801}{81} = \tfrac{89}{9}$. Sum: $\tfrac{100}{9} + \tfrac{89}{9} = \tfrac{189}{9} = 21$ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator $\langle u, u \rangle$** — Many students write $\text{proj} = \langle v, u \rangle \cdot u$ instead of $\frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} u$. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 2. **Skipping the normalization step** — In Gram–Schmidt, dividing by $\|u_i\|$ to get $e_i$ is **not** automatic when you scale. You must explicitly normalize if the problem asks for an **orthonormal** set. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 3. **Not verifying orthogonality** — KTU examiners reward the verification step $\langle u_k, u_j \rangle = 0$ for $j < k$ at the end. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing projection onto a line vs. onto a subspace** — For a 2-D or higher subspace with a non-orthonormal basis, the simple formula $\frac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} u$ does **not** apply to each basis vector separately. You must either (a) first orthogonalize via Gram–Schmidt, or (b) solve the Gram system $G\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{b}$. (Penalty: 2–3 marks)
> 5. **Sign errors in arithmetic** — Gram–Schmidt involves heavy fractions; one arithmetic slip propagates. KTU examiners are instructed not to give method marks if the final answer is wrong **and** no intermediate check is shown. Always verify your answer with the orthogonality test.
> 6. **Omitting the residual/component in $W^{\perp}$** — The problem often asks for the projection **and** the component; missing the second part loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Inner product** is bilinear (or sesquilinear in $\mathbb{C}$), conjugate-symmetric, and positive definite.
- [x] **Orthogonal complement** $W^{\perp}$ contains all vectors in $V$ perpendicular to every vector in $W$.
- [x] **Projection Theorem** guarantees unique decomposition $v = P_W(v) + (v - P_W(v))$ with $P_W(v) \in W$, $v - P_W(v) \in W^{\perp}$.
- [x] **Projection onto a line:** $P_u(v) = \dfrac{\langle v, u \rangle}{\langle u, u \rangle} u$.
- [x] **Projection onto subspace (orthonormal basis $\{e_i\}$):** $P_W(v) = \sum \langle v, e_i \rangle e_i$.
- [x] **Projection onto subspace (general basis):** Solve $G\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{b}$ where $G$ is the Gram matrix.
- [x] **Gram–Schmidt** converts any basis to an orthogonal one via $u_k = v_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle v_k, u_i \rangle}{\langle u_i, u_i \rangle} u_i$.
- [x] **Properties of $P_W$:** $P_W^2 = P_W$, $P_W^* = P_W$, $\text{Range}(P_W) = W$, $\ker(P_W) = W^{\perp}$.
- [x] **Best approximation:** $\|v - P_W(v)\| \leq \|v - w\|$ for all $w \in W$.
- [x] **Pythagoras:** $\|v\|^2 = \|P_W(v)\|^2 + \|v - P_W(v)\|^2$.
- [x] **Complement relation:** $P_W + P_{W^{\perp}} = I$ (identity operator).
- [x] **Engineering use cases:** least-squares regression, QR factorization, PCA, signal projection, image compression.
- [x] **Always verify** the orthogonality $\langle P_W(v), v - P_W(v) \rangle = 0$ at the end of a problem.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Scheme tip:** Module 3 questions on this topic usually pair Gram–Schmidt with a projection calculation — practice both in one sitting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pre-Requisite: Inner Product & Inner Product Space

Before we discuss orthogonal and orthonormal sets, we must recall the concept of an **Inner Product**. For a real vector space $V$, an inner product is a function that assigns to every pair of vectors $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in V$ a real number $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$ satisfying the following axioms (for a real inner product space):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Axioms of a Real Inner Product:**
> 1. **Symmetry:** $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u} \rangle$
> 2. **Linearity:** $\langle a\mathbf{u} + b\mathbf{w}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = a\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle + b\langle \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{v} \rangle$
> 3. **Positivity:** $\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \geq 0$ and $\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0 \iff \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$

A vector space equipped with an inner product is called an **Inner Product Space**. The standard inner product in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is the dot product:
$$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = u_1v_1 + u_2v_2 + \dots + u_nv_n$$

The **norm** (length) induced by the inner product is defined as:
$$\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}$$

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition: Orthogonal Set

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Orthogonal Vectors):** Two vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ in an inner product space $V$ are said to be **orthogonal** if their inner product is zero, i.e.,
> $$\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$$
> Geometrically, orthogonal vectors are **perpendicular** to each other. The zero vector $\mathbf{0}$ is considered orthogonal to every vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Orthogonal Set):** A set of non-zero vectors $S = \{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ in an inner product space $V$ is called an **orthogonal set** if every pair of distinct vectors in $S$ is orthogonal. That is,
> $$\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle = 0 \quad \text{for all } i \neq j$$

**Example 1:** In $\mathbb{R}^3$ with the standard dot product, consider the set
$$S = \{(1, 0, 0), (0, 1, 0), (0, 0, 1)\}$$
Check: $(1,0,0) \cdot (0,1,0) = 0$, $(1,0,0) \cdot (0,0,1) = 0$, $(0,1,0) \cdot (0,0,1) = 0$. So $S$ is an orthogonal set.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition: Orthonormal Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Orthonormal Set):** A set of vectors $S = \{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ in an inner product space $V$ is called an **orthonormal set** if it is an orthogonal set AND every vector in $S$ is a **unit vector** (norm = 1). In compact form:
> $$\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle = \delta_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i = j \\ 0 & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$$
> The symbol $\delta_{ij}$ is the famous **Kronecker Delta**.

**To convert an orthogonal set to an orthonormal set**, we simply **normalize** each vector by dividing by its norm:
$$\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{\mathbf{v}_i}{\|\mathbf{v}_i\|}$$

**Example 2:** Normalizing the orthogonal set from Example 1:
$$\mathbf{e}_1 = (1, 0, 0), \quad \mathbf{e}_2 = (0, 1, 0), \quad \mathbf{e}_3 = (0, 0, 1)$$
These are already unit vectors, so the set is already orthonormal. This is the **standard basis** of $\mathbb{R}^3$.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Orthogonal Toolbox":**
> Imagine you are a carpenter with a set of tools (vectors). If your tools are **orthogonal**, it means each tool is designed for a **completely independent purpose** — using a hammer tells you nothing about a screwdriver, and they don't overlap in function. This independence is powerful because it means no tool is "wasted" information.
>
> If your tools are **orthonormal**, they are not only independent, but each tool is the **"standard size"** (length = 1). Standard-sized tools are easy to mix and match — if you need 3 hammers and 2 screwdrivers, you know exactly what 5 tools will do without measuring anything.
>
> In math, orthogonal vectors give us **independent directions** (no redundancy), and orthonormal vectors give us a **clean, standardized coordinate system** — exactly what we need for projections, decompositions, and solving least-squares problems.

---

## 1.5 Why are Orthogonal & Orthonormal Sets Important?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> 1. **Simplify Computations:** Inner products involving orthonormal vectors reduce to simple multiplications.
> 2. **Basis Construction:** Every finite-dimensional inner product space has an orthonormal basis (proven via Gram-Schmidt Process).
> 3. **Orthogonal Projections:** The formula for projecting a vector onto a subspace becomes elegant: $\text{proj}_W(\mathbf{v}) = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_1 \rangle \mathbf{u}_1 + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_2 \rangle \mathbf{u}_2 + \dots$
> 4. **Change of Basis:** Orthonormal matrices (those satisfying $Q^TQ = I$) preserve lengths and angles. These are crucial in **QR Decomposition**, **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)**, and **Fourier Transforms**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Orthogonal Sets

Let $S = \{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ be an orthogonal set of non-zero vectors in an inner product space $V$.

### Property 1: Linear Independence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** Every orthogonal set of non-zero vectors is **linearly independent**.
>
> **Proof Sketch:** Suppose $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + \dots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{0}$. Take the inner product of both sides with $\mathbf{v}_i$ for any $i$:
> $$\langle c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \dots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle = \langle \mathbf{0}, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle = 0$$
> By linearity and orthogonality, all terms vanish except the $i$-th term:
> $$c_i \langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle = 0$$
> Since $\mathbf{v}_i \neq \mathbf{0}$, we have $\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle > 0$, so $c_i = 0$. This holds for every $i$, proving linear independence. $\blacksquare$

### Property 2: Expansion Theorem (Projection onto an Orthogonal Subspace)

Let $W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k\}$ be a subspace spanned by an orthogonal set. Then for any vector $\mathbf{v} \in W$:
$$\mathbf{v} = c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + \dots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k$$
where the coefficients are:
$$c_i = \frac{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle}{\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle} = \frac{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle}{\|\mathbf{v}_i\|^2}$$

### Property 3: Pythagorean Theorem (Generalized)

If $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k$ are mutually orthogonal, then:
$$\|\mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2 + \dots + \mathbf{v}_k\|^2 = \|\mathbf{v}_1\|^2 + \|\mathbf{v}_2\|^2 + \dots + \|\mathbf{v}_k\|^2$$

This is a generalization of the classic $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ for right triangles!

---

## 2.2 Properties of Orthonormal Sets

Let $S = \{\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2, \dots, \mathbf{u}_k\}$ be an **orthonormal set** in an inner product space $V$. Since $\|\mathbf{u}_i\| = 1$, we have $\langle \mathbf{u}_i, \mathbf{u}_i \rangle = 1$, which simplifies all formulas:

### Simplified Expansion Formula:
For $\mathbf{v} \in W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{u}_1, \dots, \mathbf{u}_k\}$:
$$\mathbf{v} = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_1 \rangle \mathbf{u}_1 + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_2 \rangle \mathbf{u}_2 + \dots + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_k \rangle \mathbf{u}_k$$

### Simplified Pythagorean Theorem:
$$\|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_1 \rangle^2 + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_2 \rangle^2 + \dots + \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_k \rangle^2$$

### Orthogonal Projection (Simplified):
The projection of any vector $\mathbf{v}$ (not necessarily in $W$) onto the subspace $W$ is:
$$\text{proj}_W \mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_i \rangle \mathbf{u}_i$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Condition |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Orthogonality | $\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle = 0$ | Inner product is zero |
| 2 | Orthogonal Set | $\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle = 0$ for all $i \neq j$ | All pairs are perpendicular |
| 3 | Orthonormal Set | $\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j \rangle = \delta_{ij}$ | Orthogonal **and** each has norm 1 |
| 4 | Normalization | $\mathbf{u}_i = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_i}{\|\mathbf{v}_i\|}$ | Convert orthogonal → orthonormal |
| 5 | Linear Independence | Any orthogonal set of non-zero vectors is LI | $\mathbf{v}_i \neq \mathbf{0}$ |
| 6 | Expansion Coefficients (Orthogonal) | $c_i = \dfrac{\langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle}{\langle \mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_i \rangle}$ | For $\mathbf{v} \in \text{span}\{\mathbf{v}_i\}$ |
| 7 | Expansion Coefficients (Orthonormal) | $c_i = \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_i \rangle$ | Norm drops out, simpler! |
| 8 | Pythagorean Theorem | $\left\| \sum \mathbf{v}_i \right\|^2 = \sum \|\mathbf{v}_i\|^2$ | Pairwise orthogonal vectors |
| 9 | Bessel's Inequality | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_i \rangle \vert^2 \leq \|\mathbf{v}\|^2$ | For any orthonormal set |
| 10 | Parseval's Identity | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_i \rangle \vert^2 = \|\mathbf{v}\|^2$ | When $\{u_i\}$ is a complete ONB |
| 11 | Orthogonal Projection | $\text{proj}_W \mathbf{v} = \sum \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{u}_i \rangle \mathbf{u}_i$ | $W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{u}_1, \dots, \mathbf{u}_k\}$ |
| 12 | Distance to Subspace | $\text{dist}(\mathbf{v}, W) = \|\mathbf{v} - \text{proj}_W \mathbf{v}\|$ | Shortest distance |
| 13 | Angle Between Vectors | $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle}{\|\mathbf{u}\| \, \|\mathbf{v}\|}$ | $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ |
| 14 | Cauchy-Schwarz | $\vert \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle \vert \leq \|\mathbf{u}\| \, \|\mathbf{v}\|$ | Always holds |
| 15 | Orthogonal Matrix | $Q^T Q = I$, so $Q^{-1} = Q^T$ | Columns are orthonormal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse **orthogonal matrix** (whose columns are orthonormal) with an **orthogonal set of vectors** (a set of mutually perpendicular vectors). An orthogonal matrix's *columns* form an orthonormal set.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Orthogonal/Orthonormal Sets |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| **Signal Processing** | Fourier basis (sines and cosines) is an orthonormal set in $L^2$ — used in MP3, JPEG compression |
| **Computer Graphics** | Rotation matrices are orthogonal — preserve lengths and angles during 3D rotations |
| **Machine Learning** | Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds orthonormal eigenvectors of the covariance matrix |
| **Communications** | Walsh-Hadamard codes are orthonormal — enables CDMA in mobile networks |
| **Numerical Methods** | QR Decomposition uses orthonormal matrices for stable least-squares solutions |
| **Quantum Computing** | Quantum states form an orthonormal basis in complex inner product space (Hilbert space) |
