> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully — Marks Lost Here!)
>
> 1. **Forgetting normalization (–2 marks):** Many students compute only $u_k$ and stop. The question asks for the *orthonormal* set, so the $e_k$ step is **mandatory** unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Arithmetic slip in dot product (–1 to –2 marks):** Cross-check $\langle v_k, u_j \rangle$ with a calculator. One wrong sign cascades into all subsequent steps.
> 3. **No verification (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for verifying orthonormality. Always show the three unit-norm checks and the three orthogonality checks.
> 4. **Confusing orthogonal with orthonormal (–1 mark):** Be precise in vocabulary. "Orthogonal" $\neq$ "Orthonormal."
> 5. **Not simplifying $u_k$ before normalizing (–0.5 to –1 mark):** If $u_3 = \frac{2}{3}(-1, 1, 1)$, normalize using $(-1, 1, 1)$ to avoid fraction clutter. The scalar $\frac{2}{3}$ cancels anyway.
> 6. **Missing line in algorithm (–1 mark):** When $k = 2$, write the formula *fully*: $u_2 = v_2 - \frac{\langle v_2, u_1 \rangle}{\langle u_1, u_1 \rangle} u_1$. Don't write "$u_2 = v_2 - \text{proj}_{u_1} v_2$" without expansion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Pin This to Your Wall Before the Exam**

- [ ] **GSOP** transforms linearly independent vectors into **orthonormal** vectors preserving the **span**.
- [ ] **Two stages:** First orthogonalize ($u_k$), then normalize ($e_k = u_k / \lVert u_k \rVert$).
- [ ] **Formula to memorize:** $u_k = v_k - \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \dfrac{\langle v_k, u_j \rangle}{\langle u_j, u_j \rangle} u_j$.
- [ ] **$u_1 = v_1$** always (base case).
- [ ] **Prerequisite:** The input vectors **must be linearly independent** (else $\lVert u_k \rVert = 0$).
- [ ] **Inner product for $\mathbb{R}^n$:** $\langle x, y \rangle = \sum x_i y_i$ (dot product).
- [ ] **Norm:** $\lVert u \rVert = \sqrt{\langle u, u \rangle} = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$.
- [ ] **Verification rules** (always include in KTU answers):
  - $\lVert e_i \rVert = 1$ for all $i$
  - $\langle e_i, e_j \rangle = 0$ for $i \neq j$
- [ ] **Application:** GSOP is the foundation of **QR Decomposition** $A = QR$.
- [ ] **In $\mathbb{R}^n$**, $n$ orthonormal vectors form an **orthonormal basis**.
- [ ] **Gram-Schmidt on dependent inputs** fails — first extract a maximal linearly independent subset.
- [ ] **Common slip:** Forgetting to verify $\langle e_i, e_j \rangle$ after construction — this is *free marks* in KTU valuation.
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** **"Subtract Shadows, then Stretch-to-Unit"** = Orthogonalize, then Normalize.
- [ ] **Worked example takeaway:** Always re-simplify $u_k$ to eliminate common scalar factors before computing the norm (saves time and reduces arithmetic errors).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Gram-Schmidt Orthonormalization Process**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Least Squares Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Least Squares Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Least Squares Problem** is a fundamental problem in numerical linear algebra that seeks to find an approximate solution to an overdetermined system of linear equations $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, where $A$ is an $m \times n$ matrix with $m > n$ (more equations than unknowns). Since an exact solution typically does not exist, the objective is to find a vector $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that **minimizes the sum of the squares of the residuals**, i.e., the squared Euclidean norm of the error vector $A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$.

Mathematically, the problem is stated as:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^2$$

The solution $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is called the **least squares solution**, and the corresponding vector $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is the **projection** of $\mathbf{b}$ onto the column space $\mathcal{C}(A)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Least Squares Problem is a cornerstone of Module 3, bridging linear algebra with real-world data fitting. It is the mathematical engine behind regression analysis, signal processing, machine learning, and computer graphics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **archaeologist** with 5 scattered data points representing the depth of an ancient riverbed at 5 different positions. You want to draw a single **straight line** that best represents the overall trend of the river. You cannot pass a line through all 5 points exactly (since the data is noisy and inconsistent), so what do you do?

You draw the line that **minimizes the total vertical distance** (squared) from each data point to the line itself. Some points will be above the line, some below — but the **sum of the squares of these vertical gaps** is as small as possible. This optimal line is the **least squares line**, and the vertical gaps are called **residuals**.

**Real-world applications** include:
- **Civil Engineering:** Fitting a curve to stress-strain experimental data.
- **Computer Science:** Linear regression in machine learning models.
- **Economics:** Modeling the relationship between supply and demand.
- **Signal Processing:** Noise filtering and channel estimation.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Definitions for KTU Exams:**
> - **Residual Vector:** $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ — the difference between actual and predicted values.
> - **Normal Equation:** $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$ — the central equation whose solution is the least squares estimate.
> - **Overdetermined System:** A system with $m > n$ (more equations than unknowns) where an exact solution is generally impossible.
> - **Best Approximation Property:** The least squares solution $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is the closest point in $\mathcal{C}(A)$ to $\mathbf{b}$ in the Euclidean norm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometry Behind Least Squares

To understand *why* the normal equations work, consider the following geometric reasoning:

- The column space of $A$, denoted $\mathcal{C}(A)$, is the subspace of $\mathbb{R}^m$ spanned by the columns of $A$.
- Since $\mathbf{b}$ generally does not lie in $\mathcal{C}(A)$ (no exact solution exists), we seek the **closest point** in $\mathcal{C}(A)$ to $\mathbf{b}$.
- The closest point is the **orthogonal projection** of $\mathbf{b}$ onto $\mathcal{C}(A)$, call it $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.
- The vector connecting $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ to $\mathbf{b}$ (i.e., the residual) must be **orthogonal** to every vector in $\mathcal{C}(A)$.

This orthogonality condition yields the normal equations directly.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Derivation

- **Step 1 (Setup):** Define the objective function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^2$.
- **Step 2 (Expand):** Use the identity $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert^2 = \mathbf{v}^T \mathbf{v}$ to write $f(\mathbf{x}) = (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$.
- **Step 3 (Differentiate):** Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ and set it to zero for the minimum.
- **Step 4 (Simplify):** The result is the normal equation $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$.
- **Step 5 (Solve):** If $A^T A$ is invertible (which happens when columns of $A$ are linearly independent), then $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$.
- **Step 6 (Project):** The least squares approximation of $\mathbf{b}$ is $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$. The matrix $P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ is called the **projection matrix** onto $\mathcal{C}(A)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All the formulas you MUST memorize for KTU Board Exams on this topic:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective Function | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^2$ | Quantity to minimize |
| 2 | Residual Vector | $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ | Error vector |
| 3 | Normal Equations | $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$ | Core equation |
| 4 | Least Squares Solution | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ | Requires $\text{rank}(A) = n$ |
| 5 | Projection Matrix | $P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ | Projects $\mathbf{b}$ onto $\mathcal{C}(A)$ |
| 6 | Approximation | $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = P\mathbf{b}$ | Best fit value |
| 7 | Minimum SSE | $\Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 - \mathbf{b}^T A \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ | Total squared error |
| 8 | Linear Fit (1D) | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Slope-intercept form |
| 9 | Quadratic Fit | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$ | Polynomial degree 2 |
| 10 | Orthogonality Condition | $A^T(\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{0}$ | Geometric foundation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **Information Science**, the projection matrix $P$ is used in dimensionality reduction (PCA), recommender systems, and least-squares classification. The normal equations are computationally foundational for training linear models.

### 2.4 Conditions for Existence and Uniqueness

- **Existence:** A least squares solution **always exists**.
- **Uniqueness:** The solution is **unique** if and only if the columns of $A$ are linearly independent, which is equivalent to $A^T A$ being invertible.
- **Rank Deficiency:** If $\text{rank}(A) < n$, infinitely many least squares solutions exist, and we typically choose the one with the smallest norm (the pseudoinverse solution).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Normal Equations

We begin with the objective function:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^2$$

Expanding using the dot product identity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}) \\
&= \mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^T A^T \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^T A \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b} \\
&= \mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2 \mathbf{x}^T A^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The cross terms $\mathbf{x}^T A^T \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{b}^T A \mathbf{x}$ are scalars, hence equal to each other and combine to give $-2\mathbf{x}^T A^T \mathbf{b}$.

To find the minimum, differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ and set the gradient to zero:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) = 2 A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2 A^T \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2 A^T \mathbf{b} &= \mathbf{0} \\
A^T A \mathbf{x} &= A^T \mathbf{b} \\
\hat{\mathbf{x}} &= (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The factor of 2 cancels on both sides. We require $A^T A$ to be invertible, which holds iff columns of $A$ are linearly independent. The matrix $A^\dagger = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ is called the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** (left pseudoinverse) of $A$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Linear Regression

**Problem [KTU University Exam Style]:** Fit a straight line $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ to the data points $(1, 2)$, $(2, 3)$, $(3, 5)$, $(4, 7)$ using the least squares method.

**Step 1 — Set up the system in matrix form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A^T A$:**

$$
A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A^T \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
A^T \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 17 \\ 51 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the normal equations $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 17 \\ 51 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant of $A^T A$ is $\det = (4)(30) - (10)(10) = 120 - 100 = 20$.

Using Cramer's rule:

$$
\beta_0 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 17 & 10 \\ 51 & 30 \end{vmatrix}}{20} = \frac{(17)(30) - (10)(51)}{20} = \frac{510 - 510}{20} = 0
$$

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 17 \\ 10 & 51 \end{vmatrix}}{20} = \frac{(4)(51) - (17)(10)}{20} = \frac{204 - 170}{20} = \frac{34}{20} = 1.7
$$

**Step 5 — Final fitted line:**

$$\boxed{y = 1.7 x}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of residuals:**

Predicted values: $\hat{y} = (1.7, 3.4, 5.1, 6.8)$.
Residuals: $(2 - 1.7, 3 - 3.4, 5 - 5.1, 7 - 6.8) = (0.3, -0.4, -0.1, 0.2)$.
Sum of squared residuals: $0.09 + 0.16 + 0.01 + 0.04 = 0.30$ (the minimum achievable value).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Quadratic Fit

**Problem:** Fit $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2$ to the data $(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 5), (3, 10)$.

**Step 1 — Form the design matrix:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 & 9 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 5 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A^T A$:**

$$
A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 14 \\ 6 & 14 & 36 \\ 14 & 36 & 98 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A^T \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
A^T \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 108 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the $3 \times 3$ linear system:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 14 \\ 6 & 14 & 36 \\ 14 & 36 & 98 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 108 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving (Gaussian elimination):

- $R_2 \to R_2 - 1.5 R_1$, $R_3 \to R_3 - 3.5 R_1$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 14 \\ 0 & 5 & 15 \\ 0 & 15 & 49 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 15 \\ 45 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- $R_3 \to R_3 - 3 R_2$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 14 \\ 0 & 5 & 15 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 15 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Back-substitution: $\beta_2 = 0$, $5\beta_1 + 15(0) = 15 \Rightarrow \beta_1 = 3$, $4\beta_0 + 6(3) + 14(0) = 18 \Rightarrow \beta_0 = 0$.

**Step 5 — Final quadratic fit:**

$$\boxed{y = 3x}$$

> *Note:* The pure quadratic coefficient vanished, indicating the data actually follows a perfect linear trend $y = 3x$, with $y(0)=1$ being a single noisy outlier.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Least Squares Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Points: x_i, y_i] --> B[Choose Model Type]
    B --> C{Linear y = b0 + b1 x}
    B --> D{Polynomial degree n}
    B --> E{Multiple Regression}
    C --> F[Construct Design Matrix A]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Form Vector b from Outputs]
    G --> H[Compute A Transpose times A]
    G --> I[Compute A Transpose times b]
    H --> J[Form Normal Equation A Transpose A x = A Transpose b]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Is A Transpose A Invertible?}
    K --> L[Yes: Unique Solution]
    K --> M[No: Use Pseudoinverse SVD]
    L --> N[Compute x_hat = A Transpose A inverse times A Transpose b]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Predictions b_hat = A times x_hat]
    O --> P[Compute Residuals r = b minus b_hat]
    P --> Q[Compute Minimum Sum of Squared Errors]
    Q --> R[Output Fitted Model Parameters]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Geometric Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vector b in R superscript m] --> B[Column Space of A: Subspace S]
    B --> C[Orthogonal Projection of b onto S: A x_hat]
    A --> D[Residual r = b minus A x_hat]
    C --> D
    D --> E[r is perpendicular to S]
    E --> F[Minimum distance achieved]
    F --> G[Least Squares Solution Found]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison: Exact vs Least Squares

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System A x = b] --> B{Consistent System?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Exact Solution Exists]
    B -- No --> D[No Exact Solution]
    C --> E[Solve directly via Gaussian Elimination]
    D --> F[Apply Least Squares Method]
    F --> G[Minimize Norm of A x minus b]
    G --> H[Solve Normal Equations]
    H --> I[Approximate Best Fit Solution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the Least Squares Problem. When does a unique least squares solution exist?

**Model Answer:**
The Least Squares Problem seeks to find $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ that minimizes $\Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^2$ for an overdetermined system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. A **unique** least squares solution exists if and only if the columns of $A$ are **linearly independent**, equivalently $A^T A$ is invertible. The unique solution is then $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is a projection matrix? State its two key properties.

**Model Answer:**
The projection matrix $P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ projects any vector onto the column space of $A$. Its two key properties are:
- **Idempotency:** $P^2 = P$ (applying the projection twice is the same as once).
- **Symmetry:** $P^T = P$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the normal equations for the least squares problem $\min \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_2^2$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Fit a least squares line $y = a + bx$ to the data points $(0, 1), (1, 3), (2, 4), (3, 6)$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to (a):**

We start with the objective function $f(\mathbf{x}) = (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^T (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$.

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= \mathbf{x}^T A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{x}^T A^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) = 2 A^T A \mathbf{x} - 2 A^T \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Therefore, the **normal equations** are:

$$A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$$

Assuming $A^T A$ is invertible:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$$

> *Logic:* The minimum is obtained by setting the gradient to zero because $f$ is a convex quadratic in $\mathbf{x}$.

---

**Solution to (b):**

Design matrix and target:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 4 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $A^T A$:

$$
A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $A^T \mathbf{b}$:

$$
A^T \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 29 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solve:

$$
\begin{vmatrix} A^T A \end{vmatrix} = (4)(14) - (6)(6) = 56 - 36 = 20
$$

$$
a = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 14 & 6 \\ 29 & 14 \end{vmatrix}}{20} = \frac{196 - 174}{20} = \frac{22}{20} = 1.1
$$

$$
b = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 14 \\ 6 & 29 \end{vmatrix}}{20} = \frac{116 - 84}{20} = \frac{32}{20} = 1.6
$$

**Fitted line:** $\boxed{y = 1.1 + 1.6 x}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the geometric interpretation of the least squares problem. Show that the residual vector is orthogonal to the column space of $A$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Find the least squares solution of the system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 7 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}$. Also compute the projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto the column space of $A$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Geometric Interpretation:**
The vector $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ generally lies outside the column space $\mathcal{C}(A)$, a 2D plane in $\mathbb{R}^3$. The least squares solution seeks the closest point in this plane to $\mathbf{b}$. That closest point is the **orthogonal projection** $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, and the line from $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ to $\mathbf{b}$ is perpendicular to the entire plane.

**Proof of Orthogonality:**
The squared distance function is $g(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$. Its minimum satisfies:

$$\frac{d}{d\mathbf{x}} g(\mathbf{x}) = 2 A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0}$$

Hence $A^T(\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{0}$, meaning the residual $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is orthogonal to every column of $A$, and therefore to the entire $\mathcal{C}(A)$.

---

**Solution to (b):**

$$
A^T A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A^T \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 7 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 19 \\ 42 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Determinant: $(3)(14) - (6)(6) = 42 - 36 = 6$.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b} = \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 19 \\ 42 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 14 - 6 \\ 6 + \ldots \end{bmatrix}
$$

Let me recompute cleanly:

$$
(A^T A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 19 \\ 42 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 266 - 252 \\ -114 + 126 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{6} \begin{bmatrix} 14 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Projection:**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A \hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 7/3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13/3 \\ 19/3 \\ 25/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Verification:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (7/3, 2)$, $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = (13/3, 19/3, 25/3)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $(A^T A)^{-1}$ correctly** — determinant errors cost 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $A^T A$ and $A A^T$** — they are different matrices; always use $A^T A$ for the normal equations (not $A A^T$).
> 3. **Not verifying invertibility of $A^T A$** — if $\det = 0$, the standard formula fails, and you must use the pseudoinverse or SVD approach.
> 4. **Arithmetic slips in $A^T \mathbf{b}$** — recheck each row multiplication carefully.
> 5. **Forgetting the formula derivation** — KTU typically awards 3-4 marks just for deriving the normal equation itself, even if the numerical answer has minor errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Least Squares Problem:**
> - The Least Squares Problem minimizes $\Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert^2$ when an exact solution is impossible.
> - The **normal equations** are $A^T A \mathbf{x} = A^T \mathbf{b}$ — the single most important equation in this topic.
> - The closed-form solution is $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$, valid when $A$ has full column rank.
> - The matrix $A^\dagger = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ is the **left pseudoinverse** of $A$ (one-sided).
> - The **projection matrix** $P = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ is **symmetric and idempotent**: $P^T = P$, $P^2 = P$.
> - The residual $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is **orthogonal** to $\mathcal{C}(A)$, encoded in $A^T \mathbf{r} = \mathbf{0}$.
> - For **linear data fitting** $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$, the design matrix has a column of ones and a column of $x$-values.
> - For **polynomial fitting** of degree $k$, the design matrix has columns $[1, x, x^2, \ldots, x^k]$.
> - **Uniqueness** requires linearly independent columns of $A$; otherwise, use the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** $A^+ = V \Sigma^+ U^T$ via SVD.
> - **Numerical warning:** For ill-conditioned $A$, forming $A^T A$ amplifies errors; prefer QR decomposition or SVD in practice.
> - The minimum sum of squared errors is $\Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert^2 - \Vert A\hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$, also equal to $\mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^T A \hat{\mathbf{x}}$.
> - **Engineering applications:** regression, signal processing, image reconstruction, control systems, machine learning linear models.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projection onto a Subspace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Projection onto a Subspace

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Let $W$ be a non-zero subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$. For each vector $\mathbf{b}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the **orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto $W$** is the unique vector $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W$ such that $(\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}})$ is orthogonal to $W$. The vector $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is denoted by $\text{proj}_W \mathbf{b}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The orthogonal projection $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ of $\mathbf{b}$ onto a subspace $W$ satisfies two conditions:
> 1. $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W$ (the projected vector lies in the subspace)
> 2. $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W^{\perp}$ (the error vector is orthogonal to $W$)

This decomposition $\mathbf{b} = \hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{z}$, where $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W$ and $\mathbf{z} \in W^{\perp}$, is called the **Orthogonal Decomposition of $\mathbf{b}$**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Shadow on the Wall"

Imagine you are holding a flashlight in a dark room, pointing it at a stick that is tilted in the air. The **shadow** cast by the stick on the **floor** is the closest point on the floor to the stick itself.

- The **stick** = original vector $\mathbf{b}$
- The **floor (or wall)** = subspace $W$
- The **shadow** = projection $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \text{proj}_W \mathbf{b}$
- The **perpendicular distance from the stick to the floor** = the orthogonal component $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The shadow is the *closest* point in the subspace to the original vector. This is the geometric foundation of the **Best Approximation Theorem** used heavily in KTU numerical methods and signal processing modules.

### 1.3 Standard Metric & Notation

The norm used is the **Euclidean norm** $\|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \cdots + v_n^2}$, and orthogonality is defined using the **standard inner product** $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i v_i$.

The **standard metric** for measuring approximation error is $\|\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}\|$, which represents the shortest distance from $\mathbf{b}$ to the subspace $W$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Projection Concept

**Step 1 — Identify the Subspace $W$:**
Determine whether $W$ is a line (1-D), plane (2-D), or higher-dimensional hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

**Step 2 — Construct a Basis for $W$:**
Find an orthogonal or orthonormal basis $\{\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{u}_p\}$ for $W$. Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization is used when only a non-orthogonal basis is given.

**Step 3 — Compute the Projection Using the Formula:**
The projection is the sum of the individual scalar projections onto each basis vector.

**Step 4 — Verify Orthogonality Condition:**
Check that $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is orthogonal to every vector in $W$.

### 2.2 Why Projection Matters in Engineering

- **Signal Processing:** Removing noise by projecting onto a signal subspace.
- **Computer Graphics:** Shadow rendering and camera projection.
- **Machine Learning:** Linear regression (least squares) is a projection of $\mathbf{y}$ onto the column space of $X$.
- **Control Systems:** State estimation via Kalman filters uses orthogonal projections.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto a single vector $\mathbf{a}$ | $\text{proj}_{\mathbf{a}}\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}$ | $\mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{0}$ |
| 2 | Projection onto line through origin | $\text{proj}_{L}\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}$ | $L = \text{span}\{\mathbf{a}\}$ |
| 3 | Projection onto orthogonal set $\{\mathbf{u}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{u}_p\}$ | $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}{\mathbf{u}_1\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}\mathbf{u}_1 + \cdots + \dfrac{\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{u}_p}{\mathbf{u}_p\cdot\mathbf{u}_p}\mathbf{u}_p$ | Requires orthogonal basis |
| 4 | Projection matrix onto $\text{Col}(A)$ | $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$ | $A$ has linearly independent columns |
| 5 | Projection matrix onto $\text{Col}(A)$ (orthonormal $Q$) | $P = QQ^T$ | $Q$ columns are orthonormal basis |
| 6 | Idempotent property | $P^2 = P$ | All projection matrices are idempotent |
| 7 | Symmetric property | $P^T = P$ | Only **orthogonal** projections |
| 8 | Pythagorean relation | $\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 = \|\hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2$ | Orthogonal decomposition |
| 9 | Normal equations for least squares | $A^TA\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^T\mathbf{b}$ | Solved to get $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ |
| 10 | Best approximation | $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T\mathbf{b}$ | Minimizes $\|\mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x}\|$ |
| 11 | Gram-Schmidt step | $\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1}\dfrac{\mathbf{x}_k\cdot\mathbf{v}_i}{\mathbf{v}_i\cdot\mathbf{v}_i}\mathbf{v}_i$ | Builds orthogonal basis |
| 12 | QR factorization | $A = QR$ | $Q$ is orthonormal, $R$ is upper triangular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Alert:** For KTU board exams, problems 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 in the formula sheet appear in **more than 80%** of Module 3 questions. Master these.

### 2.4 Geometric Interpretation of Formula 1

The scalar coefficient $\dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a}}$ is the **signed length** of the shadow of $\mathbf{b}$ along $\mathbf{a}$. If $\mathbf{a}$ is a unit vector ($\|\mathbf{a}\| = 1$), this simplifies to $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}$, which is just the component of $\mathbf{b}$ along $\mathbf{a}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto a Line $L = \text{span}\{\mathbf{a}\}$

**Setup:** Let $\mathbf{a} \neq \mathbf{0}$ and $L = \text{Span}\{\mathbf{a}\}$. We seek $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in L$ such that $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}} \perp L$, equivalently $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}} \perp \mathbf{a}$.

**Step 1:** Express $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ as a scalar multiple of $\mathbf{a}$ since $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in L$.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \alpha \mathbf{a} \quad \text{for some scalar } \alpha \in \mathbb{R}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the orthogonality condition $\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}) = 0$.

$$
\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} - \alpha \mathbf{a}) = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the dot product.

$$
\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} - \alpha (\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a}) = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\alpha$.

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\alpha$ back.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}
$$

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \text{proj}_{L}\mathbf{b} = \dfrac{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Projection Matrix $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$

**Setup:** Let $W = \text{Col}(A)$, where $A$ is an $n \times p$ matrix with linearly independent columns. We want a matrix $P$ such that $P\mathbf{b} = \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ for every $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Step 1:** Since $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in \text{Col}(A)$, there exists a unique $\hat{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ such that:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}}
$$

**Step 2:** The error vector $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ must be orthogonal to $\text{Col}(A)$, meaning it is orthogonal to every column $\mathbf{a}_j$ of $A$. This gives:

$$
\mathbf{a}_j^T(\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = 0 \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

**Step 3:** Stack these $p$ equations into a single matrix equation.

$$
A^T(\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand and rearrange — these are the **Normal Equations**.

$$
A^TA\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^T\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $A$ has linearly independent columns, $A^TA$ is invertible. Solve for $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^TA)^{-1}A^T\mathbf{b}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T\mathbf{b}
$$

$$\boxed{\;P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Proof that the Projection Matrix is Idempotent: $P^2 = P$

**Step 1:** Compute $P^2$ using the definition of $P$.

$$
P^2 = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T \cdot A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T
$$

**Step 2:** Group the inner product — $A^TA$ cancels with the inverse.

$$
P^2 = A\left[(A^TA)^{-1}(A^TA)\right](A^TA)^{-1}A^T
$$

**Step 3:** The middle term equals the identity matrix $I_p$.

$$
P^2 = A \cdot I_p \cdot (A^TA)^{-1}A^T
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$
P^2 = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T = P \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.4 Proof that the Orthogonal Projection Matrix is Symmetric: $P^T = P$

**Step 1:** Take the transpose of $P$.

$$
P^T = \left[A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T\right]^T
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the rule $(ABC)^T = C^TB^TA^T$.

$$
P^T = (A^T)^T\left[(A^TA)^{-1}\right]^T A^T = A\left[(A^TA)^{-1}\right]^T A^T
$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity $(M^{-1})^T = (M^T)^{-1}$ for any invertible matrix $M$.

$$
P^T = A\left[(A^TA)^T\right]^{-1} A^T = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T = P \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Pythagorean Relation

**Setup:** From the Orthogonal Decomposition Theorem, $\mathbf{b} = \hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{z}$ where $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W$ and $\mathbf{z} \in W^{\perp}$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\|\mathbf{b}\|^2$ using the inner product.

$$
\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 = \mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{b} = (\hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{z}) \cdot (\hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{z})
$$

**Step 2:** Expand.

$$
\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 = \hat{\mathbf{b}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{b}} + 2\hat{\mathbf{b}} \cdot \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{z} \cdot \mathbf{z}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply orthogonality: $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \cdot \mathbf{z} = 0$.

$$
\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 = \|\hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{z}\|^2 = \|\hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation of the Best Approximation Theorem (Least Squares)

**Theorem:** Given $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ is inconsistent, the least-squares solution $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ minimizes $\|\mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x}\|$ over all $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^p$.

**Step 1:** Decompose $\mathbf{b}$ orthogonally: $\mathbf{b} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{z}$, where $A\hat{\mathbf{x}} \in \text{Col}(A)$ and $\mathbf{z} \perp \text{Col}(A)$.

**Step 2:** For any $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, write $A\mathbf{x}$ in $\text{Col}(A)$. Then $\mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x} = (A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - A\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{z}$.

**Step 3:** Note that $A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - A\mathbf{x} \in \text{Col}(A)$ and $\mathbf{z} \perp \text{Col}(A)$, so they are orthogonal. Apply the Pythagorean relation.

$$
\|\mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x}\|^2 = \|A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - A\mathbf{x}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{z}\|^2
$$

**Step 4:** The term $\|\mathbf{z}\|^2$ is fixed (independent of $\mathbf{x}$). The term $\|A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - A\mathbf{x}\|^2 \geq 0$ with equality if and only if $A\mathbf{x} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, i.e., $\mathbf{x} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}$ (when $A$ has independent columns).

$$
\|\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{z}\|^2 \leq \|\mathbf{b} - A\mathbf{x}\|^2 \quad \blacksquare
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow of the Projection Operation

The block diagram below illustrates the computational flow when projecting a vector $\mathbf{b}$ onto the column space of a matrix $A$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input vector b in R^n] --> B{Choose basis for W}
    B --> C[Orthogonal Basis via Gram-Schmidt]
    B --> D[Linearly Independent Columns of A]
    C --> E[Apply proj formula using inner products]
    D --> F[Compute A transpose A and invert]
    F --> G[Form P = A A transpose A inverse A transpose]
    G --> H[Compute projection hat b = P times b]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Verify orthogonality: b minus hat b perp W]
    I --> J[Output projection hat b in W and residual z in W perp]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture of QR Factorization (Related to Projection)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A0[Matrix A in R nx p with independent columns]
    end
    subgraph GramSchmidt[Gram Schmidt Process]
        G1[Step 1: v1 equals x1]
        G2[Step 2: v2 equals x2 minus proj v1 x2]
        G3[Step k: vk equals xk minus sum of projs]
    end
    subgraph Normalize[Normalization Stage]
        N1[q1 equals v1 over norm v1]
        N2[q2 equals v2 over norm v2]
        Np[qk equals vk over norm vk]
    end
    subgraph Output[Output Matrices]
        Qout[Q in R nx p orthonormal columns]
        Rout[R in R pxp upper triangular]
    end
    A0 --> G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> N1 --> N2 --> Np
    Np --> Qout
    G1 --> Rout
    G2 --> Rout
    G3 --> Rout
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Identifying Projection Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Given vector b and subspace W] --> Q1{What is dimension of W?}
    Q1 -->|1 - line through origin| R1[Use single vector formula with a]
    Q1 -->|2 - plane through origin| R2[Use two basis vectors u1 u2]
    Q1 -->|p - general subspace| R3[Use projection matrix P = A A transpose A inverse A transpose]
    R1 --> V1[Verify: b minus hat b is perp a]
    R2 --> V2[Verify: b minus hat b is perp to u1 and u2]
    R3 --> V3[Verify: P squared equals P and P transpose equals P]
    V1 --> END[Final: projection complete]
    V2 --> END
    V3 --> END
```

### 4.4 Decomposition Schematic (Orthogonal Decomposition Theorem)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RN[Space R^n]
        B[Vector b] --> DECOMP[Orthogonal Decomposition]
    end
    subgraph W[Subspace W]
        BHAT[Projection hat b in W]
    end
    subgraph WPERP[Orthogonal Complement W perp]
        Z[Residual z perp W]
    end
    DECOMP --> BHAT
    DECOMP --> Z
    B -.->|equals| BHAT
    BHAT -.->|plus| Z
    Z -.->|equals| B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the orthogonal projection of a vector $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ onto a subspace $W$. State the Orthogonal Decomposition Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto $W$** is the unique vector $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W$ such that $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is orthogonal to $W$.

**Orthogonal Decomposition Theorem:** Let $W$ be a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$. Then each $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ can be written uniquely as:

$$
\mathbf{b} = \hat{\mathbf{b}} + \mathbf{z}, \quad \text{where } \hat{\mathbf{b}} \in W \text{ and } \mathbf{z} \in W^{\perp}
$$

Moreover, if $\{\mathbf{u}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{u}_p\}$ is an orthogonal basis for $W$, then:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{u}_1}{\mathbf{u}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_1}\mathbf{u}_1 + \dfrac{\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{u}_2}{\mathbf{u}_2 \cdot \mathbf{u}_2}\mathbf{u}_2 + \cdots + \dfrac{\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{u}_p}{\mathbf{u}_p \cdot \mathbf{u}_p}\mathbf{u}_p
$$

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for theorem statement, 1 mark for the projection formula.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and prove that an orthogonal projection matrix $P$ is idempotent, i.e., $P^2 = P$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** If $P$ is an orthogonal projection matrix onto a subspace $W$, then $P^2 = P$.

**Proof:** Let $A$ be a matrix whose columns form a basis for $W$. Then:

$$
P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T
$$

Now compute $P^2$:

$$
P^2 = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T \cdot A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T
$$

Group the inner matrices:

$$
P^2 = A\left[(A^TA)^{-1}(A^TA)\right](A^TA)^{-1}A^T = A \cdot I \cdot (A^TA)^{-1}A^T = P
$$

Hence $P^2 = P$. $\blacksquare$

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for statement, 2 marks for the algebraic proof.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the projection of $\mathbf{b} = (1, 2, 3)^T$ onto the line $L$ spanned by $\mathbf{a} = (1, 1, -1)^T$ using the orthogonal projection formula. Also verify the orthogonality condition.

**(b) [7 Marks)** If $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, find the projection matrix $P$ onto $\text{Col}(A)$ and compute $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = P\mathbf{b}$. Verify that $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ lies in $\text{Col}(A)$.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Projection onto a Line

**Step 1:** Compute $\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b}$ (the inner product).

$$
\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b} = (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (-1)(3) = 1 + 2 - 3 = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{a}$ (the squared norm).

$$
\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{a} = 1^2 + 1^2 + (-1)^2 = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the projection formula.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a} = \dfrac{0}{3}\,\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{0} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify orthogonality: $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}} = \mathbf{b} = (1,2,3)^T$.

$$
\mathbf{a} \cdot (\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}) = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

The vector $\mathbf{b}$ is already orthogonal to $L$, so its projection onto $L$ is the zero vector. This is a **counter-intuitive but important** result for KTU boards.

**Step 5:** Pythagorean check.

$$
\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 = 14, \quad \|\hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 = 0, \quad \|\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 = 14
$$

$$
14 = 0 + 14 \;\checkmark
$$

### Part (b) — Projection Matrix onto $\text{Col}(A)$

**Step 1:** Compute $A^TA$.

$$
A^TA = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(A^TA)^{-1}$. Determinant $= (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 3$.

$$
(A^TA)^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $A^TA$ multiplied by its inverse (sanity check).

$$
(A^TA)^{-1}(A^TA) = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = I_2 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$.

$$
P = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Compute the middle two matrices first:

$$
A(A^TA)^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Now multiply by $A^T$:

$$
P = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = P\mathbf{b}$.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 6+2+1 \\ 3+4-1 \\ 3-2+2 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 6 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Verify $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \in \text{Col}(A)$: we need $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\mathbf{x}$ for some $\mathbf{x}$. Solve:

$$
A\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

From the second row: $x_1 = 2$. From the third row: $x_2 = 1$. Check first row: $1+1 = 2 \neq 3$. So $\hat{\mathbf{b}} \notin \text{Col}(A)$ in this case — meaning $\mathbf{b}$ already lies in $\text{Col}(A)$ but with a different representation. Let me re-verify: actually with $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 1$, we get $A\mathbf{x} = (3, 2, 1)^T = \hat{\mathbf{b}} \;\checkmark$. The earlier row-1 miscalculation was arithmetic error: $1(2) + 1(1) = 3 \;\checkmark$. Verified.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Use Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization to construct an orthogonal basis for the subspace $W = \text{Span}\{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_3\}$ where $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1, 0, 1)^T$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1, 0, 1, 1)^T$, $\mathbf{x}_3 = (0, 1, 1, 1)^T$. Then find the projection of $\mathbf{b} = (1, 1, 1, 1)^T$ onto $W$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Solve the least-squares problem $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$. Find $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, and the residual $\mathbf{z}$.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Gram-Schmidt Orthogonalization

**Step 1:** Set $\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{x}_1 = (1, 1, 0, 1)^T$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1}{\mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1}\mathbf{v}_1$.

$$
\mathbf{x}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1 = 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 2
$$
$$
\mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1 = 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 3
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \dfrac{2}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/3 \\ -2/3 \\ 1 \\ 1/3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\mathbf{v}_3 = \mathbf{x}_3 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x}_3 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1}{\mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1}\mathbf{v}_1 - \dfrac{\mathbf{x}_3 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2}{\mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2}\mathbf{v}_2$.

$$
\mathbf{x}_3 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1 = 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 2
$$
$$
\mathbf{x}_3 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2 = 0 - 2/3 + 1 + 1/3 = 2/3
$$
$$
\mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2 = 1/9 + 4/9 + 1 + 1/9 = 15/9 = 5/3
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \dfrac{2}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \dfrac{2/3}{5/3}\begin{pmatrix} 1/3 \\ -2/3 \\ 1 \\ 1/3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 2/3 \\ 0 \\ 2/3 \end{pmatrix} - \dfrac{2}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1/3 \\ -2/3 \\ 1 \\ 1/3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_3 = \begin{pmatrix} -2/3 \\ 1/3 \\ 1 \\ 1/3 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 2/15 \\ -4/15 \\ 2/5 \\ 2/15 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -2/3 - 2/15 \\ 1/3 + 4/15 \\ 1 - 2/5 \\ 1/3 - 2/15 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -12/15 \\ 9/15 \\ 9/15 \\ 3/15 \end{pmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} -4 \\ 3 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4:** The orthogonal basis is $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3\}$.

**Step 5:** Compute $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ for $\mathbf{b} = (1, 1, 1, 1)^T$.

$$
\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{v}_1 = 1+1+0+1 = 3, \quad \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{v}_1 = 3
$$
$$
\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{v}_2 = 1/3 - 2/3 + 1 + 1/3 = 1, \quad \mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{v}_2 = 5/3
$$
$$
\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{v}_3 = (-4+3+3+1)/5 = 3/5, \quad \mathbf{v}_3 \cdot \mathbf{v}_3 = (16+9+9+1)/25 = 35/25 = 7/5
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{3}{3}\mathbf{v}_1 + \dfrac{1}{5/3}\mathbf{v}_2 + \dfrac{3/5}{7/5}\mathbf{v}_3 = \mathbf{v}_1 + \dfrac{3}{5}\mathbf{v}_2 + \dfrac{3}{7}\mathbf{v}_3
$$

### Part (b) — Least Squares Solution

**Step 1:** Compute $A^TA$.

$$
A^TA = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $A^T\mathbf{b}$.

$$
A^T\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 1+1+5+3 \\ 3-1+5-3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve the normal equations $A^TA\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^T\mathbf{b}$.

$$
4\hat{x}_1 = 12 \Rightarrow \hat{x}_1 = 3, \quad 4\hat{x}_2 = 4 \Rightarrow \hat{x}_2 = 1
$$

So $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (3, 1)^T$.

**Step 4:** Compute $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the residual $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}$.

$$
\mathbf{z} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \\ 5 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \\ 4 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Verify orthogonality of $\mathbf{z}$ to columns of $A$:

$$
\mathbf{a}_1 \cdot \mathbf{z} = -1 - 1 + 1 + 1 = 0 \;\checkmark
$$
$$
\mathbf{a}_2 \cdot \mathbf{z} = -1 + 1 + 1 - 1 = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $(A^TA)^{-1}$ exists** before using the projection formula (costs 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing projection with reflection**: reflection matrix is $2P - I$, not $P$.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in Gram-Schmidt**: forgetting the chain subtraction (each new $\mathbf{v}_k$ must be orthogonal to *all* previous $\mathbf{v}_i$, not just the most recent).
> 4. **Not verifying the residual is zero** in least-squares: this is the *defining* check of orthogonality.
> 5. **Marking $\mathbf{b}$ as the projection** when $\mathbf{b}$ happens to be in $\text{Col}(A)$ — always compute $P\mathbf{b}$ explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Projection formula onto a line:** $\hat{\mathbf{b}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}$ — must know by heart.
- **Projection matrix:** $P = A(A^TA)^{-1}A^T$ — the cornerstone formula of the entire module.
- **Two key properties of orthogonal projection matrices:** $P^2 = P$ (idempotent) and $P^T = P$ (symmetric).
- **The Pythagorean relation:** $\|\mathbf{b}\|^2 = \|\hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}\|^2$ — used in distance and energy problems.
- **Normal equations:** $A^TA\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^T\mathbf{b}$ — the bridge from projection to least squares.
- **Gram-Schmidt process:** produces orthogonal basis from any basis — the algorithm to remember is $\mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - \sum \text{proj}_{\mathbf{v}_i}\mathbf{x}_k$.
- **QR factorization:** $A = QR$ with $Q$ orthonormal and $R$ upper triangular — orthogonal projection is then $P = QQ^T$.
- **Best Approximation Theorem:** the projection $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is the *closest* point in $W$ to $\mathbf{b}$ — geometric essence of least squares.
- **Distinguish:** projection onto a line uses one formula; projection onto a plane or higher subspace requires either an orthogonal basis or the projection matrix.
- **Sanity checks:** always verify $P^2 = P$, $P^T = P$, and $\mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}} \perp W$ when solving problems.
- **Engineering relevance:** least-squares regression, signal denoising, computer graphics, and Kalman filtering all use projection theorems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** When a question asks for "the projection," always state which subspace and which method you are using (formula vs. matrix). Examiners award **0.5–1 bonus mark** for explicit method identification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving the Least Squares Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT201 - MATHEMATICS FOR INFORMATION SCIENCE – 2 > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Solving the Least Squares Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In **Linear Algebra**, a **Least Squares Problem** is an optimization problem of the form: Given a real $m \times n$ matrix $A$ (with $m > n$, i.e., more equations than unknowns) and a real $m \times 1$ vector $\mathbf{b}$, find an $n \times 1$ vector $\mathbf{x}$ that **minimizes** the sum of squared residuals: $$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}} \;\Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2} = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}x_{j} - b_{i} \right)^{2}$$ The system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ is generally **inconsistent** (no exact solution), so we seek the *best approximate* solution. The vector $\mathbf{r} = A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}$ is called the **residual vector**, and our goal is to find $\mathbf{x}$ that makes the Euclidean norm $\Vert \mathbf{r} \Vert_{2}$ as small as possible. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology:** > - $A$ is **tall and thin** (overdetermined system): $m \gg n$. > - The vector $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ (when $A$ has full column rank) is the **least squares solution**. > - The matrix $A^{+} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}$ is called the **Moore–Penrose Pseudo-inverse** (left inverse in this case). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **data scientist** who has collected 100 measurements of a process that you *believe* follows a straight line $y = c_1 + c_2 x$. When you plot all 100 points on a graph, they do not fall on a single line perfectly — there is scatter (experimental noise, measurement error). You cannot pass *one* straight line through *all* 100 points simultaneously. So what do you do? You draw the line where the **sum of squared vertical distances** from the data points to the line is **as small as possible**. This is exactly the *least squares problem*! The line itself is your $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, the observations form $\mathbf{b}$, and the design matrix $A$ encodes the model (e.g., columns of ones and $x$-values). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Intuition:** The least squares solution is the projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto the **column space** of $A$. Geometrically, $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is chosen so that the residual $A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{b}$ is **orthogonal** to $\mathrm{Col}(A)$. In symbols: > $$A^{T}(A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; A^{T}A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^{T}\mathbf{b}$$ ### 1.3 Why "Squared" and not Absolute? | Criterion | Squared Distance | Absolute Distance | |---|---|---| | Differentiability | Smooth (quadratic), easy gradient | Non-differentiable at 0 | | Penalizes large errors | **Heavily** (good for Gaussian noise) | Linearly | | Closed-form solution | **Yes** (Normal Equations) | Requires iterative methods | | Statistical optimality | **Best Linear Unbiased Estimator** under Gaussian noise | Robust to outliers | > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **Euclidean norm** (squared) case exclusively. Always state $\Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$ in the exam unless otherwise specified. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation The theory of least squares rests on the **Orthogonal Projection Theorem**: in any inner product space, the closest point in a subspace $W$ to a given vector $\mathbf{b}$ is its orthogonal projection onto $W$. For the system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$: - **Column Space** $C(A) = \{A\mathbf{x} : \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}\}$ is a subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. - Since $\mathbf{b} \notin C(A)$ (inconsistent case), the **closest point** in $C(A)$ to $\mathbf{b}$ is the projection $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$. - The vector connecting $\mathbf{b}$ to this projection, $\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, must be **perpendicular to every column of $A$**, giving $A^{T}(\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \mathbf{0}$. ### 2.2 Three Standard Solution Methods (Step Logic) **Method 1 — Normal Equations (Closed-form, simplest):** 1. Form the **Gram matrix** $A^{T}A$ ($n \times n$, symmetric positive definite when $A$ has full column rank). 2. Form the right-hand side $A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ ($n \times 1$). 3. Solve $A^{T}A\mathbf{x} = A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ using Cholesky or Gaussian elimination. **Method 2 — QR Factorization (Numerically superior):** 1. Factor $A = QR$ where $Q$ is $m \times n$ with orthonormal columns, $R$ is $n \times n$ upper triangular. 2. Since $Q^{T}Q = I$, multiply $QR\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ on the left by $Q^{T}$: $R\mathbf{x} = Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$. 3. Solve the triangular system by **back substitution**. **Method 3 — Singular Value Decomposition (Most robust, handles rank deficiency):** 1. Compute $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$. 2. Form the pseudo-inverse $\Sigma^{+}$ by inverting non-zero singular values and transposing. 3. Compute $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = V\Sigma^{+}U^{T}\mathbf{b}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is QR preferred over Normal Equations in practice?** > Computing $A^{T}A$ **squares the condition number**: $\kappa(A^{T}A) = [\kappa(A)]^{2}$. For ill-conditioned $A$, the normal equations amplify rounding errors by a factor of 100 to 10{,}000. QR has $\kappa(R) = \kappa(A)$, preserving numerical accuracy. This is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU board exams. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Statement | Meaning | Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$ | Definition of LS problem | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $m \geq n$ | | 2 | $A^{T}A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | **Normal Equations** | $A$ has full column rank | | 3 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | Closed-form solution | $A$ full column rank, $A^{T}A$ invertible | | 4 | $A^{+} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}$ | **Pseudo-inverse** (full column case) | $A$ has full column rank | | 5 | $A = QR$ | QR Factorization (thin) | $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $R \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ upper triangular | | 6 | $R\hat{\mathbf{x}} = Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | Reduced system from QR | Back-substitution yields $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ | | 7 | $H = I - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}}$ | **Householder reflector** | $\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = 1$ optional, $H^{T} = H^{-1} = H$ | | 8 | $H\mathbf{x} = \pm \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \mathbf{e}_{1}$ | Householder zeroes out components | $\mathbf{e}_{1} = [1, 0, \ldots, 0]^{T}$ | | 9 | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x} \pm \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \mathbf{e}_{1}$ | Choice of Householder vector | Sign chosen to avoid cancellation: $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x} + \mathrm{sign}(x_{1}) \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \mathbf{e}_{1}$ | | 10 | $A = U\Sigma V^{T}$ | **SVD** | $U, V$ orthogonal, $\Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(\sigma_{1}, \ldots, \sigma_{n})$ | | 11 | $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = V\Sigma^{+}U^{T}\mathbf{b}$ | SVD-based LS solution | $\Sigma^{+}_{ii} = 1/\sigma_{i}$ for $\sigma_{i} > 0$, else 0 | | 12 | $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ | Residual vector | Always $\mathbf{r} \perp C(A)$ | | 13 | $\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{T}A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ | Sum of squared errors | Used to assess fit quality | > [!NOTE] > **Notation caution:** In KTU answer sheets, always write the normal equations as $A^{T}A\mathbf{x} = A^{T}\mathbf{b}$, **not** $A^{T}Ax = A^{T}b$ (Roman bold is the university convention for vectors). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Computer Graphics & Vision:** Camera calibration, 3D point cloud fitting, pose estimation. - **Machine Learning:** Linear/Polynomial regression, neural network training (gradient descent is essentially a least-squares variant in non-linear settings). - **Signal Processing:** Channel estimation in OFDM, adaptive beamforming, noise filtering. - **Control Systems:** System identification, Kalman filtering (least squares on the innovation). - **GPS & Surveying:** Trilateration with redundant satellite measurements. - **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Curve and surface fitting from discrete sample points. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Equations **Goal:** Minimize $f(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$. **Step 1 — Expand the squared norm using the inner product identity $\Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert^{2} = \mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{y}$:** $$f(\mathbf{x}) = (A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})^{T}(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})$$ **Step 2 — Distribute the transpose using $(AB)^{T} = B^{T}A^{T}$:** $$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}A^{T}A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^{T}A^{T}\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{T}A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{b}$$ **Step 3 — Recognize that $\mathbf{x}^{T}A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{T}A\mathbf{x}$ are both scalars and therefore equal each other (transposes of scalars are the scalars themselves):** $$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^{T}A^{T}A\mathbf{x} - 2\,\mathbf{x}^{T}A^{T}\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{b}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{x}$ using the matrix calculus rules $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}^{T}M\mathbf{x}) = (M + M^{T})\mathbf{x}$ and $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}$:** Since $A^{T}A$ is symmetric, $(A^{T}A + (A^{T}A)^{T}) = 2A^{T}A$. Thus: $$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) = 2A^{T}A\mathbf{x} - 2A^{T}\mathbf{b}$$ **Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition):** $$2A^{T}A\mathbf{x} - 2A^{T}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$$ **Step 6 — Divide by 2 and rearrange — this is the celebrated Normal Equations:** $$\boxed{\,A^{T}A\,\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^{T}\mathbf{b}\,}$$ **Step 7 — If $A$ has full column rank, $A^{T}A$ is symmetric positive definite, hence invertible, yielding:** $$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}\mathbf{b}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Existence & Uniqueness Theorem (Board-favourite):** The least squares problem has a **unique** solution if and only if the columns of $A$ are **linearly independent** (i.e., $\mathrm{rank}(A) = n$). In that case $A^{T}A$ is positive definite. If $\mathrm{rank}(A) < n$, infinitely many LS solutions exist; the one with minimum norm is given by the pseudo-inverse $A^{+}\mathbf{b}$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the QR Method **Given:** $A = QR$ with $Q^{T}Q = I_{n}$ and $R$ upper triangular and invertible (when $A$ has full column rank). **Step 1 — Substitute the factorization into the LS objective:** $$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert QR\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Use the isometry property of matrices with orthonormal columns: $\Vert Q\mathbf{y} \Vert_{2} = \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert_{2}$ for any $\mathbf{y}$:** $$\Vert QR\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2} = \Vert Q(R\mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Add and subtract $Q Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$ inside the norm, noting that $QQ^{T}$ is the orthogonal projection onto $C(A)$:** $$= \Vert Q(R\mathbf{x} - Q^{T}\mathbf{b}) + (QQ^{T} - I)\mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Because $Q(R\mathbf{x} - Q^{T}\mathbf{b})$ lies in $C(A)$ and $(QQ^{T} - I)\mathbf{b}$ is orthogonal to $C(A)$, the two terms are perpendicular. By Pythagoras:** $$= \Vert R\mathbf{x} - Q^{T}\mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2} + \Vert (QQ^{T} - I)\mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$ **Step 5 — The second term is independent of $\mathbf{x}$ (it's a constant). Therefore, minimization reduces to:** $$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert R\mathbf{x} - Q^{T}\mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$$ **Step 6 — Since $R$ is square and invertible, the minimum is achieved (at zero) by solving:** $$\boxed{\,R\hat{\mathbf{x}} = Q^{T}\mathbf{b}\,}$$ This is an **upper triangular system** solvable by back substitution — only $O(n^{2})$ flops after the QR factorization is available. ### 3.3 Householder Transformation — Detailed Derivation **Goal:** Given a column $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, find an orthogonal matrix $H$ such that $H\mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{e}_{1}$ for some scalar $\alpha$ (zeroes out all components below the first). **Step 1 — Orthogonal reflection across a hyperplane orthogonal to unit vector $\mathbf{u}$ has the form:** $$H = I - 2\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{T} \quad \text{with} \quad \mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{u} = 1$$ **Step 2 — Verify orthogonality: $H^{T}H = (I - 2\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{T})^{2} = I - 4\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{T} + 4\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{u})\mathbf{u}^{T} = I$. Hence $H = H^{T} = H^{-1}$.** **Step 3 — Require $H\mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{e}_{1}$, i.e., $\mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{x}) = \alpha \mathbf{e}_{1}$. Setting $\beta = 2\mathbf{u}^{T}\mathbf{x}$:** $$\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{x} - \alpha \mathbf{e}_{1}}{\beta}$$ **Step 4 — Choose $\alpha$ to make $\mathbf{u}$ well-defined and avoid cancellation. Standard choice:** $$\alpha = -\,\mathrm{sign}(x_{1})\,\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_{2}$$ **Step 5 — Defining the (un-normalized) Householder vector $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x} - \alpha \mathbf{e}_{1}$, we have $H = I - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}}$.** **Step 6 — Cost of applying $H$ to a vector: $H\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y} - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{y}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{v}$, which is $O(m)$ operations — much cheaper than a Givens rotation for full rows.** ### 3.4 Worked Example — Comprehensive Solution **Problem:** Solve the least squares problem for $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ #### Method A — Normal Equations **Step 1:** Compute $A^{T}A$: $$A^{T}A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+1 & 0+1+2 \\ 0+1+2 & 0+1+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Compute $A^{T}\mathbf{b}$: $$A^{T}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1+2+2 \\ 0+2+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3:** Solve the $2 \times 2$ system $\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$: Determinant: $\det = 3(5) - 3(3) = 15 - 9 = 6$. Inverse: $\dfrac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. **Step 4:** Multiply: $$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 25 - 18 \\ -15 + 18 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/6 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Verification:** Compute residual $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$: $$A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 7/6 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/6 \\ 7/6 + 3/6 \\ 7/6 + 6/6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/6 \\ 10/6 \\ 13/6 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\mathbf{r} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 7/6 \\ 10/6 \\ 13/6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/6 \\ 2/6 \\ -1/6 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Orthogonality check:** $A^{T}\mathbf{r} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\dfrac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{6}\begin{bmatrix} -1+2-1 \\ 0+2-2 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0}$ ✓ **Sum of squared errors:** $\mathrm{SSE} = \Vert \mathbf{r} \Vert_{2}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{36}(1 + 4 + 1) = \dfrac{6}{36} = \dfrac{1}{6}$. #### Method B — QR Factorization (Gram–Schmidt) **Step 1:** Compute $r_{11} = \Vert \mathbf{a}_1 \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{1+1+1} = \sqrt{3}$. So $\mathbf{q}_1 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. **Step 2:** Compute $r_{12} = \mathbf{q}_1^{T}\mathbf{a}_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(0+1+2) = \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{3}} = \sqrt{3}$. **Step 3:** Project out: $\mathbf{a}_2' = \mathbf{a}_2 - r_{12}\mathbf{q}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} - \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. **Step 4:** $r_{22} = \Vert \mathbf{a}_2' \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{1+0+1} = \sqrt{2}$. So $\mathbf{q}_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. **Step 5:** Assemble: $$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} & -1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad R = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{3} & \sqrt{3} \\ 0 & \sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6:** Compute $Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$: $$Q^{T}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{3} & 1/\sqrt{3} \\ -1/\sqrt{2} & 0 & 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 7 — Back substitution on $R\mathbf{x} = Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$:** Row 2: $\sqrt{2}\,x_2 = 1/\sqrt{2} \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = \dfrac{1}{2}$. Row 1: $\sqrt{3}\,x_1 + \sqrt{3}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} \;\Rightarrow\; \sqrt{3}\,x_1 = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{3}} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{10 - 3}{2\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{7}{2\sqrt{3}}$. Thus $x_1 = \dfrac{7}{2\sqrt{3}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{7}{6}$. $$\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 7/6 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix}\;}$$ Both methods agree. ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Workflow of the Least Squares Solution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Overdetermined System Ax equals b
m greater than n] --> checkRank{A has full column rank?} checkRank -- No --> rankDef[Rank deficient
Use SVD or pseudo inverse] checkRank -- Yes --> chooseMethod{Select Solution Method} chooseMethod --> methodA[Method 1: Normal Equations
Form A transpose A and A transpose b] chooseMethod --> methodB[Method 2: QR Factorization
A equals Q R] chooseMethod --> methodC[Method 3: SVD
A equals U Sigma V transpose] methodA --> subA[Solve A transpose A x equals A transpose b
via Cholesky or Gaussian elimination] methodB --> houseH[Compute QR via Householder
H equals I minus 2 v v transpose over v transpose v] methodC --> svdStep[Form Sigma plus
invert non zero singular values] houseH --> subB[Back substitute on R x equals Q transpose b] subA --> solution[Solution x hat] subB --> solution svdStep --> xformula[x hat equals V Sigma plus U transpose b] xformula --> solution solution --> verify{Verify residual
r equals b minus A x hat} verify -- r equals 0 --> exact[Exact solution exists] verify -- r not equal 0 --> ortho[Check A transpose r equals 0] ortho -- Yes --> accept[Least squares solution accepted] ortho -- No --> error[Numerical error
Recheck computation] rankDef --> accept ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the QR Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Input Matrix A
m by n] --> extract[Extract first column
x equals a1] extract --> normStep[Compute norm of x
alpha equals norm of x] normStep --> houseStep[Form Householder vector
v equals x plus sign x1 times alpha e1] houseStep --> applyH[Apply H to remaining columns
H A transforms A to upper triangular form] applyH --> iterate{More columns?} iterate -- Yes --> shift[Shift to next column
Continue reduction] iterate -- No --> qBuild[Accumulate Q from Householder products] qBuild --> rBuild[Read off upper triangular R] rBuild --> solveR[Solve triangular system R x equals Q transpose b] solveR --> outputLS[Output least squares solution] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Projection Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart TB space[Vector Space R to power m] --> subspace[Column Space of A
Subspace of dimension n] bvec[Vector b not in C of A] --> proj[Orthogonal Projection
P equals Q Q transpose] proj --> approx[Closest point in C of A
A x hat] bvec --> residual[Residual r equals b minus A x hat
Orthogonal to C of A] residual --> perp[Residual perpendicular to all
columns of A] perp --> minNorm[Minimizes norm of b minus A x] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading guide for diagrams:** Diagram 1 outlines the **decision tree** students should follow on the exam. Diagram 2 explains the **internal Householder QR mechanism**. Diagram 3 is the **geometric backbone** of the entire theory — projection, orthogonality, and minimality are tied together in one picture. Memorize the orthogonality condition $A^{T}(A\hat{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0}$ as the *one* equation that justifies all three diagrams. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State and prove the necessary and sufficient condition for the normal equations to have a unique least squares solution.* **Model Answer:** > The least squares problem $\min \Vert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \Vert_{2}^{2}$ has a **unique** solution if and only if the columns of $A$ are **linearly independent**, i.e., $\mathrm{rank}(A) = n$. > > **Necessity:** If columns are dependent, then there exists a non-zero $\mathbf{z}$ with $A\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{0}$. Then for any solution $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, the vector $\hat{\mathbf{x}} + c\mathbf{z}$ gives the same residual, so the solution is not unique. > > **Sufficiency:** If $\mathrm{rank}(A) = n$, then $A^{T}A$ is symmetric positive definite (since $\mathbf{x}^{T}A^{T}A\mathbf{x} = \Vert A\mathbf{x} \Vert_{2}^{2} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$). Positive definiteness guarantees invertibility, so $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^{T}A)^{-1}A^{T}\mathbf{b}$ is the unique minimizer. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *What is a Householder transformation? Show that it is orthogonal.* **Model Answer:** > A **Householder transformation** is a matrix of the form $H = I - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}}$, where $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is a non-zero vector. It represents a reflection across the hyperplane orthogonal to $\mathbf{v}$. > > **Proof of orthogonality:** $H^{T} = \left(I - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}}\right)^{T} = I - 2\dfrac{(\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T})^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}} = I - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}} = H$ (since $\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}$ is symmetric). > > Now $H^{T}H = H^{2} = \left(I - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}}\right)^{2} = I - \dfrac{4\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}} + \dfrac{4\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v})\mathbf{v}^{T}}{(\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v})^{2}} = I - \dfrac{4\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}} + \dfrac{4\mathbf{v}\mathbf{v}^{T}}{\mathbf{v}^{T}\mathbf{v}} = I$. > > Hence $H^{T}H = I$, proving $H$ is orthogonal. Since $H = H^{T}$, it is also **symmetric**. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Solve the following least squares problem using **normal equations**: $$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Verify the orthogonality condition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the **QR factorization** of the same $A$ using the **Gram–Schmidt process**, and use it to find the least squares solution. Compare with part (a). **(7 marks)** --- **Solution A(a):** **Step 1 — Compute $A^{T}A$:** $$A^{T}A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4+1+1 & 2+3+1 \\ 2+3+1 & 1+9+1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 \\ 6 & 11 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $A^{T}\mathbf{b}$:** $$A^{T}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8+5+2 \\ 4+15+2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 21 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Solve $\begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 \\ 6 & 11 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 21 \end{bmatrix}$:** Determinant: $6 \cdot 11 - 6 \cdot 6 = 66 - 36 = 30$. Inverse: $\dfrac{1}{30}\begin{bmatrix} 11 & -6 \\ -6 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$. $$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{30}\begin{bmatrix} 11 & -6 \\ -6 & 6 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 21 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{30}\begin{bmatrix} 165 - 126 \\ -90 + 126 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{30}\begin{bmatrix} 39 \\ 36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13/10 \\ 6/5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Orthogonality verification:** $$A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 13/10 \\ 6/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 26/10 + 6/5 \\ 13/10 + 18/5 \\ 13/10 + 6/5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 26/10 + 12/10 \\ 13/10 + 36/10 \\ 13/10 + 12/10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 38/10 \\ 49/10 \\ 25/10 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 - 3.8 \\ 5 - 4.9 \\ 2 - 2.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.1 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 \\ 1/10 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$A^{T}\mathbf{r} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1/5 \\ 1/10 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/5 + 1/10 - 1/2 \\ 1/5 + 3/10 - 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/10 + 1/10 - 5/10 \\ 2/10 + 3/10 - 5/10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \;\checkmark$$ **Final answer:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \left[\dfrac{13}{10},\, \dfrac{6}{5}\right]^{T}$. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution A(b):** **Step 1 — Compute $r_{11}$ and $\mathbf{q}_1$:** $$r_{11} = \Vert \mathbf{a}_1 \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{4+1+1} = \sqrt{6}$$ $$\mathbf{q}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $r_{12}$:** $$r_{12} = \mathbf{q}_1^{T}\mathbf{a}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 1) = \frac{6}{\sqrt{6}} = \sqrt{6}$$ **Step 3 — Form $\mathbf{a}_2'$:** $$\mathbf{a}_2' = \mathbf{a}_2 - r_{12}\mathbf{q}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \sqrt{6} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $r_{22}$ and $\mathbf{q}_2$:** $$r_{22} = \Vert \mathbf{a}_2' \Vert_{2} = \sqrt{1+4+0} = \sqrt{5}$$ $$\mathbf{q}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 5 — Assemble $Q$ and $R$:** $$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 2/\sqrt{6} & -1/\sqrt{5} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & 2/\sqrt{5} \\ 1/\sqrt{6} & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad R = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{6} & \sqrt{6} \\ 0 & \sqrt{5} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 6 — Compute $Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$:** $$Q^{T}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{6} & 1/\sqrt{6} \\ -1/\sqrt{5} & 2/\sqrt{5} & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8/\sqrt{6} + 5/\sqrt{6} + 2/\sqrt{6} \\ -4/\sqrt{5} + 10/\sqrt{5} + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15/\sqrt{6} \\ 6/\sqrt{5} \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 7 — Back substitution on $R\mathbf{x} = Q^{T}\mathbf{b}$:** Row 2: $\sqrt{5}\,x_2 = \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = \dfrac{6}{5}$.
